During last evening's seminar, we engaged in a thoughtful discussion about Edward S. Casey's seminal work, *Getting Back into Place: Toward a Renewed Study of the Place-World*. The text's opening chapter examines the concept of "implacement," while the subsequent section explores "displacement." Casey presents a compelling argument in the initial chapter, contrasting our modern temporal-centric worldview—with its pervasive notions of time scarcity, efficiency, and punctuality—with his proposed paradigm that positions place as the fundamental organizing principle of culture. This spatial framework, he contends, fosters more rooted and cohesive communities. (I offer this as a broad interpretation—should my professor or peers encounter this reflection, I acknowledge it as a simplified overview.) Among our assigned readings, Casey's work stands out as the first to truly captivate me, offering what feels like an intellectual awakening.

A woman from the United States has filed a lawsuit against Hewlett-Packard (HP), alleging that its printer ink cartridges are designed to expire on a specific date. This woman, hailing from Georgia and choosing not to reveal her identity, claims that a chip inside these cartridges signals the printer to require a refill even when it's not necessary. The lawsuit aims to include all individuals in the United States who have bought an HP inkjet printer since February 2001. HP, known as the largest printer manufacturer globally, has not provided any comments on this legal action. It is known that HP ink cartridges use chip technology to detect low ink levels and alert users to replace them. However, the lawsuit contends that these chips also deactivate the cartridges after a set period, regardless of whether or not they are actually empty.

Cardinal Cormac Murphy-O'Connor has criticised the Iraq War, stating that the billions of pounds spent on the conflict in Iraq and the Middle East should have been used to reduce poverty. The head of the Catholic Church in England and Wales made these remarks on BBC Radio 4 and will reiterate his stance during his Christmas Midnight Mass. In his Christmas message, the cardinal denounced the war in Iraq as a "terrible" waste of money. Both he and the Archbishop of Canterbury have spoken out against the war. During his appearance on BBC Radio 4's *Thought for the Day* segment, he criticized the allocation of "billions" to war efforts instead of using those funds to lift people "out of dire poverty, malnourishment, and disease." The cardinal declared that 2005 should be the year to campaign to "make poverty history.

I know this may sound unusual, but I believe robots can inspire us to become better humans. Growing up in Bethlehem, Pennsylvania, the birthplace of Bethlehem Steel, I was fortunate to have a father who was an engineer. He would teach me about how things worked. We would build projects together, such as model rockets and slot cars. Here's the go-kart that we built together. That's me behind the wheel, with my sister and my best friend at the time. One day, when I was around 10 years old, he came home and announced that our next project would be building a robot, a robot.

Let me start off by saying I did not purchase EU when it first came out and that I got it on sale, which included EW, so I never experienced the issues that some people claim to have had with that game. I loved that game; however, it was unforgiving in the early stages but rewarding when you did things right. As for XCOM2, I love it. The game plays pretty well for me on medium, and I am running a GTX560 with 2GB of VRAM. At first, the game set it to high, and it was running a bit sluggishly but not to the point of being unplayable. When I dropped it down to medium, it smoothed out. I'm not sure if I am just lucky when it comes to having issues with games or what, but it seems that most of the negative reviews sound like they are coming from some really entitled people.

While not perfect, this game has been incredibly enjoyable. The save system and risk of sudden death can be frustrating at times. During my first playthrough, I lost my main character around the 10-hour mark of a 20-hour campaign, and I would have appreciated the option to reload from a previous save. To mitigate such losses, I've resorted to using a third-party mod and save tool. Additionally, the aiming reticule is somewhat small, and the zoom feature could be more powerful. Despite these issues, the game has been a blast. As someone who isn't typically a fan of zombie games, I've been drawn in by the combination of strategy, missions, and first-person shooter and beat-em-up elements. I find myself playing as often as possible, always tempted to complete just one more mission or supply run before shutting down the game.

At a glance: Tory health checks  
The UK's opposition Conservative Party has announced plans to implement mandatory health screenings for immigrants if they win the upcoming general election. Here’s an overview of the proposal:  

- Individuals relocating to Britain from outside the EU for work or residence would be subject to health checks.  
- Those intending to stay for six months or longer, originating from countries with high tuberculosis (TB) rates, would require a chest X-ray and additional tests if necessary.  
- All non-EU nationals seeking to remain in the UK for a year or more would undergo a comprehensive medical examination, covering conditions such as tuberculosis, hepatitis B, and HIV.  
- A positive TB test would result in an automatic visa rejection.  
- Other medical conditions would be assessed individually on a case-by-case basis.

The estimation of the intrinsic dimension of a dataset is a crucial step in many dimensionality reduction techniques. This article showcases intrinsic, an R package that incorporates novel state-of-the-art likelihood-based estimators of the intrinsic dimension of a dataset. In particular, the package includes three models: two-nn, gride, and hidalgo. To make these innovative estimators easily accessible, the package features a few high-level, intuitive functions that leverage a broader set of efficient, low-level routines. Intrinsic encompasses models that fall into two categories: homogeneous and heterogeneous intrinsic dimension estimators. The first category consists of the two-nn and gride models, which offer inference under both frequentist and Bayesian frameworks. The second category features hidalgo, a Bayesian mixture model, for which an efficient Gibbs sampler has been implemented.

Research indicates that high-speed internet access is significantly boosting online self-expression. According to a report by the UK think tank Demos, a substantial segment of broadband users in Britain, approximately one-quarter, routinely upload content and manage personal websites. The report highlights how an always-on, fast internet connection is transforming internet usage patterns. Currently, over five million UK households have broadband, and this number continues to rise swiftly. The Demos report analyzed the influence of broadband on internet habits, revealing that more than half of the broadband users access the web before breakfast. Remarkably, one in five confessed to browsing the internet in the middle of the night. Most notably, the report asserts that broadband is propelling individuals toward more active online engagement.

The reboot of the Devil May Cry franchise has brought about numerous changes for the series, particularly for its protagonist, Dante. While longtime fans of the original series may be displeased with the alterations to their beloved character, it is undeniable that the changes are for the better. In fact, this new game by Ninja Theory showcases Dante's origins as an arrogant and cynical young man, and chronicles his transformative journey towards selflessness. Throughout, the game masterfully crafts a compelling narrative arc for the new Dante, making him an engaging character to follow. The story depicts Dante's introduction into a corrupt world he willfully chose to ignore, offering a fresh perspective on his character.

While I personally can't recommend this game, it wasn't completely awful. The gunplay is decent, provided you have the funds to buy gun parts—which brings me to my primary criticism: it's pay-to-win. If I could overlook that aspect, the game would actually be enjoyable, as the hardsuit is a blast to use and HRV is a brilliant concept. I'd choose this over any Call of Duty title without hesitation, but its pay-to-win microtransaction system is a dealbreaker for me. If you're fine with this model, then by all means, enjoy it. But if you share my distaste for pay-to-win mechanics, it's best to pass—this one just isn't for us.

Novel concepts, perspectives, and challenges in measuring and controlling an open quantum system via sequential schemes are showcased. We discuss how similar protocols, relying on both repeated quantum measurements and dynamic decoupling control pulses, can enable  (i) confining and protecting quantum dynamics in accordance with the zero-point physics. (ii) analytically predicting the probability that a quantum system is transferred into a target quantum state by means of stochastic sequential measurements. (iii) optimally reconstructing the spectral density of environmental noise sources by orthogonalizing in the frequency domain the filter functions driving the designed quantum sensor. The achievement of these tasks will enhance our ability to observe and manipulate open quantum systems, thus bringing advances to quantum science and technologies.

Podcasting: The Rise of Digital Audio Content
Podcasting is a form of digital audio content that has gained popularity in recent years. It is the production and distribution of non-interactive audio content, typically in the form of a series of episodes, that can be downloaded or streamed online. The term "podcasting" comes from the fact that the first podcasts were created and distributed on Apple's iPod, but it is not limited to Apple devices.
Adam Curry, a former MTV host and VJ, is often credited as the pioneer of podcasting. He started creating and distributing his own audio content in the early 2000s, and his work helped to popularize the medium.
Podcasting has become a popular way for people to share their thoughts, opinions, and ideas on a wide range of topics, from news and politics to comedy and entertainment. It has also become a way for people to build a community around their content, with listeners subscribing and sharing their favorite shows with others.
The advantages of podcasting include:
1. Low cost: Podcasting is a relatively low-cost way to produce and distribute audio content, as it eliminates the need for expensive studios or radio stations.
2. Flexibility: Podcasts can be created and distributed at any time and from any location, as long as there is

Two twin domes, two radically opposed design cultures. One is made of thousands of steel parts, the other of a single silk thread. One is synthetic, the other organic. One is imposed on the environment, the other creates it. One is designed for nature, the other is designed by her. Michelangelo said that when he looked at raw marble, he saw a figure struggling to be free. The chisel was Michelangelo's only tool. But living things are not chiseled. They grow. And in our smallest units of life, our cells, we carry all the information required for every other cell to function and to replicate. Tools also have consequences. At least since the Industrial Revolution, the world of design has been dominated by the rigors of manufacturing and mass production.

We introduce a novel Skyrme-like conserved current in the effective theory of pions and vector mesons based on the idea of hidden local symmetry. The associated charge is equivalent to the Skyrmion charge for any smooth configuration. In addition, there exist singular configurations that can be identified as \( n_f=1 \) baryons charged under the new symmetry. Under this identification, the vector mesons play the role of the Chern-Simons vector fields living on the quantum Hall droplet that forms the \( n_f=1 \) baryon. We propose that this current is the correct effective expression for the baryon current at low energies. This proposal gives a unified picture for the two types of baryons and allows them to continuously transform one to the other in a natural way. In addition, Chern-Simons dualities on the droplet can be interpreted as a result of Seiberg-like duality between gluons and vector mesons.

In a scathing critique of the Iraq War, Cardinal Cormac Murphy-O'Connor, the head of the Catholic Church in England and Wales, has lamented the staggering billions of pounds squandered on conflict in the Middle East, arguing that these vast resources could have been better utilised to alleviate the scourge of poverty. The Cardinal's impassioned remarks, aired on BBC Radio 4, will be reiterated in his Christmas Midnight Mass, as he seizes the opportunity to reiterate his fervent opposition to the war. In a poignant Christmas message, Cardinal Murphy-O'Connor denounced the Iraq War as a "terrible" misallocation of funds, underscoring the tragic consequences of diverting vital resources away from the most vulnerable members of society. His sentiments echo those of the Archbishop of Canterbury, who has also spoken out against the war. The Cardinal's powerful plea, delivered during the 'Thought for the Day' segment on BBC Radio 4, served as a clarion call to action, urging the world to harness its collective energy to eradicate the debilitating spectres of poverty, malnutrition, and disease. As the world embarks on a new year, Cardinal Murphy-O'Connor has issued a rallying cry, proclaiming 2005 as a pivotal moment to galvanise global efforts and "make history" by vanquishing poverty.

The emaciated orphan pleading for another meager portion of gruel. The lovelorn maiden fading into oblivion while clinging to her threadbare bridal gown. The callous miser tormented by spectral visions of bygone holidays. Even after a hundred years, these vivid characters from Charles Dickens' literary universe remain indelibly etched in our collective consciousness. His extraordinary oeuvre was so distinctive that it spawned its own descriptive term in the English lexicon. Yet what quintessential elements render Dickens' prose so remarkably enduring?

Dickens' narratives pulsate with suspense, masterfully crafted through atmospheric settings brimming with foreboding, unexpected narrative turns, and tantalizing enigmas. These signature techniques created an insatiable hunger among his readership, compelling them to eagerly await each new installment. Originally, his tales unfolded serially - published chapter by chapter in inexpensive periodicals before being compiled into bound volumes, a format that perfectly suited his suspenseful storytelling genius.

We are confronted with a crisis of unparalleled magnitude. Please prepare to receive a statement from António Guterres, the Secretary-General of the United Nations.

There is an increasing sense of urgency and impatience, particularly among the youth, due to the lack of decisive global action. It is crucial that we see greater ambition from all sectors: governments, cities, businesses, investors, and individuals alike.

Now, let us engage in this race with renewed political resolve and vigor.

Warframe is a fun game for the first few hours of play. After those initial enjoyable hours, you'll begin to notice that the game's offerings become repetitive in its combat, enemies, and gameplay. One major highlight is that the developers are working hard and frequently release updates that generally include a lot of content, from sweeping changes to some of the game's systems, to new warframes and other tweaks/features. It's a good game for casual play, though I doubt it will hold your attention beyond a few months unless you really enjoy grinding. The upside to all of this is that the game is free, so I'd recommend giving it a try. If you're looking for a quick fix, try it out.

+RPG leveling +Wide variety of ship types +Physics-based movement for both ships and projectiles/missiles +Easy-to-learn resource management +Dark humor -Grind-intensive (if underutilizing the civvy station types) -Subpar AI teammates -Low variety of equipment types -Low variety of leveling skills (no branching)  

Listen, this game is fun as hell. Despite the minuses above, there's still plenty of variety and strategy to enjoy. It starts off fast and really picks up. Even with limited equipment variety, it feels like there's always another ship to unlock or skill to level up. The best way to extend the game's lifespan is through mods. *Fight for Universe* is easily the standout, but *Big Fight for Beef* is also great.

Menstruation. A natural biological process, long shrouded in secrecy and stigma, has been transformed into a hushed conversation. Despite the fact that over 800 million women worldwide are menstruating at any given time, the topic remains a source of discomfort and embarrassment. As I speak, women are silently enduring the physical and emotional toll of their periods, often hiding their discomfort with a sense of shame. From my own experience and that of those around me, I can attest to the exhaustion that comes with navigating this natural yet taboo aspect of womanhood. The constant need to be discreet, the struggle to maintain a sense of normalcy despite the pain and discomfort, and the isolation that can result from feeling like an outcast are all too familiar. It is time for this stigma to be broken, for menstruation to be recognized as a natural and necessary part of life, and for women to be afforded the respect and dignity they deserve.

Suddenly, over the past two days, I've been overwhelmed by severe bouts of exhaustion—utterly bone-tiring. Last evening, I went home and fell asleep, only waking when my mother called me around eight-thirty. When I woke up, it was completely dark, and I hadn't turned on the lights, so for a moment, I couldn't tell what time or day it was. It was just my mother's voice breaking through the darkness while I tried to grasp reality. I wonder why I'm feeling so drained... The weather is nice this morning. It was drizzling when I arrived. Though I've heard it's been raining in Calcutta for the last three days.

"The Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe (OSCE) was invited by the U.S. State Department to observe the upcoming presidential election. The monitoring team, drawn from the OSCE’s Office for Democratic Institutions and Human Rights, marks the first time such international observers will be present for a U.S. election.  

Meanwhile, a group of thirteen congressional Democrats—including four representatives from New York—has formally requested that U.N. Secretary-General Kofi Annan deploy international monitors to Florida to oversee the voting process. However, I respectfully disagree with this proposal. Available information suggests that the OSCE’s role is to study the U.S. electoral system rather than to supervise it—a distinction that renders the earlier call for U.N. intervention both politically motivated and unnecessary.

Prince was crowned the top music earner in 2004, outperforming other pop legends such as Madonna and Elton John in US Magazine's annual list. The singer generated a staggering $56.5 million (£30.4 million) from concerts, album, and publishing sales with his successful Musicology Tour and album. He maintained his lead over Madonna, who earned $54.9 million (£29.5 million) during her global re-invention tour. Veteran duo Simon and Garfunkel placed 10th, with their comeback tour contributing $24.9 million (£13.4 million) to their earnings last year. Prince's triumphant return to the stage after a decade in the commercial wilderness was hailed by the magazine, with his 2004 tour grossing a remarkable $90.3 million (£48.5 million) in ticket sales and selling 1.9 million copies of his critically acclaimed album Musicology.

I've been overcome with an overwhelming sense of exhaustion for the past two days. I've been plagued by debilitating fatigue, feeling as though I'm running on empty. Last night, I returned home and collapsed onto my bed, only stirring when my mother's voice pierced the darkness around 8 30 pm. As I slowly came to, I was disoriented and disoriented, struggling to discern the time or date. For a few disorienting moments, it was just my mother's gentle voice in the darkness, and I was grappling with the harsh reality. I find myself wondering why I'm feeling so drained... The morning brought a welcome respite, with a gentle drizzle falling from the sky. Although I'm told it's been raining continuously for the past three days in Calcutta.

Some people are jumping to connect the dots: the Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe was invited to monitor the election by the State Department. The observers will come from the OSCE's Office for Democratic Institutions and Human Rights. This will be the first time such a team has been present for a U.S. presidential election. Meanwhile, a baker's dozen of congressional Democrats, including four New Yorkers, has petitioned U.N. Secretary-General Kofi Annan to dispatch international observers to Florida to oversee this year's presidential balloting there. I humbly disagree. Based on the available information, the OSCE seems to be coming here to learn from the U.S. election process, not to oversee it, which was the original partisan and childish call for U.N. involvement.

As the deadline approaches for the Fiat-GM agreement, both automotive giants have until midnight on February 1st to resolve their conflict over a possible acquisition. This deadline is crucial as it would allow Fiat to sell its car division to General Motors, a provision stemming from their 2000 alliance. However, GM, grappling with its own financial losses in its European division, is now reluctant to take on the financially troubled Fiat unit. News of stalled negotiations led to a 1.2% drop in Fiat's share price on Tuesday, following a 4% increase on Monday prompted by speculation of a lucrative settlement. GM reportedly proposes to pay approximately $2 billion (£1.06 billion) to disengage from the agreement, asserting that the deal was nullified by Fiat's decision to divest its finance arm and dilute GM's stake through a capital restructuring. The original 2000 agreement emerged during a competitive period when GM and DaimlerChrysler vied for a partnership with Fiat.

When a new user signs up on a website, we typically have no information about him or her, i.e., no interaction with items, no user profile, and no social links with other users. Under such circumstances, it is crucial for our recommender systems to effectively attract the users at the first interaction, so that the users decide to stay on the website and become active users. This problem falls under the category of new user cold-start, and it is crucial to the development and survival of a company. Existing works on user cold-start recommendation either require additional user efforts, such as setting up an interview process, or making use of side information, such as user demographics, locations, social relations, etc. However, users may not be willing to provide the interview and side information on cold-start users is usually not available, which can hinder the effectiveness of these methods.

For two decades, I dedicated my career to the world of finance, mastering its intricacies with absolute certainty. The global economy wasn't just my profession—it was my life. While I could discuss ecological concerns or apocalyptic theories with effortless familiarity, I dismissed them as improbable. Then came 2007, a year marked by an unprecedented upheaval: the subprime crisis and the catastrophic fall of major American banks. This seismic event struck me like lightning. It sparked heated debates with my wife about my vocation and the fundamental flaws in our economic framework. Beyond the immediate turmoil, I came to a sobering realization—my convictions had been profoundly misguided, and we were steering toward catastrophe.

According to a two-year study, more women and older individuals are taking proactive measures to secure their home computers against online threats. The number of women purchasing software to shield PCs from viruses, spam, and spyware has increased by 11.2% annually between 2002 and 2004. Conducted for the net security firm Preventon, the study indicates that security awareness is expanding among a diverse range of internet users. Notably, 40% of those who purchase home internet security programs are retirees, with this number rising by an average of 13.2% over the past three years. Interestingly, more retired women (53%) are investing in security software compared to their male counterparts. This research highlights the evolving demographics and shattered stereotypes of web users, alongside an increasing recognition of the risks associated with high-speed broadband connections.

The orientational dynamics within the isotropic phase of a comb-shaped nematic polymer—incorporating both mesogenic and functional side groups—were investigated through Kerr effect measurements and dielectric spectroscopy. This study revealed a novel finding: unlike low-molecular-weight mesogens, the electric birefringence relaxation in a mesogenic polymer melt above the nematic-isotropic transition temperature can be characterized by a superposition of multiple exponential decay processes. Among these, two dominant relaxation mechanisms were identified, which exhibit a temperature-dependent exchange of dominance across an approximately 50-degree range.  

Complementary dielectric spectroscopy further resolved two distinct orientational relaxation processes: the first arising from the rotational motion of the mesogenic side groups and the second linked to the segmental dynamics of the polymer backbone. This dual-method approach provides unprecedented insight into the hierarchical relaxation behavior of comb-shaped nematic polymers in their isotropic state.

Density-based clustering operates on the principle of associating groups with specific characteristics of the underlying data's probability distribution. By referencing the true (though unknown) population structure, this approach positions the clustering problem within a standard inferential framework, where the ideal population clustering is defined as the partition generated by the true density function. The nonparametric implementation of this method, termed modal clustering, establishes a connection between groups and the domains of attraction of the density's modes. Practically, this requires a nonparametric density estimate, and careful selection of the smoothing parameter—which influences both the density's shape and its modal structure—is essential for determining the final partition. In this study, we examine the challenge of density estimation for modal clustering from an asymptotic standpoint.

Attention, Magic: The Gathering enthusiasts—prepare for disappointment. Wizards of the Coast has failed to deliver a truly engaging digital experience for its beloved card game. At its heart, Magic thrives on social interaction—the camaraderie of friends (or rivals), the banter, the shared excitement of game-changing plays. Yet Wizards appears to have lost sight of this essence.  

The current system actively discourages playing with friends by denying gold rewards, the game's essential currency. While the daily gold cap makes sense—given that gold unlocks nearly all in-game content—it forces players into a tedious, isolating grind. You're left with two bleak options: mindless 1v1 matches against AI or silent, impersonal battles with strangers.  

For free-to-play users, the grind is especially punishing. Expect to invest roughly four hours daily for over a month—solo—just to build a viable collection. Is this really how Magic should be experienced? The answer is a resounding no.

I recently downloaded the game and spent a brief period playing it, but unfortunately, it failed to impress me. The login process was more time-consuming than the actual gameplay, as I encountered a recurring issue where the "update to terms of service" prompt would repeatedly appear, requiring me to restart the client to progress. This is somewhat inconvenient, especially since I was asked to link my Steam and GFace accounts, citing the need for downloadable content (DLC). However, this linking is not reversible, which means that a company I'm not familiar with now has access to my Steam information. In terms of the game itself, I found it to be a noticeable clone of another game, with the added disappointment of subpar gunplay. It's astonishing that the developers managed to make it worse, as it's clear that they had the potential to do better.

The Paris triumph has sparked hope among Welsh fans. Is there a more thrilling Six Nations match than Saturday's unforgettable clash in Paris? And can the Welsh resurgence continue to claim their first Grand Slam since 1978? These are the pressing questions not just for Welsh supporters, but for rugby fans everywhere, following a spectacular performance in Paris. Martyn Williams, who scored two tries for Wales that day, described it as "one of the most surreal games I've ever played." A dejected French coach, Bernard Laporte, simply noted: "There was a French half and there was a Welsh half.

Continuous-time quantum walks have proven to be a highly versatile framework for the development of various quantum algorithms. The running time of these algorithms is often characterized by the quantum hitting time, which represents the time required for the quantum walk to locate a vertex of interest with a high probability. In this article, we present refined upper bounds for the quantum hitting time that can be applied to several ctqw-based quantum algorithms. Specifically, we utilize our techniques to enhance the hitting time upper bound for the glued-trees problem from $o(n^5)$ to $o(n^2\log n)$. Moreover, our methods also lead to an exponential improvement in the dependence on precision of the continuous-time quantum walk-based algorithm for finding a marked node on any ergodic, reversible Markov chain, as demonstrated by Chakraborty et al.

I realize this might sound unusual, but I believe robots have the potential to inspire us to become better humans. I spent my childhood in Bethlehem, Pennsylvania, a town closely tied to Bethlehem Steel. My father was an engineer, and during my formative years, he would teach me the intricacies of how things function. Together, we embarked on projects like building model rockets and slot cars. This is the go-kart we constructed as a team. That's me at the wheel, alongside my sister and my best friend from that time. One day, when I was around 10 years old, my father came home and, during dinner, revealed that our next venture would be to build... a robot.

Sideways dominated this year's Independent Spirit Awards, taking home all six of the awards it was nominated for, including Best Film, Director, and Screenplay. Alexander Payne and writing partner Jim Taylor also won for their work on the film, while actors Paul Giamatti, Thomas Haden Church, and Virginia Madsen were recognized for their performances. Sideways is expected to fare well at the upcoming Oscars, with five nominations under its belt. The Independent Spirit Awards, now in their 20th year, are held the day before the Oscars and recognize films made outside of the traditional studio system. Other winners included Catalina Sandino Moreno, who took home the Best Actress award for her role as a drug smuggler in the Colombian drama Maria Full of Grace. Moreno is also nominated for Best Actress at the Oscars.

Summer television used to be a wasteland filled with reruns, but now two exciting new shows have captured my attention. I've been watching both shows religiously over the past week. One of them, "Blow Out," features an unexpectedly flamboyant concept with hardly any visible LGBTQIA+ representation. It follows hairstylist Jonathan Antin, who is aiming to open a second branch of his widely successful West Hollywood salon, this time in Beverly Hills. Ordinarily, I wouldn't be interested at all. However, while flipping through channels, I happened upon Jonathan, who, with a mix of vanity and monotony, gazed at an empty space on Rodeo Drive and declared, "I am going to build here the greatest salon in the history of the world ever!" Strangely, I was hooked. Not since "Rocco" have I found a character this compelling on television, despite their apparent foolishness.

Enthusiasts of the Magic: The Gathering universe, be warned: Wizards of the Coast appears to have lost sight of the game's fundamental social essence in its digital iteration. At its heart, Magic is a communal experience, meant to be savored with friends and acquaintances, replete with lively banter, good-natured teasing, and the thrill of landing a game-changing blow. Regrettably, the digital version seems to neglect this crucial aspect, instead prioritizing solitary play. Those who opt to play with friends are denied the opportunity to earn in-game gold, a vital resource that unlocks the full potential of the game. While the daily gold cap is understandable, given its significance in accessing premium content, the consequence is a tedious and isolating grind against AI opponents or random players, devoid of meaningful interaction. For those who choose to play without a subscription, be prepared for a lengthy and monotonous journey, requiring a substantial time commitment of approximately four hours per day, spanning several weeks, all while playing in solitude, in order to unlock the coveted cards.

It's 11:14 p.m. MST, and here I sit in my bedroom fixing the personal computer of the personnel director from my job. Not that I mind doing it, but I sometimes wonder how often I find myself fixing someone else's computer as a favor. I'm glad to be able to help and support people in this way, but where is the line drawn when you reach the point of being taken advantage of? The last couple of weeks, I've been staying up late reading and such, so I was looking forward to going to bed early until I realized it would be more prudent to get this computer out of my bedroom and back to its owner. *laugh* No rest for the weary, right? So I found myself watching Joe Schmo 2 again tonight.

I'm thrilled to embark on the epic quest of the Rosetta spacecraft, a mission that has captivated my attention for the past two years. As we prepare to escort and land the probe on a comet, I'd like to take a moment to share with you the fascinating story of the solar system's origin. Let's journey back in time, approximately 4.5 billion years ago, to a era when a vast cloud of gas and dust enveloped our cosmic neighborhood. At the heart of this primordial cloud, our sun burst forth, igniting the spark of life. As the planets, comets, and asteroids took shape, a pivotal event occurred  the delivery of comets to Earth. According to theory, these celestial bodies, now known as comets, struck our planet with immense force, depositing water and potentially even complex organic matter, which may have jumpstarted the emergence of life on Earth.

I had the opportunity to experience EU at a later stage, having purchased it on sale, which also included EW. As a result, I was fortunate enough to avoid the initial issues that some players encountered. Despite its challenging early stages, I thoroughly enjoyed the game, finding it to be a truly rewarding experience when executed correctly. In contrast, my experience with XCOM2 has been overwhelmingly positive. The game runs smoothly on medium settings, even on my relatively outdated GTX560 with 2GB of VRAM. Initially, the game defaulted to high settings, resulting in some minor performance issues, but reducing the settings to medium resolved the problem entirely. It's possible that I've simply been fortunate in avoiding the issues that have plagued some players, but it's difficult not to notice that many of the negative reviews seem to stem from an unrealistic sense of entitlement.

**Polished Version:**  

Daily health headlines bombard us with bold claims—from a single vitamin slashing heart disease risk to chocolate easing student stress, or breakthrough drugs extending lives in rare conditions. Yet these attention-grabbing statements often clash with the nuanced, incremental findings of the actual research they cite. How, then, can you discern fact from exaggeration? The key lies in scrutinizing the original studies behind the headlines. To illustrate this, we’ve crafted three hypothetical research scenarios mirroring these flashy claims. Watch as we unpack the first example, then pause to reflect on the headline’s validity. Note: these are simplified for clarity.

The orientational dynamics in the isotropic phase of a comb-shaped nematic polymer with mesogenic and functional side groups were studied using Kerr effect and dielectric spectroscopy. For the first time, it was found that in a mesogenic polymer, unlike low-molecular-weight mesogens, the relaxation of electric birefringence of a melt above the nematic-isotropic phase transition temperature can be described by a sum of several exponential processes, two of which play a decisive role. These main processes replace each other within a temperature range of about fifty degrees. Dielectric spectroscopy also revealed two orientational relaxation processes: the first arises from rotation of the side mesogenic groups, while the second is associated with motion of the main chain segments.

You’ve been accused of a crime you did not commit. It’s impossible to prove your innocence. If you insist that you’re innocent anyway, you’ll likely be found guilty and executed. But if you confess, apologize, and implicate others for good measure, you’ll go free. Do you give a false confession—or risk a public hanging? This was the choice facing those accused of witchcraft in the village of Salem, Massachusetts, between February 1692 and May 1693. They were the victims of paranoia about the supernatural, misdirected religious fervor—and a justice system that valued repentance over truth. Salem was settled in 1626 by Puritans, a group of English Protestants. Life was strict and isolated for the people of Salem. Battles with their Native American neighbors and groups of French settlers were commonplace.

Without a doubt, this is my favorite first-person shooter of all time. It's not for those who favor run-and-gun tactics. The recoil and ballistics are remarkably realistic: a single hit to the chest, neck, or head from a rifle-caliber weapon almost always results in death unless it just grazes you. Bullets drop over distance, and short, controlled bursts are necessary to stay on target. There isn't an unlimited ammunition pool; if you consistently reload halfway through a magazine, you'll end up with a collection of half-full mags. You can't run indefinitely—if you sprint for a while, your aim will suffer as you catch your breath. Steady your aim by resting your weapon on your elbows while prone or on a window sill. Accuracy diminishes when standing.

We study the phenomenology of a hypercharge-zero triplet scalar, motivated by two-step electroweak symmetry-breaking. We consider both the possibility that the triplets are stable and contribute to the dark matter density, or that they decay via mixing with the standard model Higgs boson. The former is constrained by disappearing charged track searches at the LHC and by dark matter direct detection experiments, while the latter is constrained by existing multi-lepton collider searches. We find that a two-step electroweak phase transition involving a stable triplet with a negative quadratic term is ruled out by direct detection searches, and an unstable triplet with a mass less than 230 GeV is excluded at 95% confidence level.

More women are turning to internet security, as older people and women increasingly take charge of protecting home computers against malicious online attacks, according to a two-year study. The number of women buying programs to protect PCs from viruses, spam, and spyware attacks rose by 11.2% annually between 2002 and 2004. The study, conducted for internet security firm Preventon, shows that security messages are reaching a diverse range of internet users. It is estimated that 40% of those purchasing home internet security programs are retired. Over the last three years, this figure has increased by an average of 13.2%. However, more retired women (53%) bought security software than retired men. The research reflects shifting stereotypes and demographics of web users, as well as growing awareness of the heightened risks that high-speed broadband connections can pose to internet users.

The quest to unravel how strongly correlated two-dimensional systems can produce unconventional superconductivity with elevated critical temperatures remains a pivotal challenge in condensed matter physics. Ultracold two-dimensional Fermi gases have emerged as pristine and tunable platforms for probing the intricate relationship between strong correlations and reduced dimensionality. However, definitive proof of superfluidity in such systems has remained elusive.  

In this study, we present conclusive evidence of superfluidity in an ultracold 2D Fermi gas by observing the absence of dissipation when a periodic potential is swept through the system below a critical velocity, \( v_{\rm c} \). We systematically measure \( v_{\rm c} \) across varying interaction strengths and identify a peak in the crossover regime between bosonic and fermionic superfluidity. These findings solidify ultracold Fermi gases as a versatile experimental framework for investigating the profound effects of reduced dimensionality on strongly correlated superfluids.

Entanglement serves as a fundamental resource in quantum information processing. A commonly employed method for detecting entanglement is the entanglement witness, where the measurement of the witness operator yields non-negative results for all separable states but may produce negative values for certain entangled states. In practice, constructing an efficient entanglement witness for general multipartite entangled states is highly challenging due to the exponential growth of the Hilbert-space dimension with increasing system size. For $n$-partite Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ)-like states, the most robust witness scheme necessitates $n+1$ local measurement settings and can withstand up to $1/2$ white noise. In contrast, the most efficient witness for GHZ-like states requires only two local measurement settings and remains effective for white noise levels up to $1/3$.

What is a parent? It's not an easy question. Today, we have adoption, stepfamilies, and surrogate mothers. Many parents face tough questions and tough decisions. Should we tell our child about the sperm donation? If so, when? What words should we use? Sperm donors are often referred to as "biological fathers," but should we really be using the word "father"? As a philosopher and social scientist, I have been studying these questions about the concept of parenthood. But today, I will talk to you about what I learned from talking to parents and children. I will show you that they know what matters most in a family, even though their family may look a little different.

This paper introduces a millimeter-scale CMOS 64$\times$64 single charged particle radiation detector system designed for external beam cancer radiotherapy. A 1$\times$1 $\mu m^2$ diode is employed to measure the energy deposited by a single charged particle within the depletion region. The array offers a substantial detection area of 512$\times$512 $\mu m^2$. Instead of detecting the voltage drop due to radiation, the system captures the pulse width, which is the duration for the voltage to return to its baseline. This approach eliminates the need for power-intensive and bulky analog-to-digital converters. A prototype ASIC has been fabricated using the TSMC 65 nm LP CMOS process, with an average static power consumption of 0.535 mW under a 1.2 V analog and digital power supply. The entire system's functionality is successfully validated in a clinical setting using a 67.5 MeV proton beam.

Understanding how strongly correlated two-dimensional (2D) systems can lead to unconventional superconductivity with high critical temperatures remains one of the most significant unresolved challenges in condensed matter physics. Ultracold 2D Fermi gases have become ideal model systems—clean and highly controllable—for investigating the interplay between strong correlations and reduced dimensionality. However, direct evidence of superfluidity in such systems has remained elusive. In this study, we observe superfluidity in an ultracold 2D Fermi gas by moving a periodic potential through the system and detecting the absence of dissipation below a critical velocity \(v_{\rm c}\). We measure \(v_{\rm c}\) across varying interaction strengths and identify a peak in the crossover regime between bosonic and fermionic superfluidity. These results firmly establish ultracold Fermi gases as a versatile platform for exploring the effects of reduced dimensionality on strongly correlated superfluids.

England rugby union captain Jason Robinson has set his sights on a historic dual-code triumph over Australia this Saturday. As a former rugby league international who made the switch to union in 2000, Robinson will lead his team against the Wallabies at Twickenham, with the match kicking off at 1430 GMT. Later that evening, at 1815 GMT, Great Britain's rugby league team will face off against Australia in the Tri-Nations tournament final at Elland Road. Achieving victories in both matches would be a monumental feat, particularly for the rugby league code, according to Robinson. A win for England would not only secure their third consecutive autumn international victory, following successes against Canada and South Africa, but also provide a chance for redemption after their crushing 51-15 defeat to Australia in June. Meanwhile, a Great Britain victory would bring an end to a 34-year drought without a win against their Australian counterparts.

Deadline looms for Fiat-GM deal; Fiat and General Motors (GM) have until midnight on February 1 to settle a disagreement over a potential takeover. The deadline marks the point at which Fiat will gain the right to sell its car division to GM, as part of an alliance agreed upon in 2000. However, GM, whose European operations are currently losing money, no longer wishes to own the unprofitable Fiat unit. Reports of deadlocked talks sent Fiat shares down 1.2% on Tuesday, after Monday's 4% gain on hopes of a resolution. The US firm is believed to be offering around $2 billion (£1.06 billion) to extricate itself from the arrangement. GM has argued that the deal was voided by Fiat's decision to sell off its finance arm and halve GM's stake through a capital-raising effort.

The ordinary differential equation (ODE) is a mathematical model widely used in various application domains, including climatology, bioinformatics, and chemical engineering, due to its intuitive ability to model complex phenomena. Despite the widespread use of ODEs in modeling, the frequent absence of their analytical solutions makes it challenging to estimate ODE parameters from the data, particularly when the model has a large number of variables and parameters. This paper proposes a Bayesian ODE parameter estimation algorithm that is fast and accurate, even for models with numerous parameters. The proposed method approximates an ODE model with a state-space model based on equations of a numerical solver, enabling fast estimation by avoiding the computation of a complete numerical solution in the likelihood. The posterior is obtained through a variational Bayes method, specifically, the approximate Riemannian conjugate gradient method (Honkela et al.

We developed single-shot, shaped pulses for ultra-high fidelity population transfer on a three-level quantum system in a lambda configuration. To ensure high fidelity, we employ the Lewis-Riesenfeld (L-R) method to derive a family of solutions leading to an exact transfer, where the solutions follow a single dynamical mode of the L-R invariant. Among this family, we identify a tracking solution with a single parameter to control simultaneously the fidelity of the transfer, the population of the excited state, and robustness. We define a measure of the robustness of an ultra-high-fidelity transfer as the minimum percentile deviation on the pulse areas at which the infidelity rises above 10^-4. The robustness of our shaped pulses is found superior to that of Gaussian and adiabatically-optimized pulses for moderate pulse areas.

I can't think of anyone who has only one interest in life, and that's all they want to do for the rest of their life. Around 15% of American workers don't have traditional full-time jobs; they're half-time, part-time, contract workers or temps. The term "side hustle" seems to fit with this ethos, where people are piecing together a few different things to make a living. The word "side hustle" has its roots in popular African American newspapers. By the 1950s, these papers used the term "hustle" to refer to some kind of scam, but by the 1950s, they were also using "side hustle" to refer to legitimate work. A side hustle is distinct from a second job, which is often about necessity.

The capacity for human communication is a remarkable phenomenon, one that enables us to convey intricate ideas and thoughts to one another with remarkable precision. This extraordinary ability is rooted in our unique capacity for language, which allows us to encode complex concepts into a series of sounds, tones, and vibrations that can be transmitted through the air. As I speak, my vocalizations create a cascade of pressure waves that propagate through the atmosphere, eventually reaching your ears and triggering a response in your brain. This process, known as auditory perception, enables your brain to decode the vibrations and interpret them as meaningful thoughts and ideas. Through this remarkable process, we are able to transcend spatial and temporal boundaries, sharing knowledge and ideas across vast distances and bridging the gaps between individual minds.

Imagine being able to connect your mind to a device that promises endless pleasure for the rest of your life. If presented with this opportunity, would you take it? This is the intriguing question posed by philosopher Robert Nozick in his thought experiment known as the Experience Machine. This scenario invites us to envision a world where scientists have created a machine that replicates real life while ensuring perpetual pleasure without any suffering. The trade-off? You must abandon reality permanently, although you would scarcely notice the difference. Your experiences would feel indistinguishable from real life. The natural highs and lows of life would be replaced with a never-ending series of highs. Tempting, isn't it?

The provided text is a summary of a research paper that aims to investigate multi-agent systems with partial observation. The authors propose a scalable supervisor, which is a supervisory system that can handle a large number of agents and can be used in systems where only partial observation is possible. The supervisor is designed to be independent of the number of agents in the system and can be used in both centralized and decentralized systems. The authors also propose sufficient conditions for safety and maximal permissiveness of the scalable least restrictive supervisor on the template level. An example is provided to illustrate the proposed approach.

While I personally cannot recommend this game, it wasn't absolutely terrible. The gunplay is alright, so long as you have the money to purchase gun parts, which would be my main complaint with this game: it's pay-to-win. If I could get past that, the game would be enjoyable, as the hardsuit is fun to use and HRV is an excellent idea. I'd take this game over any Call of Duty title any day, but its pay-to-win microtransaction format is off-putting for me. If you're alright with this model, then by all means, have fun. But if you're like me and dislike pay-to-win games, just let this one slide; it's not our cup of tea.

Warframe is an enjoyable game for the initial few hours of play, offering an engaging experience. However, as time progresses, the game's limitations become apparent, with repetition in combat, enemies, and gameplay becoming the primary focus. A notable exception is the developers' dedication to regular updates, which often include substantial content additions, such as significant system overhauls, new warframes, and various tweaks and features. This makes Warframe suitable for casual play. Nevertheless, its ability to sustain long-term interest is questionable, unless you have a strong affinity for grinding. On the positive side, the game is free, making it worth trying out, especially if you're looking for a brief gaming fix.

This game is so... very... boring. I bought it because I believed it was a PC version of *Katamari Damacy*, a popular and fun PS2 game. Sadly, it's far from it. In this game, you go around absorbing ever bigger things to grow larger and absorb even bigger things. But the gameplay isn't as smooth as it could be, the levels aren't inventive, and what's there is a surreal horror show. I don't mean an entertaining horror show—rather, one that makes you feel depressed and question whether being alive is all it's cracked up to be. Every "living" creature in this game, from small pets to human beings, makes me wonder if the creators had an interest in taxidermy.

The Weak Gravity Conjecture (WGC) requires the existence of superextremal particles in any consistent quantum theory of gravity. The standard understanding is that these particles ensure extremal black holes are either unstable or marginally stable, but it remains unclear what goes wrong if this condition fails. This note demonstrates that, for a generic Einsteinian quantum theory of gravity in AdS, exact stability of extremal black branes conflicts with rigorously proven quantum information theorems about entanglement entropy. Avoiding this contradiction leads to a nonperturbative version of the WGC, which reduces to the usual statement at weak coupling. The argument is general and does not rely on supersymmetry or a specific UV completion, assuming only the validity of Einsteinian gravity, effective field theory, and holography.

Baryonic feedback effects lead to a suppression of the weak lensing angular power spectrum on small scales. The poorly constrained shape and amplitude of this suppression are an important source of uncertainties for upcoming cosmological weak lensing surveys such as Euclid or LSST. In this first paper in a series of two, we use simulations to build a Euclid-like tomographic mock data-set for the cosmic shear power spectrum and the corresponding covariance matrix, which are both corrected for baryonic effects following the baryonification method of Schneider et al. (2019). In addition, we develop an emulator to obtain fast predictions of the baryonic power suppression, allowing us to perform a likelihood inference analysis for a standard $\Lambda$CDM cosmology with both cosmological and astrophysical parameters.

I'm a cultural omnivore, whose daily commute is fueled by an iPod filled with a diverse range of artists, from classical composers like Wagner and Mozart, to pop diva Christina Aguilera, country singer Josh Turner, and gangsta rap artist Kirk Franklin. My music library also features concerti, symphonies, and much more. As a voracious reader, my tastes span from Ian McEwan to Stephanie Meyer, and I've even devoured the entire Twilight tetralogy. At home, I indulge in my home theater, where I watch a wide array of DVDs, video on demand, and television shows. My favorite programs include "Law & Order: SVU," Tina Fey's "30 Rock," and "Judge Judy," where the cases are real, the people are real, and the rulings are final.

Sid Meier's Civilization: Beyond Earth is a turn-based game that challenges players to build their civilization to a predetermined goal or 'victory.' Unlike previous games in the series, this installment takes place in the future and introduces new mechanics such as the affinity system and orbital layer. While I appreciate 2K's effort to innovate and add new features, I found the game to be overwhelming at times due to these new mechanics. One area that could be improved is the addition of new artwork and visuals, particularly in the communication and interaction with other leaders. By incorporating more detailed and visually striking environments, the game could become more immersive and engaging.

Spirit Awards hail 'Sideways' as the comedy has dominated this year's Independent Spirit Awards, winning all six of the awards for which it was nominated. It was named Best Film, while Alexander Payne won Best Director and Best Screenplay, along with writing partner Jim Taylor. It also won acting awards for stars Paul Giamatti, Thomas Haden Church, and Virginia Madsen. 'Sideways' is tipped to do well at Sunday's Oscars, with five nominations. The awards, now in their 20th year, are given to films made outside the traditional studio system and are traditionally held the day before the Oscars. Other winners included Catalina Sandino Moreno, who took Best Actress for her role as a drug smuggler in the Colombian drama 'Maria Full of Grace.' Moreno is also nominated for Best Actress at the Oscars. The Best First Screenplay award went to Joshua Marston for 'Maria Full of Grace.'

You can't help it, you can't evade it: a piece of you will always live in me. Even if I never carried you child, even if I was never married to you, even if I was never an official pair to you... I loved you immensely, and to this day the feeling carries an echo through my heart. Forever will I always be reminded of the very few nights we spent together, hearts and limbs entwined in a soul-embrace, drenched in the salty darkness of an evening by the beach... Or a kiss under the rain. Whenever I get the feeling of a dark glass puncturing my heart, you will be there. That piece of you... Bleeding the eternal love that once lived in me... And still remains. For always.

Damn fine AAA FPS shooter - that's the bottom line. It looks, sounds, and plays great - great FPS mechanics, nice story, cool characters, flawless voice acting, and a badass soundtrack. Not for the faint-hearted, though - some of those cutscenes were pretty darn cruel and vivid. But when a game overall is so great, it's easier to spot the flaws, and here they are as I saw them: - no SLI support - what the heck? I had to put the game away until I upgraded my card, as it ran too poorly on my old dual-GPU GTX 590. Make sure you have a fairly decent single-GPU card. - Stupidly small obstacles are often unclimbable.

I downloaded the game and played it for a short time, but it didn’t stand out. I spent more time attempting to log in than actually playing. There's a bug that claims the terms of service have been updated, but when you click 'accept,' the message just reappears. You have to restart the client to resolve this issue. That alone isn't too bad, but why do I need to link my Steam and Gface accounts? They claim it's necessary for DLC, yet this seems unnecessary. Moreover, it's irreversible, so now a company I don't care about has access to my Steam information. The game itself is just average. It clearly imitates Call of Duty, but they've somehow managed to make the gunplay even worse. How is that even possible?

I find investigative reporting to be problematic. It seems wrong to me, or perhaps I should rephrase that, the article I'm working on and its consequences are not exactly what I'm comfortable with. Perhaps it's because I don't fully believe in it, and therefore I can't fully embrace it. The angst behind it. The full-out passion and commitment required to question everything. I've grown up in a country where questioning is not tolerated. Really, you just don't do it. They shoot you for that shit. They put you in jail with all these internal security measures. I'm not used to this freedom of speech...not used to questioning out loud. Sure, I'm not the easiest person to get along with.

This past week has been incredibly exhausting. From Tuesday to Thursday, I was busy completing the transition to my new classroom. The new space is larger and closer to my grade-level team, which are important factors as I invest countless hours setting it up for the year ahead. Had I known about the move before the school year ended, I would have parted with many unused books and supplies. Unfortunately, I ended up bringing everything along. So, this week was filled with moving, unpacking, and a thorough decluttering process. I'm very grateful to my teammate Jill for her assistance. She continued teaching in my old room this month, and with the help of her wonderful students, she managed to move almost everything for me!

In this paper, we investigate multi-agent discrete-event systems with partial observation. The agents are divided into several groups, where each group contains agents with similar (isomorphic) state transition structures, allowing them to be relabeled into the same template. Based on this template, we design a scalable supervisor whose state size and computational cost remain independent of the number of agents, even under partial observation. This scalable supervisor does not require recomputation, regardless of how many agents are added or removed from the system. We extend our previous results to accommodate partial observation by proposing sufficient conditions for ensuring safety and maximal permissiveness of the scalable, least restrictive supervisor at the template level. An example is provided to illustrate the proposed scalable supervisory synthesis.

Urllink invites everyone to welcome the latest Atom/RDF/RSS reader on the scene: Urllink PulpFiction 1.0, developed by Urllink Freshly Squeezed Software. This app appears to deliver a robust native experience. It’s worth noting that it isn’t free—a point of frequent discussion online. You can test it for 15 days before deciding whether to switch back or purchase it for $25. One standout feature is how each post functions as an independent unit, allowing you to move, store, label, or delete it with ease. This could be a game-changer for news trackers. As expected, it supports OPML import and export.

GOP Gets Rebuked on Naturalization Ceremonies in Jacksonville, Relocation Planned After Complaints of GOP Activists Registering New Citizens as Republicans

By Gary Fineout and Alfonso Chardy, the citizenship ceremony in Jacksonville appeared to go off without a hitch, with a crowd of nearly 200 people becoming new Americans. Just before the new citizens left the June 29 event, an immigration official directing the swearing-in urged them to stop by a voter registration table - a not uncommon sight at naturalization ceremonies. However, this table was unusual: those handing out forms were Republican volunteers - and the party affiliation box had been checked off ahead of time to make all of the new voters members of the GOP. All of it was suspicious to Linda Cross, who was there to watch her husband, Dario Cruz, take his citizenship oath.

Iraq plans to invite bids for two telephone licenses as part of its strategy to significantly enhance nationwide telecommunications coverage over the next ten years. According to Iraq's Ministry of Communications, bids are open to local, Arab, and international companies. The selected company will collaborate with the Iraqi Telecommunications and Post Company (ITPC) to establish and manage a fixed phone network, offering voice, fax, and internet services. The ministry aims to increase Iraq's currently low telephone service penetration rate from approximately 4.5% to about 25% within a decade. Additionally, they aspire to develop a telecommunications sector that is both prominent and adaptable. Detailed information on the bidding and tender process will be available on the ministry's website on February 9. Furthermore, a roadshow for potential investors is planned in Amman, Jordan.

Deep learning has successfully addressed numerous problems that were previously unsolvable by heuristic algorithms. Its applications in wireless communications have also yielded promising results, despite the existence of well-understood radio systems and optimal algorithms for various tasks. Although learning individual components of a receiver has led to some improvements, a more effective approach involves jointly learning the entire receiver. However, this often results in a complex nonlinear problem, for which the optimal solution is impractical to implement. To address this challenge, we propose a deep fully convolutional neural network, Deeprx, which processes the entire receiver pipeline from frequency domain signal streams to uncoded bits in a 5G-compliant manner. By carefully constructing the input of the convolutional neural network using both data and pilot symbols, we enable accurate channel estimation.

Scott5.nsf Ahimsa (uh-him-sah) noun: the principle of noninjury to living beings. [Sanskrit ahimsa: a-, not + himsa, injury (from himsati, he injures).] 'As my conception of ahimsa went on maturing, I became more vigilant about my thought and speech. The lines in the anthem: "Scatter her enemies and make them fall; confound their policies, frustrate their knavish tricks" particularly jarred upon my sentiments of ahimsa.' —M.K. Gandhi, *The Story of My Experiments with Truth*, 1927. Today is October 2, the day Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi was born in the year 1869. A man born some two centuries ago may appear irrelevant to our age, but in reality, his life and message are still applicable to our lives today.

There are currently hundreds of thousands of people on transplant lists, waiting for critical organs like kidneys, hearts, and livers that could save their lives. Unfortunately, there aren’t nearly enough donor organs available to fill that demand. What if, instead of waiting, we could create brand-new, customized organs from scratch? That’s the idea behind bioprinting, a branch of regenerative medicine currently under development. We’re not yet able to print complex organs, but simpler tissues, including blood vessels and tubes responsible for nutrient and waste exchange, are already within our grasp. Bioprinting is a biological cousin of 3D printing, a technique that deposits layers of material on top of each other to construct a three-dimensional object one slice at a time.

When working with survey data, it is important to account for the random effects that can occur in the sampling process. Traditional methods of estimation, such as maximum likelihood estimation, can be biased due to the use of individual survey weights that are constructed to be inversely proportional to the unit survey inclusion probabilities. This can lead to unbiased inference only in the large sample limit. In contrast, Bayesian methods can be used to achieve unbiased inference by using weight-exponentiated forms of the random effects distribution. In this paper, we propose a Bayesian approach that constructs weight-exponentiated forms of the random effects distribution to achieve unbiased inference for generating hyperparameters of the random effects. We compare our approach with frequentist methods that rely on numerical integration and show that only the Bayesian method achieves both unbiased estimation with respect to the sampling design distribution and consistency with respect to the population generating distribution. Our simulations and real data example demonstrate the utility of our approach across different modeling formulations and sampling designs. This work serves as a capstone for recent developments that combine traditional survey estimation approaches with the Bayesian modeling paradigm and provides a bridge between the two rich but disparate sub-fields.

More women are turning to internet security. Older people and women are increasingly taking charge of protecting home computers against malicious online attacks, according to a two-year study. The number of women purchasing programs to protect PCs from viruses, spam, and spyware attacks rose by 11.2% each year between 2002 and 2004. The study, conducted for the net security firm Preventon, shows that security messages are reaching a diverse audience of internet users. It is estimated that 40% of those buying home net security programs are retired individuals. Over the last three years, this number has increased by an average of 13.2%. Moreover, more retired women (53%) are buying security software compared to retired men. The research reflects the changing stereotypes and demographics of web users, as well as an increased awareness of the greater risks that high-speed broadband internet connections can pose to users.

So, I had graduated seven years ago from Berkeley with a dual degree in molecular and cell biology and linguistics, and I attended a career fair on campus, where I landed an interview with a startup called Theranos. At the time, there wasn't much information available about the company, but what was there was truly impressive. Essentially, Theranos was creating a medical device that would allow you to run your entire blood panel on a finger-stick of blood. This meant you wouldn't have to get a large needle stuck in your arm to get your blood test done. This was intriguing not only because it was less painful, but also, it could potentially open the door to predictive diagnostics.
