Pros: +graphics Cons: -bugs (especially in the environment, where you might get stuck, or your shots may hit an invisible wall despite having a clear line of sight to the enemy) -the game is handholding/babysitting you -dumb AI -enemies always seem to know where you are when you're spotted -godlike accuracy by the enemy, which is frustrating like in the first Ghost Warrior -terrible checkpoints and saves -boring story that I couldn't care less about. I will not recommend it unless it's 90% off; even then, I find playing this game to be an extremely boring chore, just playing for the sake of finishing it to move on to the next game in my library. Considering the time I wasted on the first Ghost Warrior (which was equally boring and I felt liberated when I finished it), I probably won't play it until the end.

Anti-spam laws bite spammer hard. The net's self-declared spam king is seeking bankruptcy protection. Scott Richter, the man behind optinrealbig.com and billions of junk mail messages, said lawsuits had forced the company into Chapter 11. Optinrealbig was fighting several legal battles, most notably against Microsoft, which is pushing for millions of dollars in damages. The company said filing for Chapter 11 would help it try to resolve its legal problems but still keep trading. Listed as the third biggest spammer in the world by junk mail watchdog Spamhaus, Optinrealbig was sued in December 2003 for sending mail messages that violated anti-spam laws. The lawsuit was brought by Microsoft and New York Attorney General Eliot Spitzer, who alleged that Mr. Richter and his accomplices sent billions of spam messages through 514 compromised net addresses in 35 countries.

While not perfect, this game has been a thoroughly enjoyable experience. However, the save system and the possibility of sudden death can lead to some frustration, particularly during the early stages of the game. I experienced this firsthand when I lost my main character around the 10th hour of my 20-hour playthrough. A save feature to reload at that point would have been greatly appreciated. Fortunately, I've since discovered a third-party mod/save tool to mitigate the risk of catastrophic failures. Additionally, the aiming reticle could be slightly larger and the zoom function could be more powerful. Despite these minor drawbacks, I've thoroughly enjoyed my time with the game. As someone who doesn't typically play zombie-themed games, I've found the combination of strategy, missions, and first-person shooter/beat-em-up elements to be particularly engaging. I've found myself playing it as often as I can get away, often getting sucked into just one more mission or supply run before reluctantly shutting the game down.

Ireland surged past Scotland as they maintained their Six Nations Grand Slam ambitions with an impressive victory at Murrayfield. Hugo Southwell's try gave the Scots an early 8-0 lead, but scores from locks Malcolm O'Kelly and Paul O'Connell put the visitors in command by half-time. A third try from wing Denis Hickie and a third penalty from Ronan O'Gara, who kicked 13 points, extended the lead. Jon Petrie scored a second try for Scotland, but late scores from John Hayes and Gavin Duffy sealed the victory. After two hard-earned away victories, Eddie O'Sullivan's side can now look forward to welcoming England to Lansdowne Road in a fortnight. Scotland will try to give their coach Matt Williams a first Six Nations victory when Italy come to Edinburgh, but they again struggled to turn pressure into points.

China's Beijing-based Three Gorges Corporation, the builder of the massive Three Gorges Dam, is defying a government order to halt construction of one of its massive projects, the Xiluodu Dam, according to the Chinese State Press. The company is instead choosing to pay a fine for non-compliance, despite being ordered to suspend work on the dam, which is one of 30 large-scale construction projects halted due to a lack of proper environmental assessments. Additionally, the company has ignored orders to cease construction at two other projects, the Three Gorges Underground Power Plant and the Three Gorges Project Electrical Power Supply Plant. As of now, only 22 of the 30 projects targeted by China's State Environmental Protection Agency (SEPA) for failing to conduct mandatory environmental impact assessments have complied with the shutdown order, while the remaining eight projects, including the Xiluodu Dam, continue to operate without proper environmental checks.

Embracing the notion of flag-burning as a legitimate offense presents an intriguing paradox - one that could have potentially served as a safeguard against the brutal persecution of alleged witches. As a non-American blogger, I have been hesitant to engage in discussions surrounding this topic, particularly those centered on national emblems. However, having initiated this conversation, I feel compelled to share my thoughts. In my opinion, the idea of enacting legislation to prohibit flag-burning is utterly misguided. The moment a symbol is afforded automatic reverence and protection, a subtle yet significant step is taken towards the erosion of democratic values and the gradual slide into totalitarianism. Symbols, by their very nature, are devoid of inherent meaning; their significance is derived from the perceptions and interpretations imposed upon them by society.

Density-based clustering relies on the idea of linking groups to specific features of the probability distribution underlying the data. The reference to a true, yet unknown, population structure allows framing the clustering problem in a standard inferential setting, where the concept of ideal population clustering is defined as the partition induced by the true density function. The nonparametric formulation of this approach, known as modal clustering, draws a correspondence between the groups and the domains of attraction of the density modes. Operationally, a nonparametric density estimate is required, and a proper selection of the amount of smoothing—governing the shape of the density and hence possibly the modal structure—is crucial to identify the final partition. In this work, we address the issue of density estimation for modal clustering from an asymptotic perspective.

Downloads enter the US singles chart as digital music downloads are incorporated into the main US singles chart for the first time. Billboard's revamped Hot 100 chart now combines data from sales of music downloads, previously tracked on a separate download chart, with physical singles sales and airplay information. Green Day's 'Boulevard of Broken Dreams' currently holds the number two spot on Billboard's pop chart and tops the digital chart. The UK singles chart is also set to include download sales later this year. In the US, digital sales are already used to compile Billboard's Hot Digital Sales chart, and will now be merged with physical singles sales and airplay data to form the new Hot 100 chart. Additionally, Billboard's new Pop 100 chart combines airplay, digital, and physical sales, but limits its airplay information to US radio stations that play chart music.

We introduce a novel mixed-variable symplectic (MVS) integrator designed for planetary systems, capable of fully resolving close encounters. This method employs a time regularization technique that preserves the stability properties of symplectic integrators while dynamically reducing the effective step size during planetary close approaches. By utilizing a high-order MVS scheme, the algorithm maintains efficiency with large time steps in regions devoid of close encounters. We demonstrate that this approach can accurately resolve near-collisions (with separations as small as a fraction of a planetary radius) while employing the same time-step used for weakly perturbed systems, such as the Solar System. The long-term stability of the method is validated through simulations of six super-Earths undergoing strong gravitational scattering over 50 kyr. Comparisons with hybrid methods like Mercury reveal superior energy conservation at comparable computational cost.

A recent two-year study reveals a significant shift in internet security trends, with older adults and women increasingly taking proactive measures to safeguard their home computers against cyber threats. Research conducted for cybersecurity firm Preventon indicates that female consumers purchasing antivirus, anti-spam, and anti-spyware solutions showed an annual growth rate of 11.2% from 2002 to 2004.

The findings demonstrate that cybersecurity awareness campaigns are effectively reaching diverse online populations. Notably, retirees now account for 40% of home security software purchases, with this demographic growing at an average annual rate of 13.2% over the past three years. Among senior citizens, women lead in cybersecurity adoption, comprising 53% of purchases compared to their male counterparts.

This research highlights evolving user demographics and challenges traditional stereotypes about internet usage patterns. It also underscores the heightened security risks associated with high-speed broadband connections, which appear to be driving greater security consciousness across all age groups and genders.

So it's not really a holiday, but I've been accumulating days off and decided to cash one in today, Wednesday the 10th in Japan (the post time gives the US date, so it's technically not correct). So I can just take a couple of days to daydream and recharge my creative energy. It's a nice change of pace for a foreigner like me, and my timing was good too. The illustration part is the cherry on top. I'd probably complain more if it didn't fall into my lap. I'm aware that I'm getting a raw deal, but I'm not blind to that fact. I'm feeling a bit peckish, might head to Speak Easy for some American-style Mexican food across the street from the post office in my little prefecture.

Yes, this is a nostalgia trip for thirty-somethings who remember watching the ever-irritating Timmy Mallet in the UK, just for the five-minute Transformers cartoon. The voice actors for Optimus, Megatron, Bumblebee, and many others are back, and instantly recognisable. The distinctive cartoon transformation sound is also present. Graphically, the cell-shaded presentation is perfect and very faithful to Generation One. But aside from that, it's actually a very enjoyable game. The combat mechanics are extremely well done, with transforming mid-fight often being a necessity, seamlessly woven into combos, making it quite distinct from High Moon's excellent War for/Fall of Cybertron games. Another distinction is the heavy focus on melee combat, although some enemies, such as Seekers, require a switch to a ranged blaster.

$context$. The assembly history experienced by the Milky Way is currently being unveiled thanks to the data provided by the $Gaia$ mission. It is likely that the globular cluster system of our galaxy has followed a similarly intricate formation path. $aims$. To constrain this formation path, we explore the link between the globular clusters and the known merging events that the Milky Way has experienced. $methods$. To this end, we combined the kinematic information provided by $Gaia$ for almost all Galactic clusters, with the largest sample of cluster ages available after carefully correcting for systematic errors. To identify clusters with a common origin, we analyzed their dynamical properties, particularly in the space of integrals of motion. $results$. We find that about 40% of the clusters likely formed in situ.

Manchester United narrowed Chelsea's Premier League advantage to nine points with a hard-fought 2-0 victory over Manchester City at the Etihad Stadium. The breakthrough came when Wayne Rooney cleverly redirected Gary Neville's cross at the near post, with the ball taking a decisive deflection off Richard Dunne to beat David James. The City defender's misfortune continued seven minutes later as he inadvertently volleyed the ball over his own goalkeeper's head for United's second. 

Despite dominating possession, City squandered their best opportunity when Steve McManaman fired wide from point-blank range in the first half. United initially struggled to impose themselves, displaying more effort than finesse in their build-up play during the opening period. Paul Scholes' wayward header represented their only genuine chance before the interval in what appeared to be an unconvincing performance from Sir Alex Ferguson's side. However, their second-half resurgence ultimately secured three crucial points in their pursuit of league leaders Chelsea.

Last night, I completed this game and must say, the storytelling is what truly sets it apart. Although the narrative is not particularly deep or engaging, the way it is presented is exceptional. The game's atmosphere is richly detailed, with a wealth of collectibles that do an excellent job of fleshing out the world of Batman. I learned a great deal about the Caped Crusader's universe, which was a nice surprise. However, I was disappointed that only a few bosses were included in the game. Nonetheless, there is always the opportunity to improve upon this in future titles. The combat is well-balanced and challenging, but it is also on the easier side. Leveling up Batman himself was not particularly satisfying, but the gadgets and abilities you acquire during the campaign add new layers of gameplay. Exploring previously inaccessible areas was a nice sense of progression.

Attitude: indifferent, looking forward to tonight.

Had a long, tiring day in the trenches today, so little time to write. However, it's great to see Tigger (now Akcron) taking the lead and discussing topics. Not much to add, really. Yesterday's Shakespeare play was lovely, but I had to leave before the end, which was disappointing. I hope to see the whole performance someday. Akcron is right about the shower situation - it's frustrating, and the looks people give when indulging in self-torture can be quite amusing.

Another week flew by me. I drove by the new house yesterday and the front window was covered in boxes. We may get the keys at closing, I am getting so excited. If everything goes well this week, we will be set for closing a week from Wednesday. I was up until after 3 00 am last night watching movies with Bethany. There has been a lot of movie watching going on around here lately. Here's my rundown  last night we watched thirteen, a movie every mother of a teenager should see. It was better than I expected. Then we watched Gigli, yes, it was just as bad as the media said it was, but we had to see for ourselves.

I am a cultural omnivore, reliant on my iPod for daily commutes filled with a diverse array of musical genres, from Wagner and Mozart to Christina Aguilera and Josh Turner, as well as country singer Kirk Franklin. I'm also an avid reader, indulging in works by Ian McEwan and Stephanie Meyer, and have devoured the Twilight tetralogy. In my free time, I enjoy immersing myself in my home theater, where I consume a wide range of DVDs, video on demand, and television shows, including "Law & Order: SVU," "30 Rock," and "Judge Judy." The people, cases, and rulings are all real, providing a compelling and entertaining experience.

A US woman sues over cartridges. A US woman is suing Hewlett Packard (HP), saying its printer ink cartridges are secretly programmed to expire on a certain date. The unnamed woman from Georgia says that a chip inside the cartridge tells the printer that it needs refilling, even when it does not. The lawsuit seeks to represent anyone in the US who has purchased an HP inkjet printer since February 2001. HP, the world's biggest printer firm, declined to comment on the lawsuit. HP ink cartridges use chip technology to sense when they are low on ink and advise the user to make a change. But the suit claims the chips also shut down the cartridges at a predetermined date, regardless of whether they are empty.

This was a good weekend. I did a lot of God's green leaf, after a long time, I listened to a lot of Nevermore (something I should have started doing much earlier) and read a lot. I finished three books over the weekend - Bruce Sterling's Heavy Weather, Larry Niven's World of Ptavvs, and Octavia Butler's Parable of the Sower. Heavy Weather was a good cyberpunk romp through a near-future USA, devastated by global warming and general anarchy. While it was clever, well-paced, and altogether very convincing, I must confess that I failed to come away with any really significant sensations about the story or the characters. Not that both aren't well-constructed and vividly etched - it's a good book, but not a great one, I guess.

Before the world of humans began, there was the world of the gods, made of fields, plains, and gardens. Four brothers wandered this celestial realm. They had no family other than each other—they didn’t even know who their parents were. One of the brothers, Deminan, looked different from the others. His skin was covered in painful scabs, and he wondered why he alone had been marked with this affliction. One day, while the supreme spirit Yaya was out in his gardens, Deminan and his brothers sneaked into Yaya’s house. After feasting and exploring, they spotted a giant gourd hanging in the corner. But as they tried to look inside the gourd, they dropped it.

Currently, a significant proportion of the global refugee population resides in urban areas, accounting for over 60% of the total number of refugees worldwide. This demographic shift presents a compelling case for a paradigm shift and innovative thinking. Rather than allocating resources towards constructing physical barriers, it would be more effective to invest in programs that empower refugees to become self-sufficient and contribute to the development of their host countries. As refugees, we often have to leave behind our material possessions, but not our skills and knowledge. If allowed to lead a productive life, refugees can not only help themselves but also contribute to the growth and prosperity of their host nations. My personal story is a testament to this potential. I was born in Bukavu, a city in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, as the fifth of 12 siblings in a family that has faced numerous challenges and overcome incredible odds.

Sid Meier's Civilization: Beyond Earth is a turn-based game where you build your civilization toward a specific goal or 'victory.' In previous games, you progressed through Earth's timeline, advancing through periods like the Stone Age, Medieval Age, and Modern Age. However, in this game, you are set in the future on a mission to colonize a new planet. What I enjoy about this game is that 2K took a fresh approach by introducing new mechanics like the affinity system and orbital layer. What I didn’t enjoy was that these new mechanics could sometimes feel overwhelming. One improvement I’d suggest is incorporating new artwork, such as the environment you see when communicating with another leader.

We consider the connection between two constructions of the mirror partner for the Calabi-Yau orbifold. This orbifold is defined as a quotient by a suitable subgroup $g$ of the phase symmetries of the hypersurface $x_m$ in the weighted projective space, cut out by a quasi-homogeneous polynomial $w_m$. The first, Berglund-H\"ubsch-Krawitz (BHK) construction, uses another weighted projective space and the quotient of a new hypersurface $x_{m^t}$ inside it by some dual group $g^t$. In the second, Batyrev construction, the mirror partner is constructed as a hypersurface in the toric variety defined by the reflexive polytope dual to the polytope associated with the original Calabi-Yau orbifold. We provide a simple evidence of the equivalence of these two constructions.

(pauses to breathe in, then out) So, I didn't always make my living from music. For about five years after graduating from an outstanding liberal arts university, this was my day job. (laughs) I was a self-employed living statue called the eight-foot bride, and I love telling people I did this for a job, because everybody always wants to know, 'Who are these freaks in real life?' (laughs) Hello. I painted myself white one day, stood on a box, put a hat or a can at my feet, and when someone came by and dropped in money, I handed them a flower – and some intense eye contact. And if they didn't take the flower, I threw in a gesture of sadness and longing – as they walked away.

I am a cultural omnivore, someone whose daily commute is enriched by an iPod filled with an eclectic mix—Wagner and Mozart, pop diva Christina Aguilera, country singer Josh Turner, gangsta rap artist Kirk Franklin, concerti, symphonies, and more. I'm a voracious reader, delving into works by Ian McEwan as well as Stephanie Meyer. I've read the Twilight tetralogy. I live for my home theater, where I consume DVDs, video on demand, and a variety of television. For me, shows like "Law & Order: SVU," Tina Fey's "30 Rock," and "Judge Judy"—"where the people are real, the cases are real, the rulings are final"—are essential viewing.

Weight pruning has been widely recognized as a straightforward and effective method for eliminating redundancy in deep neural networks (DNNs), thereby achieving acceleration on various platforms. However, most pruning techniques are essentially trade-offs between model accuracy and regularity, leading to impaired inference accuracy and limited on-device acceleration performance. To address this issue, we introduce a novel sparsity dimension, namely pattern-based sparsity, which combines pattern and connectivity sparsity, and proves to be highly accurate and hardware-friendly. With carefully designed patterns, our approach consistently achieves accuracy enhancement and better feature extraction ability on different DNN structures and datasets, and our pattern-aware pruning framework also enables pattern library extraction, pattern selection, pattern, and connectivity pruning and weight training simultaneously. Our approach naturally fits seamlessly into compiler optimization for highly efficient DNN execution on mobile platforms.

The article proposes a new protocol for sequential measurements of a quantum system, which is based on the principles of stochastic dynamics and zeno physics. The protocol aims to confine and protect quantum dynamics from decoherence in accordance with the Zeno physics, by means of repeated measurements and dynamical decoupling control pulses. The protocol consists of three main tasks:
 (i) analytically predict the probability that a quantum system is transferred into a target quantum state by means of stochastic sequential measurements. (ii) analytically predict the probability that a quantum system is transferred into a target quantum state by means of stochastic sequential measurements. (iii) optimally reconstruct the spectral density of environmental noise sources by orthogonalizing in the frequency domain the filter functions driving the designed quantum-sensor.

The proposed protocol has the potential to enhance our capability to observe and manipulate open quantum systems, thus bringing advances to quantum science and technologies. The protocol can be used to study the dynamics of open quantum systems, which are quantum systems interacting with their environments, and to develop new quantum technologies such as quantum sensors and quantum communication.

The article is written in a clear and concise manner, with a good balance between theoretical background and experimental perspectives. The authors provide a detailed explanation of the Zeno physics and its relevance to the protocol

Where does all the carbon we release go? Carbon works in a natural cycle, present in all living things: in the soil, in the oceans, in the atmosphere, and in rocks deep underground. When plants and other living things die, much of their stored carbon makes its way back to the atmosphere until new plants grow and reabsorb the carbon. But over millions of years, some of the carbon stored in ancient trees and sea life becomes rock, and some becomes fossil fuels: coal, oil, and natural gas. Natural events like volcanic eruptions release some of the carbon trapped in rock. And human activities, like burning fossil fuels, also release some of that prehistoric carbon. Today, humans release around 60 times more carbon than all the volcanoes on the planet each year.

Have you ever experienced that surreal moment of introspection, where your mind drifts from mundane reality to profound existential questions? Picture this: you're trapped in a monotonous math lecture, your consciousness detaching as you ponder the very mechanisms of thought that allow you to comprehend equations. In this strange epiphany, you become acutely aware of your own existence—here, now, in this classroom—and question the meaning behind it all. The sheer wonder of being alive washes over you... only to be undercut by the crushing realization that such philosophical musings during basic algebra are frankly ridiculous.  

And while we're discussing academic grievances, let's address the true atrocity: block scheduling. Whoever devised this torturous system clearly never endured the agony of a 150-minute lecture on any subject. It's nothing short of institutionalized cruelty.

Roxy Music on the Isle of Wight will appear at the Isle of Wight Music Festival, alongside Morrissey, Supergrass, and Idlewild. The festival takes place from June 10-12. Original band members Bryan Ferry, Phil Manzarek, Andy McKay, and Paul Thompson will take to the stage on the Saturday night of the festival. The band is also working on new material, their first since the 1982 album Avalon. Roxy Music was formed in 1971 by Ferry, McKay, and Brian Eno, with Manzarek joining the following year. Their first hit was 1972's 'Virginia Plain', with other hits including 'Street Life', 'Love is the Drug', and the 1981 John Lennon cover, 'Jealous Guy'.

You can tell by the hours I’ve put into this game that I truly enjoy it. This is everything I hoped a tactical shooter from Call of Duty would be. However, don’t buy this game expecting it to play like COD, Battlefield, or any other mainstream shooter. It stands in a league of its own and should be appreciated as such. I can’t count how many times I’ve heard new players complain about dying too quickly. Seriously—god forbid you get shot in the face and actually die. It’s not like that never happens in real life, right?  

Edit: Okay, so I’ve been playing this game for a while now and have invested quite a bit into it. It’s one of the best multiplayer experiences out there—perfect for when you’re not in the mood for CS:GO.

We demonstrate canonical decompositions for the evaluations of global Mackey functors on orthogonal, unitary, and symplectic groups. Specifically, the restriction maps connecting these groups in consecutive dimensions admit natural splittings as surjective homomorphisms. These structural results hold particular significance in equivariant stable homotopy theory, where the homotopy groups of any global spectrum naturally constitute a global Mackey functor. Consequently, our decompositions induce a fragmentation of certain long exact sequences into more tractable short exact sequences. When applied to the real and complex global Thom spectra $\mathbf{mo}$ and $\mathbf{mu}$, these splittings further reveal the regularity properties of Euler classes associated with the tautological representations of the orthogonal group $O(n)$ and unitary group $U(n)$.

The Conservative Party has unveiled a groundbreaking proposal to revolutionize local policing, empowering communities to take a more active role in shaping law enforcement. Under this innovative plan, residents would be given the opportunity to elect their own area police commissioner, thereby fostering a more direct connection between the public and the policing authorities. This new role is designed to replace the existing, often invisible, police authorities, and would operate in tandem with the chief constable, rather than superseding their position.

However, the proposal has sparked intense debate, with the Liberal Democrats warning that it could potentially allow extremist groups to exert undue influence over policing. The Labour Party has also voiced concerns, criticizing the Conservative Party's "extravagant" promises on policing as unrealistic and lacking in substance.

Jan Berry, Chairman of the Police Federation of England and Wales, which represents the interests of rank-and-file officers, has expressed reservations about the plan, emphasizing the importance of maintaining operational independence. She cautioned that the proposed reforms could lead to policing becoming a "political football," with those holding extreme views dictating policy on the ground. Berry's concerns highlight the need for a balanced approach, one that prioritizes the needs of the community while safeguarding the integrity of the policing service.

Palms sweaty, heart racing, stomach in knots. You can't cry for help. Not only is your throat too tight to breathe, but it'd be so embarrassing. No, you aren't being stalked by a monster, you're speaking in public, a fate some deem worse than death. See, when you're dead, you feel nothing; at a podium, you feel stage fright. But at some point, we've all had to communicate in front of people, so you have to try and overcome it. To start, understand what stage fright is. Humans, social animals that we are, are wired to worry about reputation. Public speaking can threaten it. Before a speech, you fret, "What if people think I'm awful and I'm an idiot?

I must start by mentioning that I didn't buy the game "EU" when it was initially released. Instead, I got it during a sale that also included "EW," so I didn't encounter the same issues some players reported with the game. I really enjoyed it; the game was challenging in the beginning but very rewarding once you mastered it. As for "XCOM 2," I absolutely love it. The game runs smoothly for me on medium settings, and I'm using a GTX 560 with 2GB of VRAM. Initially, the game defaulted to high settings, and it ran a bit sluggishly—not enough to be unplayable, but noticeable. When I switched to medium settings, everything ran smoothly. I might just be fortunate with my gaming experiences, but it seems to me that a lot of the negative reviews are coming from people with unrealistic expectations.

"Hapoel Bnei Sakhnin, a trailblazing Israeli club, is set to make history by becoming the first Arab side to compete in a European competition, as they prepare to face English Premier League giants Newcastle United in the UEFA Cup. The team's impressive roster, which includes Zimbabwean goalkeeper Energy Murambadoro, is bolstered by his exceptional performance in the African Nations Cup finals in Tunisia, where he showcased his exceptional skills. After a brief stint with South African club Hellas, Murambadoro joined Hapoel Bnei Sakhnin, who recently lifted the Israeli Cup title and are based in the coastal city of Sakhnin, near Haifa. The club is renowned for its strong commitment to promoting peace and cooperation within Israel, making their entry into European competition a significant milestone in the country's football history.

I’m certain my sisters are completely intoxicated by now. Today was Pride in San Diego, and knowing them, when there’s an opportunity to drink, they take full advantage. That’s one trait I didn’t inherit from the Buenviajes—their enthusiasm for alcohol. While I occasionally don’t mind a drink, those moments are exceedingly rare.  

We now have just one month left before Ed departs, and the reality of his leaving is beginning to settle in for him—and for all of us. Personally, I came to terms with it long ago. My only hope is that he’ll still be here for Bailey’s surgery. We’ve been closely monitoring the swelling, and there’s no denying it’s become quite significant.

Warframe is an enjoyable game during the first few hours of play. However, after those initial hours, you may find that the game primarily offers repetition in its combat, enemies, and gameplay. A major highlight of the game is that the developers are committed and frequently update it, often introducing significant content changes, new warframes, and various tweaks/features. It's a good game for casual play, though I doubt it will hold your attention beyond a few months unless you particularly enjoy grinding. The positive aspect is that the game is free, so I recommend giving it a try. If you're looking for something quick and engaging, it's worth checking out.

Last night was the worst. I threw away the sweater I was knitting. I only had about 14 inches. I lost probably $10 worth of yarn (it's $8 a skein). But it had too many stitches by the time I got to the arm holes and the mohair was knotting itself together as I knitted, so frogging (ripping out rows) didn't work well. The yarn broke a lot. I didn't even swear, which is unusual for me. I just morosely ripped and listened to season two of Sex and the City (way to go, library!). And then I got sick of it and threw it away. I suppose I could say I learned a lot while trying to do this sweater.

Undertale comes together to create something greater than the sum of its parts. The graphics are solid for a retro title, and the music is nice, but paired together, they create some truly beautiful and impactful moments. The script runs the gamut from comedic to dramatic to heartbreaking to hopeful, sometimes all at once, and it does so while turning RPG tropes on their head. And all of this without even mentioning the world, characters, story, or gameplay... I could write whole pages about it. But instead, I’ll just say this: if you like RPGs, play this game. If you like video games, play this game. If you have seven free hours between now and your death, play this game.

A groundbreaking report from University College London reveals that slashing the drink-drive limit by a third could save a staggering 65 lives annually, far exceeding initial estimates. The proposed reduction from 80mg of alcohol per 100ml of blood to 50mg would not only save lives but also lead to a significant decrease in serious injuries on the UK's roads. According to the research conducted by transport expert Professor Richard Allsop, the move would result in 230 fewer serious injuries compared to 2003. This reduction in road accidents would have a substantial economic impact, saving the country an estimated £119 million annually. A previous estimate, contained in a 1998 Department of Transport document, had previously suggested that such a move could save 50 lives a year, highlighting the significance of this new research. As MPs prepare to vote on the government's road safety bill, which includes changes to speeding fines but not the drink-drive limit, this study serves as a timely reminder of the importance of evidence-based policy-making.

Summer television used to be a wasteland of reruns, but now two fantastic new shows have caught my attention, both of which I've been following religiously over the past week. One of them, "Blow Out," boasts the most flamboyant concept, yet oddly features no openly gay individuals. The show follows hairstylist Jonathan Antin as he ambitiously plans to open a second branch of his wildly successful West Hollywood salon in the upscale area of Beverly Hills. Typically, such a venture wouldn't capture my interest, yet as I was browsing through channels, I stumbled across Jonathan—exuding equal parts narcissism and blandness—as he gazed at a vacant space on Rodeo Drive and declared with grandiosity, "I am going to build the greatest salon in the history of the world right here!" And at that moment, I was hooked. Not since the days of Rocco have I found such a captivatingly foolish persona on television.

We introduce natural decompositions for the values of global Mackey functors at orthogonal, unitary, and symplectic groups. Specifically, the restriction homomorphisms linking the orthogonal, unitary, and symplectic groups across successive dimensions are inherently split epimorphisms. This splitting is particularly relevant in the context of equivariant stable homotopy theory. In this framework, the equivariant stable homotopy groups of any global spectrum organize into a global Mackey functor, making the splittings instrumental in breaking down certain long exact homotopy group sequences into shorter exact sequences. In the case of the real and complex global Thom spectra, denoted as $\mathbf{mo}$ and $\mathbf{mu}$, these splittings affirm the consistency of various Euler classes associated with the tautological representations of $o(n)$ and $u(n)$.

Two twin domes, two radically contrasting design philosophies. One is constructed from countless steel components, the other woven from a single silk thread. One is synthetic, the other natural. One imposes itself upon the environment, the other emerges from it. One is designed for nature, the other is nature’s own design.  

Michelangelo once remarked that within raw marble, he glimpsed a figure yearning to break free. His chisel was his sole instrument. Yet living beings are not carved—they evolve. Within our most fundamental units of life, our cells, lies the complete blueprint for every function and replication.  

Tools, too, leave their mark. Since the dawn of the Industrial Revolution, the realm of design has been shaped by the demands of manufacturing and mass production.

The weak gravity conjecture (WGC) posits that superextremal particles must exist in any consistent quantum theory of gravity. The prevailing belief is that these particles are necessary to ensure that extremal black holes are either unstable or marginally stable. However, the implications if this is not the case remain unclear. This note demonstrates that, in a generic Einstein quantum theory of gravity in Anti-de Sitter (AdS) space, the exact stability of extremal black branes conflicts with rigorously proven quantum information theorems concerning entanglement entropy. To resolve this contradiction, a nonperturbative version of the WGC emerges, which aligns with the conventional statement at weak coupling. The argument is broad and does not depend on supersymmetry or any specific ultraviolet completion, relying solely on the validity of Einsteinian gravity, effective field theory, and holography.

Deep hashing methods have demonstrated remarkable retrieval accuracy and efficiency in large-scale image retrieval. Optimizing discrete hash bits is the primary focus in deep hashing methods. A common approach in these methods is to adopt an activation function, such as the sigmoid or tanh function, and minimize a quantization loss to approximate discrete values. However, this paradigm may result in more and more hash bits being stuck in the saturated area of the activation functions and never escaping. We refer to this problem as the "dead bits problem." Moreover, the existing quantization loss will exacerbate this issue. In this paper, we propose a simple yet effective gradient amplifier that acts before activation functions to alleviate this problem, thereby alleviating the "dead bits problem.

Ordinary differential equations (ODEs) are mathematical models used in many application areas such as climatology, bioinformatics, and chemical engineering due to their intuitive appeal for modeling. Despite the wide usage of ODEs in modeling, the frequent absence of analytic solutions makes it difficult to estimate ODE parameters from data, especially when the model has many variables and parameters. This paper proposes a Bayesian ODE parameter estimation algorithm that is fast and accurate even for models with numerous parameters. The proposed method approximates an ODE model with a state-space model based on equations of a numeric solver. It allows fast estimation by avoiding computations of a whole numerical solution in the likelihood. The posterior is obtained using a variational Bayes method, more specifically, the approximate Riemannian conjugate gradient method (Honkela et al.).

Very late last night, I finished this. The story is not very deep or interesting, but what makes it stand out is the storytelling. The atmosphere is filled to the brim, and the many collectibles do a great job at fleshing it out. I did learn a lot about the world of Batman, so it was a little disappointing that so few bosses were in the game. Oh well, there are enough future games to rectify that. The combat is the right amount of challenge, but it's more on the easy side. Leveling up Batman wasn't very satisfying. It's the gadgets you upgrade and acquire during the campaign that add nice new gameplay mechanics. Getting access to previously unreachable places makes for a nice sense of progression.

A poignant documentary offering a deeply personal glimpse into the life of the late music icon George Michael is set to premiere at the prestigious Berlin Film Festival. Titled 'A Different Story', the film will be showcased in the festival's Panorama section, running from 10-20 February. This intimate portrait delves into the highs and lows of Michael's remarkable career, from his early days as a member of the chart-topping duo Wham! to his later years as a solo artist, marked by both creative triumphs and personal struggles. The film features Michael himself, sharing candid insights into his life, including his high-profile battles with the music industry, his outspoken stance against the Iraq War, and his critiques of American politics. To mark the occasion, George Michael will attend the festival on 16 February to introduce the screening, joined by the film's director, Southan Morris, and executive producer, Andy Stephens. Clocking in at 93 minutes, 'A Different Story' promises to be a powerful and moving tribute to the enduring legacy of a music legend.

Hands down my favorite first-person shooter of all time. Run-and-gun rambos need not apply. Recoil and ballistics are realistic. Single hits to the chest, neck, or head from rifle caliber weapons almost always result in death, unless it just grazes you. Rounds drop over distance and short, controlled bursts are required if you wish to remain on target. There is no magical ammunition pool. If you reload halfway through your magazine, all the time, you will have a pouch full of half-full mags. You can't run forever. If you sprint for a while, your aim will suffer as you gasp heavily until you have caught your breath. Rest your weapon on your elbows while prone or on a window sill to steady your aim. Accuracy suffers when standing.

When I look in the mirror today, I see a justice and education scholar at Columbia University, a youth mentor, an activist, and a future New York State senator. (Cheering) I see all of that and a man who spent a quarter of his life in state prison — six years, to be exact, starting as a teenager on Rikers Island for an act that nearly cost a man his life. Allan Scott has made a promising start to his international career, finishing fourth in the 60m hurdles at the prestigious Jose M Cagigal Memorial Meeting in Madrid. The 22-year-old Scottish athlete, who hails from East Kilbride, is now setting his sights on a medal at the upcoming European Indoor Championships. Despite a slight dip in performance in the final, where he clocked 7.68 seconds after winning his heat in 7.64 seconds, Scott remains optimistic about his chances. "The experience was invaluable, and I'm definitely not ruling out a medal challenge next week," he said. The event was won by Spain's Felipe Vivancos, who equalled the national record, with Sweden's Robert Kronberg and Haiti's Dudley Dorival securing second and third place respectively.

Following the suggestion from the Monte–Carlo experiments in Jiménez (J. of Turbul. 2020), that dipoles are as important to the dynamics of decaying two-dimensional turbulence as individual vortex cores, it is found that the kinetic energy of this flow is carried by elongated streams formed by the concatenation of dipoles. Vortices separate into a family of small fast-moving cores, and another family of larger slowly moving ones, which can be described as 'frozen' into a slowly evolving 'crystal'. The kinematics of both families are very different, and only the former is self-similar. The latter is responsible for most of the kinetic energy of the flow, and its vortices form the dipoles and the streams. Mechanisms are discussed for the growth of this slow component.

Metal ion implantation into ceramics has proven to be an effective and controllable method for modifying the surface electrical conductivity of ceramic materials, finding utility in various applications. Notably, this technique provides a way to manage voltage distribution across high-voltage insulators, thereby enhancing the maximum operational voltage that can be sustained without triggering surface flashover. However, a potential concern is the long-term stability of the enhanced conductivity from the implantation, particularly if the implanted metal is prone to oxidation. In this study, we explore the long-term conductivity behavior of titanium-implanted alumina. Our findings show that although there is an initial reduction in conductivity of up to 40% within the first few weeks post-implantation, this is followed by a very gradual decline of approximately 10% over the subsequent year.

Voters flock to blog awards site; voting is underway for the annual Bloggies, which recognize the best web blogs - online spaces where people publish their thoughts - of the year. Nominations were announced on Sunday, but traffic to the official site was so heavy that the website was temporarily closed due to an overwhelming number of visitors. Weblogs have been nominated in 30 categories, ranging from the top regional blog to the best-kept-secret blog. Blogs had a huge year, with a top US dictionary naming "blog" the word of 2004. Technorati, a blog search engine, tracks about six million blogs and reports that more than 12,000 are added daily. A new blog is created every 5.8 seconds, according to US research think-tank Pew Internet and American Life, but less than 40% of the total are updated at least once every two months.

In order to analyze spontaneous conversations in real-world settings, such as those found in child-centered recordings, we have discovered that these types of audio files are amongst the most challenging to process. However, developing speech processing models capable of handling such a wide range of conditions would be particularly valuable for language acquisition studies, where researchers are interested in the quantity and quality of the speech that children hear and produce, as well as for early diagnosis and measuring the effects of remediation. In this paper, we present our approach to designing an open-source neural network to classify audio segments into vocalizations produced by the child wearing the recording device, vocalizations produced by other children, adult male speech, and adult female speech. We have gathered diverse child-centered corpora, which totals 260 hours of recordings and encompasses 10 languages.

Novel concepts, perspectives, and challenges in measuring and controlling an open quantum system via sequential schemes are demonstrated. We discuss how similar protocols, relying both on repeated quantum measurements and dynamical decoupling control pulses, can enable us to  (i) confine and protect quantum dynamics from decoherence in accordance with the zeno physics. (ii) analytically predict the probability that a quantum system is transferred into a target quantum state by means of stochastic sequential measurements. (iii) optimally reconstruct the spectral density of environmental noise sources by orthogonalizing in the frequency domain the filter functions driving the designed quantum-sensor. The achievement of these tasks will enhance our capability to observe and manipulate open quantum systems, thus bringing advances to quantum science and technologies.

So many things were done wrong. I don’t even know how to start this review. This all relates to my career. Electric network frequency (ENF) fluctuations serve as a powerful tool in multimedia forensics. An efficient approach for ENF estimation has been introduced, utilizing temporal windowing based on the filter-bank Capon spectral estimator. This method employs a type of Gohberg-Semencul factorization of the model covariance matrix, taking advantage of its Toeplitz structure. Notably, this approach is the first in the field of ENF to use a temporal window, which does not have to be rectangular, prior to spectral estimation. Krylov matrices are used for the fast implementation of matrix inversions. The proposed approach surpasses current state-of-the-art methods in ENF estimation, particularly when a short time window of one second is used in power recordings. In speech recordings, this approach provides highly accurate results regarding both time complexity and accuracy.

Do I even need to explain why Portal is amazing? Portal is a puzzle game that introduces a concept and then steps aside. It's an amazing way to test your mind while still being fun. Although this game isn't as story-heavy, it did give birth to one of the most popular games of 2011. I still play this game and beat it when I'm feeling bored, and I love to come back and see what gave birth to a legend and what is a legend. Gameplay: Portal's gameplay is quite simple to explain, at least. You use portals to your advantage, along with buttons that activate a part of the puzzle. There are two types of buttons in the game: one that you press and one that requires a cube to be placed on it.

Iraq invites phone licence bids from local, Arab and foreign companies to significantly boost nationwide coverage over the next decade. The ministry of communications has invited bids for two telephone licences, with the goal of increasing Iraq's very low telephone service penetration rate from approximately 4.5% to around 25% within the next 10 years. The successful bidder will partner with the Iraqi telecommunications and post company (ITPC) to install and operate a fixed phone network, providing voice, fax, and internet services. The ministry aims to develop a highly visible and changeable telecommunications sector, with a road-show for investors scheduled in Amman, Jordan. The tender details will be published on the ministry's website on February 9. This move is expected to boost the country's telecommunications sector and enhance its global connectivity.

It's not particularly exciting, but I've been accumulating sub-days and decided to use one today, Wednesday the 10th in Japan (note that the post-time reflects the U.S. date, so it's technically not accurate), allowing me to take a couple of days off to unwind. There's nothing quite like 48 hours of daydreaming to recharge one's creative energy. As a foreigner, I have it relatively good, and my timing has been fortunate. The illustration work is the icing on the cake – I'd likely be more dissatisfied if that opportunity hadn't come my way. I'm aware that I'm not being fairly compensated, though. I'm getting hungry, so I might head to Speak Easy, the 'American-style' diner just across the street from the post office in my prefecture, to grab some Mexican food.

Saturday, we took Connor to Fireman's Park, thinking the fireman's Easter egg hunt was to start at 11 a.m. It turned out it was on Friday. Thank goodness I brought a bag of eggs with me just in case. So Connor had a great time finding eggs out in the park. Sunday morning, when he woke up, he was so excited that the Easter bunny had come. There's nothing better than seeing the excitement on your child's face. After egg hunting, we all went to church. Even though the Easter bunny is fun and all, we do make sure our kids know the true meaning of the holidays. This was Megan's first Easter, and she looked so cute in her jean dress and stockings.

This game is incredibly boring. I bought it thinking it was the PC version of 'Katamari Damacy,' a popular and enjoyable PS2 game. Unfortunately, it falls far short of that. In this game, you move around absorbing increasingly larger objects to grow, allowing you to absorb even bigger things. However, the gameplay isn't as smooth as it should be, the levels lack creativity, and what's present is more like a surreal horror show. I don't mean an entertaining horror show but one that leaves you feeling depressed and questioning if life is all it should be. Every 'living' creature in the game, from small pets to humans, makes me wonder if the creators had an interest in taxidermy.

At a glance: Tory health checks. The UK's opposition Conservatives have unveiled plans to introduce health checks for immigrants if they win the general election. Here's a guide to the plan: people coming to live and work in Britain from outside the EU. If they plan to stay six months or more and are from a country with high levels of TB, they would have to have a chest x-ray and further tests if appropriate. All people from outside the European Union who want to stay a year or more will have to undergo a full medical. Tuberculosis, hepatitis B, and HIV. A positive test for TB would automatically mean visa applications being turned down. All other conditions would be dealt with on a case-by-case basis.

This study presents a novel extension of existing human gait models, drawing inspiration from the works of Keller and Kockshenev. By integrating a metabolic energy minimization strategy with a non-linear description of the mass center's oscillatory motion, the hybrid model aims to accurately replicate the walk-run transition phenomenon. The analysis of experimental data across various speed regimes reveals that the trajectory perimeter of the mass center is a crucial parameter in quantifying this dynamic behavior. This research was conducted in collaboration with the biomedical engineering, basic sciences, and laboratory research groups at the Manuela Beltrán University in Bogotá, Colombia, utilizing a specially designed experimental protocol.

I believe this Assassin's Creed installment perfected the mechanics across all aspects—ship battles, sailing, fishing, parkour, and more. I also felt a deeper connection to the protagonist. The collectibles in this game felt more meaningful than in previous entries, perhaps because they served a greater purpose within the world. They weren’t mandatory, but they were enjoyable to pursue, offering well-balanced rewards.

For the past 24 years, I have had the privilege of serving as a firefighter in Huntington, West Virginia. As a woman leading a department in a traditionally male-dominated profession, I have had the unique opportunity to break down barriers and challenge the status quo. Ten years ago, I made the conscious decision to expand my skillset and pursued a nursing degree. This was a pivotal moment, as it became clear that the next significant threat facing not only my city, but also cities across the country, was not a one-and-done disaster, where a single heroic act would suffice. Rather, it was a complex, ongoing battle that required a multifaceted approach, where I could leverage my medical knowledge to make a meaningful difference and create a lasting impact.

When random effects are correlated with sample design variables, the usual approach of employing individual survey weights (constructed to be inversely proportional to the unit survey inclusion probabilities) to form a pseudo-likelihood no longer produces asymptotically unbiased inference. We construct a weight-exponentiated formulation for the random effects distribution that achieves unbiased inference for generating hyperparameters of the random effects. We contrast our approach with frequentist methods that rely on numerical integration to reveal that only the Bayesian method achieves both unbiased estimation with respect to the sampling design distribution and consistency with respect to the population generating distribution. Our simulations and real data example for a survey of business establishments demonstrate the utility of our approach across different modeling formulations and sampling designs. This work serves as a capstone for recent developmental efforts that combine traditional survey estimation approaches with the Bayesian modeling paradigm and provides a bridge across the two rich but disparate subfields.

Podcasting: The Rise of DIY Radio
Podcasting has become a popular medium for self-expression and creativity, allowing individuals to produce and distribute their own audio content without the need for traditional radio or television broadcasting. The term "podcasting" comes from the Apple iPod, but you don't need an iPod to create or listen to a podcast. Podcasting is essentially internet-based radio, where podcasters create and distribute their shows from their own homes, using a microphone, a computer, and some editing software.
Podcasting's rise has been led by Adam Curry, a former MTV host and VJ (video jockey), who is credited with coining the term "podcasting." Podcasting has become a way for people to share their thoughts, ideas, and passions with a global audience, without the need for traditional media outlets. With the rise of podcasting, people can now create and distribute their own content, giving them a platform to express themselves and reach a wide audience.

More than 2,000 business and political leaders from around the world are converging on the Swiss mountain resort of Davos for the annual World Economic Forum (WEF). For five days, they will engage in discussions on a range of pressing global issues, including China's economic prowess and Iraq's future after this Sunday's elections. Notable attendees include UK Prime Minister Tony Blair and South African President Thabo Mbeki, among more than 20 government leaders and heads of state. In a departure from previous years, the WEF is expected to be less contentious, as anti-globalization campaigners have called off a demonstration planned for the weekend. Meanwhile, the Brazilian city of Porto Alegre will host the rival World Social Forum, which will run concurrently with the WEF's more exclusive event in Davos.

Menstruation: A Natural Process Weighed Down by Stigma

Menstruation, a biological process that is essential for life, has been shrouded in secrecy and shame for centuries. Despite the fact that over 800 million women worldwide experience it every month, it remains a taboo topic in many cultures. As I speak to you now, women around the world are silently enduring the physical and emotional challenges of menstruation, hiding their sanitary products in brown paper bags and rushing to the bathroom to deal with the pain and discomfort in private.

The stigma surrounding menstruation has a profound impact on women's lives. It can make it difficult to discuss openly and honestly about their periods, leading to feelings of isolation and embarrassment. The shame and secrecy surrounding menstruation can also lead to a lack of understanding and support for women's reproductive health, perpetuating harmful attitudes and practices.

Davies Favors Gloucester Future

Wales hooker Mefin Davies is likely to remain with the English side Gloucester, despite reported interest from the Neath-Swansea Ospreys. BBC Wales understands that while the Ospreys are interested in the 32-year-old, Davies would prefer to stay where he is. A standout in Saturday's RBS Six Nations win over England, Davies is currently on a one-year contract at Kingsholm. However, the hooker has demonstrated his value to the Zurich Premiership side, and he is likely to secure a new deal next season. The dissolution of the Celtic Warriors region last summer left Davies in a difficult position, forcing him to accept a semi-professional contract with Neath RFC. Although he gained match time with the Ospreys at the behest of the Wales management, he expressed anger over how he was treated before moving to Gloucester.

The palms of your hands are sweaty, your heart is racing, and your stomach is in knots. You can't cry out for help, not only because your throat is too tight to breathe, but also because it would be an embarrassing ordeal. No, you're not being stalked by a monster; you're speaking in public, a fate that some people deem worse than death. When you're dead, you feel nothing; but at a podium, you feel stage fright. However, at some point, we've all had to communicate in front of people, so you have to try and overcome it. To start, understanding what stage fright is, it's essential. As humans, social animals that we are, we're wired to worry about our reputation. Public speaking can threaten it. Before a speech, you fret, 'What if people think I'm awful and I'm an idiot?' It's natural to feel this way, but it's crucial to acknowledge that it's a normal feeling, and with practice, you can learn to overcome it.

Baryonic feedback effects result in the suppression of the weak lensing angular power spectrum at small scales. The shape and amplitude of this suppression are poorly constrained, leading to significant uncertainties for upcoming cosmological weak lensing surveys like Euclid or LSST. This first paper in a two-part series uses simulations to create a Euclid-like tomographic mock dataset for the cosmic shear power spectrum and its covariance matrix, both corrected for baryonic effects using the baryonification method of Schneider et al. (2019). Additionally, we develop an emulator to quickly predict baryonic power suppression, enabling us to conduct a likelihood inference analysis for a standard ΛCDM cosmology with both cosmological and astrophysical parameters.

According to a two-year study, the number of women taking charge of protecting their home computers from malicious internet attacks is increasing, with a significant rise in the number of women purchasing programs to safeguard their PCs from virus, spam, and spyware attacks. The study, conducted by a net security firm, reveals that security messages are reaching a diverse range of users. Notably, 40% of those purchasing home internet security software are retired individuals, a figure that has increased by an average of 13.2% over the past three years. Interestingly, a higher percentage of retired women (53%) are buying security software compared to retired men. This shift reflects the evolving demographics of web users, as well as the growing awareness of the increased risks associated with high-speed broadband internet connections.

Ordinary differential equations (ODEs) are mathematical models used in many application areas such as climatology, bioinformatics, and chemical engineering due to their intuitive appeal for modeling. Despite ODEs' wide usage in modeling, the frequent absence of analytic solutions makes it difficult to estimate ODE parameters from data, especially when the model has many variables and parameters. This paper proposes a Bayesian ODE parameter estimation algorithm that is fast and accurate even for models with many parameters. The proposed method approximates an ODE model with a state-space model based on the equations of a numerical solver. It enables fast estimation by avoiding computations of a full numerical solution in the likelihood. The posterior is obtained by a variational Bayes method, more specifically, the approximate Riemannian conjugate gradient method (Honkela et al.).

On October 4, 1957, the world watched in awe and fear as the Soviet Union launched Sputnik, the world's first man-made satellite, into space. This little metal ball, smaller than two feet in diameter, launched a space race between the U.S. and U.S.S.R. that would last for eighteen years and change the world as we know it. Sputnik was not the first piece of human technology to enter space, however. That superlative goes to the V-2 rocket used by Germany in missile attacks against Allied cities as a last-ditch effort in the final years of World War II. Although it was not very effective, both the U.S. and U.S.S.R. captured the technology and scientists responsible for its development and began using them for their own projects.

Imagine a future where the desperate wait for a life-saving organ transplant becomes a thing of the past. Today, hundreds of thousands of individuals are languishing on transplant lists, their lives hanging precariously in the balance as they await the gift of a kidney, heart, or liver. The harsh reality is that the demand for donor organs far outstrips the available supply, leaving countless people to face an uncertain fate. But what if we could revolutionize this landscape by creating bespoke organs from scratch, tailored to the specific needs of each patient? This is the groundbreaking promise of bioprinting, a pioneering field within regenerative medicine that is rapidly gaining momentum. Although we are not yet at the stage where we can print intricate organs, significant strides have already been made in the creation of simpler tissues, including blood vessels and tubular structures that facilitate the exchange of vital nutrients and waste products. Bioprinting is, in essence, the biological equivalent of 3D printing, a cutting-edge technique that involves the layer-by-layer deposition of materials to craft complex, three-dimensional objects with unprecedented precision and accuracy.

The film features Michael reflecting on his career and personal life, spanning his early days in Wham! to more recent events. Michael will attend the festival to introduce the screening on 16 February, joined by director Southan Morris and executive producer Andy Stephens. The 93-minute documentary sees Michael discussing his Wham! days, his later career, including his high-profile legal battles with Sony, and his outspoken stance against the Iraq War and American politics.

The comedy "Sideways" dominated this year's Independent Spirit Awards, winning all six awards for which it was nominated. It was named Best Film, and Alexander Payne, along with writing partner Jim Taylor, won Best Director and Best Screenplay. Acting awards were also given to stars Paul Giamatti, Thomas Haden Church, and Virginia Madsen. "Sideways" is expected to perform well at Sunday's Oscars, where it has five nominations. The Spirit Awards, now in their 20th year, are presented to films made outside the traditional studio system and are traditionally held the day before the Oscars. Other winners included Catalina Sandino Moreno, who won Best Actress for her role as a drug smuggler in the Colombian drama "Maria Full of Grace." Moreno is also nominated for Best Actress at the Oscars. The Best First Screenplay Award went to Joshua Marston for "Maria Full of Grace.

Observations of thermally driven transverse vibrations in a photonic crystal waveguide (pcw) are reported. The pcw consists of two parallel nanobeams separated by a 240 nm vacuum gap. Models are developed and validated for the transduction of beam motion into phase and amplitude modulation of a weak optical probe propagating in a guided mode (gm) of the pcw, for probe frequencies both far from and near the dielectric band edge. Since our pcw is designed for near-field atom trapping, this research establishes a foundation for assessing potential detrimental effects of thermal motion on optical atomic traps near pcw surfaces. Longer-term goals include achieving strong atom-mediated coupling between individual phonons of vibration and single photons propagating in the pcw's gms, enabling quantum-level optomechanics with atoms, photons, and phonons.

Halloween writer Debra Hill dies. Screenwriter and producer Debra Hill, best known for her work on the 1970s horror classic Halloween, has died in Los Angeles at age 54. Hill, who had been battling cancer, co-wrote the 1978 film, which starred Jamie Lee Curtis as a babysitter terrorized by a psychopath. Directed by John Carpenter, it earned over $60 million (£31.3 million) worldwide—a record for independent films at the time. Hill also collaborated with Carpenter on Escape from New York and The Fog. Born in New Jersey, Hill began her career as a production assistant and worked her way up, becoming an assistant director and second-unit director before partnering with Carpenter. She was widely regarded as a pioneering woman in film, taking on roles in the 1970s that were more commonly held by men.

Spintronic nanodevices exhibit ultrafast nonlinear dynamic and recurrence behaviors on a nanosecond scale, promising to enable spintronic reservoir computing (RC) systems. Two physical RC systems, based on a single magnetic skyrmion memristor (MSM) and 24 spin-torque nano-oscillators (STNO), were proposed and modeled to perform image classification and nonlinear dynamic system prediction tasks. Our micromagnetic simulation results demonstrate that a single MSM can achieve outstanding performance in handwritten digit recognition, while a physical RC system consisting of 24 STNOs can effectively solve complex unknown nonlinear dynamic problems. These findings open up new avenues for the development of spintronic RC systems for various applications.

A woman in the United States has filed a lawsuit against Hewlett-Packard (HP), claiming that its printer ink cartridges are designed to expire on a set date. The plaintiff, a resident of Georgia, alleges that a chip within the cartridge signals the printer to request a refill even when it is not necessary. The legal action aims to include all U.S. consumers who have purchased an HP inkjet printer since February 2001. HP, the leading printer manufacturer globally, has declined to comment on the case. The company’s ink cartridges employ chip technology to detect low ink levels and inform users when a change is needed. However, the lawsuit asserts that these chips also deactivate the cartridges at a predetermined time, irrespective of their ink status.

Beyond Earth, the latest installment in the Sid Meier's Civilization series, is a turn-based game where you guide your civilization's evolution to achieve a specific objective or 'victory'. Unlike previous games in the series, which spanned the Earth's timeline through various eras such as the Stone Age, Medieval Age, and Modern Era, this game takes a bold leap into the future by setting you on a mission to colonize a new planet. I appreciate the innovative approach taken by the developers, which introduced fresh mechanics such as the Affinity System and Orbital Layer. However, I found that these new mechanics sometimes led to an overwhelming experience. To further enhance the game, I believe that incorporating updated artwork, particularly the environmental elements encountered during interactions with other leaders, could greatly improve the overall gameplay experience.

Hello. This is my first trip outside of Gaza and it's my first time leaving the city. I'm so happy to be here. (applause) My ambition has always been to be a pilot and fly a plane, to feel free to soar through the sky, to touch the sky. But that didn't happen. Unfortunately, I live in Gaza, and there is no airport. All borders are closed on every side. We live in one of the biggest prisons in the world. The only thing I can do is just look up to the sky. On some days, we are lucky if we have electricity for four or five hours. When it's cold, we make a fire on the front or on the roof of our homes.

**Time Running Out for Fiat-GM Negotiations as Key Deadline Looms**  

Fiat and General Motors (GM) face a critical deadline of February 1st at midnight to resolve their ongoing dispute regarding a potential acquisition. This cutoff date signifies the moment Fiat gains the contractual right to sell its automotive division to GM—a provision established under their 2000 partnership agreement.  

GM, grappling with financial losses in its European operations, has sought to avoid absorbing Fiat’s unprofitable automotive unit. Market sentiment shifted this week as reports of stalled negotiations drove Fiat’s stock down 1.2% on Tuesday, erasing part of Monday’s 4% surge fueled by hopes of a lucrative settlement.  

Sources indicate GM has proposed approximately $2 billion (£1.06 billion) to terminate the alliance. The automaker contends that Fiat’s divestment of its finance division and dilution of GM’s stake through a capital increase invalidated the original agreement.  

The 2000 partnership emerged from a competitive bid between GM and DaimlerChrysler to secure an alliance with the Italian automaker. Now, with hours remaining, the fate of this long-standing deal hangs in the balance.

The direpack package aims to provide a set of modern statistical dimension reduction techniques as a single, consistent package in Python. The included methods fall into three categories: projection pursuit-based dimension reduction, sufficient dimension reduction, and robust M estimators for dimension reduction. In addition, regularized regression estimators based on these reduced dimension spaces are provided, ranging from classical principal component regression to sparse partial robust M regression. The package also includes a variety of pre-processing utilities, such as generalized spatial signs, as well as dedicated plotting functionality and cross-validation utilities. Furthermore, direpack has been designed to be consistent with the scikit-learn API, making it easy to incorporate the included estimators into statistical and/or machine learning pipelines.

A Russian oil company, Yukos, is facing a legal battle with the Russian government over the forced sale of its key oil production unit, Yuganskneftegas. Yukos had filed for bankruptcy in the US in an attempt to use international business law to halt the forced sale of the unit, which was sold for $9.4 billion to state oil firm Rosneft. However, the sale was disrupted by the Russian authorities, who are seeking to collect a $27 billion (£14 billion) tax bill from the company. Yukos' lawyers are now claiming that the auction violated US bankruptcy law, and the company and its main shareholders are vowing to go after any company that buys its assets using all and every legal means.

Understanding the evolution of graphs over time is a complex and multifaceted concept across various domains, including bioinformatics, physics, and social networks. In many cases, edges in graphs can represent short-term interactions, such as messaging, or long-term structural connections, such as friendships. In practice, long-term edges are often specified by humans, which can be both costly and suboptimal for the downstream task. To address these challenges, we propose a novel approach based on temporal point processes and variational autoencoders that learns to infer temporal attention between nodes by observing node communication dynamics. By leveraging the temporal attention mechanism, the model can learn to adapt to the evolving nature of node interactions, providing more flexibility while avoiding the issues associated with human-specified edges.

This groundbreaking paper presents a cutting-edge millimeter-scale CMOS 64x64 single charged particle radiation detector system for external beam cancer radiotherapy. By leveraging a 1x1 μm^2 diode for energy deposition measurement in the depletion region, the innovative array design boasts a colossal detection area of 512x512 μm^2. Instead of relying on voltage drop sensing, the proposed system revolutionizes the field by measuring the pulse width, or the time it takes for the voltage to return to its baseline, providing a more accurate and efficient means of radiation detection.

The prototype ASIC, fabricated in TSMC's 65 nm LP CMOS process, consumes an average static power of just 0.535 mW under 1.2 V analog and digital power supply, making it a power-efficient and compact solution. To further validate the system's functionality, the authors successfully verified its operation in a clinical 67.5 MeV proton beam setting, marking a significant breakthrough in the field of radiation detection. With its unparalleled detection capabilities and energy efficiency, this system poises to transform the field of cancer radiotherapy and beyond.

My journey to becoming a polar specialist, photographing and specializing in the polar regions, began when I was four years old, when my family relocated from southern Canada to northern Baffin Island, near Greenland. There, we lived among the Inuit in a small community of 200 Inuit people, where we were one of only three non-Inuit families. In this community, we didn't have the luxury of modern conveniences like television, computers, or even a telephone. As a result, I spent most of my time outdoors with the Inuit, playing and learning. The snow and ice served as my playground, and the Inuit were my guides. It was there that I developed a deep fascination with the polar realm.

Electric network frequency fluctuations constitute a powerful tool in multimedia forensics. An efficient approach for ENF estimation is introduced, utilizing temporal windowing based on the filter-bank capon spectral estimator. A type of Gohberg-Semencul factorization of the model covariance matrix is employed due to the Toeplitz structure of the covariance matrix. Moreover, this approach utilizes a temporal window, not necessarily rectangular, at the stage preceding spectral estimation. Krylov matrices are leveraged for fast implementation of matrix inversions. The proposed approach outperforms state-of-the-art methods in ENF estimation, when a short time window of 1 second is employed in power recordings. In speech recordings, the proposed approach yields highly accurate results with respect to both time complexity and accuracy.

The CFTC (Commodity Futures Trading Commission) has approved new rule 6.40C for the NYMEX (New York Mercantile Exchange), which relates to average price calculations by futures commission merchants. The new rule is effective as of October 3, 2000, and it allows FCMS (Futures Commission Merchants) to use their own internal, proprietary systems to generate average price ("AP") calculations when multiple prices are received on an order or series of orders.
The notice to all NYMEX and COMEX members states that the CFTC has approved new rule 6.40C, which is attached to the notice and is now in effect. The new rule allows FCMS to use their own internal, proprietary systems to generate AP calculations when multiple prices are received on an order or series of orders.
The new rule is consistent with a recent CFTC "advisory" regarding the use of internal systems for AP calculations. The advisory was issued to ensure that FCMS are using accurate and reliable methods for calculating APs, particularly in light of the increasing use of electronic trading systems and the potential for errors or discrepancies in the transmission and processing of price data.
