When a new user signs up on a website, we typically have limited information about them, i.e., no interaction with items, no user profile, and no social links with other users. In such circumstances, it is crucial for our recommender systems to effectively attract users at the first point of contact, so that the users decide to stay on the website and become active users. This problem falls under the category of new user cold-start, which is a crucial aspect of the development and survival of a company. Existing approaches on user cold-start recommendation often require additional user efforts, such as setting up an interview process or leveraging side information, such as user demographics, locations, social relations, and so on. However, users may not be willing to provide the required information, and side information on cold-start users is often not available, making it a challenging problem to solve.

Two domes, each embodying contrasting design philosophies. One consists of thousands of steel components, while the other is crafted from a single silk thread. One is synthetic, the other organic. One is imposed upon the environment, the other shapes it. One is designed for nature, the other is designed by nature. Michelangelo famously said that when he gazed upon raw marble, he perceived a figure yearning to be liberated, with the chisel as his sole instrument. However, living beings are not sculpted; they grow naturally. Within our smallest units of life—our cells—resides all the information needed for each cell to function and replicate. Tools come with consequences. Since the Industrial Revolution, the design world has been largely influenced by the demands of manufacturing and mass production.

Sun Microsystems has revolutionized the computing landscape with the launch of its pioneering pay-as-you-go service, offering customers the flexibility to rent immense processing power by the hour. Dubbed Sun Grid, this innovative solution empowers users to tap into the company's vast computing resources, with costs starting at just $1 per hour for a comprehensive package of processing and storage capabilities. This groundbreaking concept, known as grid computing, is poised to transform the industry, with Sun Microsystems at the forefront. By providing on-demand access to computing capacity, the company is redefining the traditional hardware and software paradigm, positioning computing power as a valuable commodity in its own right. Drawing parallels with the evolution of essential utilities, Sun's Chief Operating Officer, Jonathan Schwartz, envisions a future where grid computing matures into a ubiquitous service, akin to electricity and water. "Why invest in building your own grid when you can leverage ours for a fraction of the cost?" Schwartz posed during a webcast at Sun's quarterly Network Computing event in California, highlighting the unparalleled value proposition of Sun Grid.

I have two questions for you. First: whose shoulders do you stand on? And second: what do you stand for? These are the two questions I always begin my poetry workshops with students because, at times, poetry can seem like a dead art form for old white men who appear as if they were born to be old—like Benjamin Button or something. I ask my students these two questions, and then I share how I answer them, which is in these three sentences: I am the daughter of Black writers, who are descended from freedom fighters who broke their chains and changed the world. They call me. These are words I repeat like a mantra before every poetry performance. In fact, I was doing it in the corner over there—making faces.

We introduce a novel mixed variable symplectic (MVS) integrator for planetary systems that facilitates accurate resolution of close encounters while maintaining stability and efficiency. Our approach combines a high-order MVS scheme with a time regularization technique, enabling the algorithm to adapt to the varying distance between planets during close encounters while maintaining a fixed time step. This allows for the efficient integration of systems with large time steps far away from close encounters, while still resolving almost exact collisions when they occur. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our algorithm on a system of six super-Earths undergoing strong scattering over a period of 50 kyr, comparing its performance to hybrid methods such as Mercury. Our results show that, at an equivalent cost, our algorithm provides significantly better energy conservation and more accurate long-term behavior.

Iraq's Ministry of Communications has invited bids for two telephone licenses, aiming to significantly boost nationwide coverage over the next decade. The ministry has invited local, Arab, and foreign companies to submit their bids. The successful bidder will partner with the Iraqi Telecommunications and Post Company (ITPC) to install and operate a fixed phone network, providing voice, fax, and internet services. The ministry aims to increase Iraq's very low telephone service penetration rate from around 4.5% to about 25% within 10 years, also hoping to develop a highly visible and changeable telecommunications sector. The details of the bidding and tender process will be published on the ministry's website on February 9. Additionally, the ministry is planning a roadshow for investors in Amman, Jordan.

Hearts secured a commanding 2-1 victory over Livingston in the Scottish Cup quarter-finals, delivering an explosive start with two early goals. The match ignited within the first minute as Lee Miller capitalized on Jamie McAllister's precision free-kick, soaring above the defense to nod a looping header past goalkeeper Colin Meldrum. McAllister then turned scorer just nine minutes later, meeting Saul Mikoliunas' pinpoint cross at the far post to extend Hearts' dominance. Despite a contentious second-half header from Craig Easton, the hosts maintained their composure to seal the win.  

The breakthrough came after Gus Bahoken's rash challenge on Miller 35 yards out. McAllister's expertly delivered set-piece found Miller, who outmaneuvered Livingston's stagnant backline to guide his effort over Meldrum. Hearts' relentless pressure continued, and Mikoliunas' blistering run down the right flank set up McAllister for a clinical finish, leaving Livingston with too much ground to recover.

Sustainability encompasses the what, where, and how of seafood production. However, it's the who and why that truly matter to me. I'm interested in learning about the people behind my dinner choices, understanding how my decisions impact them, and how they, in turn, impact me. I want to know their motivations for fishing and how they rely on the ocean's resources for their livelihood. By gaining this insight, we can shift our perspective on seafood from a commodity to an opportunity to restore our ecosystem. This allows us to appreciate the seafood we're fortunate to eat, while also promoting a more positive relationship with the environment. I believe this approach can be described as restorative seafood. While sustainability focuses on endurance and maintenance, restorative seafood is about replenishing and progressing, ultimately creating a more sustainable future.

As the third installment in the trilogy, I'll address the drawbacks first. I'm generally not a fan of prequels because they move backward in a narrative rather than advancing it (a common issue with all prequels). Nevertheless, they often add depth to the storyline. Unfortunately, the combat in this game feels less refined than the sharp mechanics achieved in Arkham City. At the time of writing this review (October 26, 2013), there's a game-breaking bug that prevents the completion of a side mission, although the main storyline remains unaffected. Additionally, some gadgets are assigned to impractical hotkeys, requiring you to stretch across the keyboard. This might not seem significant, but it can disrupt your combo in the heat of combat.

In the spreadsheet below, prepared by Jdasovic.nsf, you'll find the implicit commodity values based on the effective dates of four different rates within the same rate schedule (Schedule DR). For the period from August 2000 to September 2000, the rate schedule results in a significantly higher commodity charge. This is because SDG&E customers are expected to receive a lump sum reimbursement from SDG&E during these months, leading them to anticipate lower electric bills. If we do not consider this lump sum reimbursement in the implicit energy component, the average from June 2000 through January 2001 is 6.2 cents, while from February 2001 through December 2001, it's 7.3 cents. The overall average implicit commodity for June 2000 through December 2001 is 6.9 cents. For deliveries beginning September 1, 2000, the average remains at 6.9 cents.

Doves soar to UK album summit: Manchester rock band Doves have entered the UK album chart at number one with their new release, *Some Cities*. The trio replaces flamboyant US act Scissor Sisters at the top. The album follows the single *Black and White Town*, which reached number six.  

R&B star Nelly has the new number one single with *Over and Over*, featuring Tim McGraw. Girls Aloud, Akon, and Kaiser Chiefs all have new singles in the top ten, alongside Futureheads and Usher.  

The latest Elvis Presley re-release, *(Marie's the Name) His Latest Flame*, entered the chart at number three, one place ahead of Girls Aloud's *Wake Me Up*. Hip-hop performer Akon's *Locked Up* is at number five, while hotly tipped Leeds band Kaiser Chiefs have their second chart hit at number six with *Oh My God*.

Chris Anderson: Hello, TED community, welcome back for another live conversation. This one is particularly significant. When we created our 'Build Back Better' series, our goal was to address the issues arising from the pandemic and imagine how to rebuild from it. However, the events of the past week, including the horrific death of George Floyd and the subsequent daily protests, have added a new sense of urgency that we must address. Can we build back better from this? I think before we can even begin to answer that question, we need to seek to understand the magnitude of this moment. Whitney Pennington Rodgers: That's right, Chris.

This game was a delightful surprise. The reviews of certain products are often misleading, but in this case, the reviews accurately reflect the enjoyable experience of this charming game. It's an intriguing blend of a classic 2D platformer and a MOBA, executed with style. I found myself growing attached to characters I wouldn't typically enjoy in other MOBA games. Initially, I was skeptical about the melee tank, but was pleasantly surprised by the outcome and ended up trying out more characters as they became available through playing games. The art style and interface are delightfully charming. As a result, I now use one of the characters as my avatar and a wallpaper from creating a card set for the game.

I'm a cultural omnivore, whose daily commute is facilitated by my attachment to an iPod, which plays a diverse range of music, from Wagner and Mozart to pop diva Christina Aguilera, country singer Josh Turner, gangsta rap artist Kirk Franklin, concerti, symphonies, and more. As a voracious reader, I've devoured the works of Ian McEwan and Stephanie Meyer, including the Twilight tetralogy. I'm also passionate about my home theater, where I indulge in DVDs, video-on-demand, and a wide variety of television shows. For me, 'Law & Order  SVU', Tina Fey's '30 Rock', and 'Judge Judy' are must-watches – the people are real, the cases are real, and the rulings are final.

Citizens of the world, we are facing a global crisis of unprecedented scale. The climate emergency is the defining challenge of our time, and we are in a race against time to avert the worst impacts of global heating. A growing tide of impatience is building, particularly among young people, over the lack of global action. It is imperative that we step up our efforts to achieve a carbon-neutral world by 2050. I am pleased to see the launch of TED Countdown, as your influence and ideas can help accelerate momentum towards this critical goal. We have the tools, science, and resources at our disposal. Now, let us muster the political will and energy to overcome this challenge and secure a sustainable future for all.

The other day, I was reading something, and the person said, probably the largest single cause of disease is handshaking in the West.

Where does all the carbon we release go? Carbon plays a crucial role in a natural cycle, present in all living things  in the soil, in the oceans, in the atmosphere, and in rocks deep underground. When plants and other living organisms die, much of their stored carbon is released back to the atmosphere, where it is absorbed by new plants and other organisms. However, over millions of years, some of the carbon stored in ancient trees and sea life becomes rock, while some becomes fossil fuels  coal, oil, and natural gas. Natural events like volcanic eruptions also release some of the carbon trapped in rock. Furthermore, human activities, such as burning fossil fuels, also release a significant amount of prehistoric carbon. Today, humans release approximately 60 times more carbon than all the volcanoes on the planet each year.

We introduce a novel mixed variable symplectic (MVS) integrator for planetary systems, capable of resolving close encounters with precision. Our approach is founded on a time-regularization technique that preserves the stability properties of symplectic integrators while adaptively reducing the step size during close encounters between planets. By employing a high-order MVS scheme, we enable the integration of large time steps, even in the presence of distant close encounters. We demonstrate the efficacy of this algorithm in resolving almost exact collisions, with a mutual separation of a fraction of the physical radius, using the same time-step as in weakly perturbed systems, such as the solar system. Our long-term simulations of six super-Earths experiencing strong scattering over 50,000 years reveal the stability and accuracy of our approach. We compare our method to hybrid methods like Mercury and show that, for an equivalent computational cost, we achieve significantly better energy conservation.

Britain's economic future will be central to Labour's election campaign, Chancellor Gordon Brown has stated. He spoke after cabinet members held their final meeting at No. 10 before the expected election announcement. Brown asserted that voters would recognize Labour's role in bringing stability and growth and that this would continue. Meanwhile, the Tories outlined their plans to address "yob culture," and the Lib Dems provided more details about their proposals to replace council tax. Earlier, the Archbishop of Canterbury wrote to all three parties, urging them not to exploit people's fears during the election. In an open letter, he called on them not to make the election a contest about who can best scare voters regarding terrorism, asylum, and crime.

I have only had this game for a few months, but in that short amount of time, two DLCs have come out, along with patches that have turned an already great game into an excellent one. The devs are very active in the community, and it shows in the various discussion forums and dev diaries. There is a fairly steep learning curve to the game's mechanics, which I am still trying to learn; however, this breadth of strategy allows for a myriad of ways to reach goals and take advantage of certain situations. The game, in my mind, is almost infinitely replayable. I find myself drawn into every session I play, regardless of how the play-through is going.

Overall, the game is a fantastic FPS shooter. The graphics, sound, and gameplay are all top-notch - great mechanics, engaging story, memorable characters, and a fantastic voice acting, along with a killer soundtrack. However, it's not for the faint of heart, as some of the cutscenes can be quite graphic and intense. Despite this, it's hard to overlook the flaws when the game is this good, and I noticed a few issues  no SLI support, which made the game run poorly on my old dual-GPU GTX 590. Make sure to have a decent single-GPU card to play this game smoothly.

One simple vitamin can reduce your risk of heart disease. Eating chocolate reduces stress in students. A new drug prolongs the lives of patients with a rare disease. Health headlines like these are published every day, sometimes even making opposite claims from each other. There can be a disconnect between broad, attention-grabbing headlines and the often specific, incremental results of the medical research they cover. So how can you avoid being misled by such grabby headlines? The best way to assess a headline’s credibility is to look at the original research it reports on. We’ve come up with a hypothetical research scenario for each of these three headlines. Keep watching for the explanation of the first example; then pause at the headline to answer the question. These are simplified scenarios.

Warframe is an engaging game for the initial hours of play. However, beyond that, you may start to notice its repetitive nature in combat, enemies, and overall gameplay. A notable strength is the dedication of its developers, who frequently release updates packed with substantial new content, from extensive system changes to new warframes and other enhancements. It's an enjoyable game for casual play, although it might struggle to keep your interest for more than a few months unless you particularly enjoy grinding. The advantage is that it's free to play, so I recommend giving it a try. If you're in need of a quick gaming experience, it's worth checking out.

Very late last night, I finished this. The story isn't very deep or interesting, but what makes it stand out is the storytelling. The atmosphere is rich, and the many collectibles do a great job of fleshing it out. I did learn a lot about the world of Batman, so it was a bit disappointing that there were so few bosses in the game. Oh well, there are enough future games to rectify that. The combat offers the right amount of challenge, though it's more on the easy side. Leveling up Batman wasn't very satisfying; it's the gadgets you upgrade and acquire during the campaign that add nice new gameplay mechanics. Gaining access to previously unreachable places provides a nice sense of progression.

For two decades, I was deeply entrenched in the world of finance, where I had cultivated an unparalleled expertise. My profession had become an integral part of my life, allowing me to develop a profound understanding of the global economy. I was well-versed in a wide range of topics, from ecological concerns to the potential consequences of global catastrophes. However, despite my extensive knowledge, I must admit that I was skeptical about the severity of these issues. That was until 2007, when the subprime crisis struck, sending shockwaves through the financial sector and culminating in the collapse of several major American banks. This unprecedented event served as a wake-up call, prompting me to reevaluate my career and the limitations of the financial models I had long relied on. The crisis sparked heated debates with my wife, as I began to realize that my previous convictions were misguided, and that we were, in fact, on a catastrophic trajectory.

Frank Arnesen, the sporting director of Tottenham, has dismissed claims of a conflict with coach Jacques Santini, asserting that Santini's resignation was not due to a personality clash at White Hart Lane. Recent media reports suggested that Santini felt undermined by Arnesen's position at the club. However, Arnesen countered these assertions in an interview with BBC Radio Five Live, stating, "That is absolutely not true. The only reason for his resignation is his personal issues." Arnesen mentioned that Santini had recently communicated to him that the matter was strictly personal. Despite this, Arnesen admitted he could not provide details about the personal issues that led Santini to step down after just 13 games in charge. He remarked, "Jacques has never shared specifics, but I trust him and believe we must respect his decision.

I was initially biased against this game before I played it and wasn't even sure about buying it. I don’t know; the whole portal gun and portals concept seemed odd to me, even though I like science fiction. Boy, was I wrong. The jokes and voice acting in this game are phenomenal—simply amazing. It's not cheesy (okay, sometimes it is), and each character has their own unique sense of humor and distinct personality. And the level design? It's just fantastic. By the way, I never played the first Portal game. One of these days, I'm planning to, if I can find the time. I would never have played it if I hadn't played the sequel.

A new generation of two-dimensional materials, including res$_2$ and rese$_2$ monolayers, black phosphorus monolayers, and group-iv monochalcogenide monolayers, as well as borophene, is redefining the landscape of low-dimensional physics. Unlike graphene, hexagonal boron nitride, and transition metal dichalcogenides, which exhibit the characteristic honeycomb lattice structure, these emerging materials possess intricate atomistic arrangements that deviate from high symmetry. This reduced symmetry gives rise to a rich tapestry of inhomogeneous responses, encompassing electron, optical, valley, and spin phenomena, as well as novel properties such as ferroelasticity, ferroelectricity, magnetism, and superconductivity. Furthermore, the unique structural characteristics of these materials enable the emergence of nonlinear optical properties, spin-wave phenomena, photogalvanic effects, and large nonlinear elastic responses. The interplay between low symmetry and anisotropy also facilitates the manifestation of novel electronic topological properties, nonlinear elastic behavior, and structural phase transformations. In recognition of the burgeoning interest in these materials, the special topic "Beyond Graphene: Low-Symmetry and Anisotropic 2D Materials" has been curated to showcase the latest experimental and theoretical advances in this rapidly evolving field.

Robinson wants dual-code success; England rugby union captain Jason Robinson has targeted dual-code success over Australia on Saturday. Robinson, a former rugby league international before switching codes in 2000, leads England against Australia at Twickenham at 1430 GMT. And at 1815 GMT, Great Britain's rugby league team take on Australia in the final of the Tri-Nations tournament. "Beating the Aussies in both games would be a massive achievement, especially for league," said Robinson. England have the chance to seal their third autumn international victory after successive wins over Canada and South Africa, as well as gaining revenge for June's 51-15 hammering by the Wallabies. Meanwhile, Great Britain could end 34 years of failure against Australia with victory at Elland Road.

We examine the connection between the two constructions of the mirror partner for the Calabi-Yau orbifold. This orbifold is defined as a quotient by a suitable subgroup g of the phase symmetries of the hypersurface x_m in the weighted projective space, cut out by a quasi-homogeneous polynomial w_m. The first Berglund-H\"ubsch-Krawitz (BHKS) construction employs another weighted projective space and the quotient of a new hypersurface x_{m^t} within it by a dual group g^t. In the second Batyrev construction, the mirror partner is constructed as a hypersurface in the toric variety defined by the reflexive polytope dual to the polytope associated with the original Calabi-Yau orbifold. We provide a straightforward proof of the equivalence of these two constructions, demonstrating the identical nature of the resulting mirror partners.

In this paper, we investigate multi-agent discrete-event systems with partial observation. The agents can be divided into several groups, in each of which the agents have similar (isomorphic) state transition structures and thus can be relabeled into the same template. Based on the template, a scalable supervisor whose state size and computational cost are independent of the number of agents is designed for the case of partial observation. The scalable supervisor under partial observation does not need to be recomputed, regardless of how many agents are added to or removed from the system. We generalize our earlier results to partial observation by proposing sufficient conditions for safety and maximal permissiveness of the scalable least restrictive supervisor at the template level. An example is provided to illustrate the proposed scalable supervisory synthesis.

The direpack package aims to establish a comprehensive and modern set of statistical dimension reduction techniques within the Python universe as a single, unified package. The dimension reduction methods included in this package are categorized into three distinct categories  projection pursuit-based dimension reduction, sufficient dimension reduction, and robust M-estimators for dimension reduction. As a corollary, regularized regression estimators based on these reduced dimension spaces are also provided, ranging from classical principal component regression to sparse partial robust M-regression. The package also includes a suite of classical and robust pre-processing utilities, including generalized spatial signs, as well as dedicated plotting functionality and cross-validation utilities. Furthermore, direpack has been written to be consistent with the scikit-learn API, ensuring that the estimators can seamlessly be integrated into statistical and/or machine learning pipelines within that framework.

Where do I even start with this absolutely beautiful game? Its story is absolutely charming, with so much detail put into it, making it a bundle of happiness, bad puns, annoying dogs, and secrets. It knows when to be serious, but also isn't a stranger to funny moments. The character design is on point, featuring various characters with unique personalities and secrets. What sets it apart from other RPGs is that you don't have to kill anyone, a refreshingly unused concept in the genre. Drawing inspiration from Earthbound, one of the best RPGs I've seen, it's clear that this game is something special. The soundtrack is also noteworthy, easily the best I've heard in a long time.

Sid Meier's Civilization: Beyond Earth is a turn-based game where you build your civilization to achieve a certain goal or 'victory'. Unlike previous games, which followed the Earth's timeline through periods like the Stone Age, Medieval Age, and Modern Age, this game takes place in the future, where you're on a mission to colonize a new planet. What I enjoy about this game is that 2K took a new approach and added mechanics like the affinity system and orbital layer. However, I found that these new mechanics could be overwhelming at times. One potential improvement could be incorporating new artwork, such as the environment you experience when communicating with another leader.

Andrea Levy's novel, 'Small Island', has won the Whitbread Novel of the Year award, marking a major achievement for the author. Levy's book, which was previously a favourite to win the overall prize, has beaten off the Booker Prize-winning author, Alan Hollinghurst, for his novel 'The Line of Beauty'. Meanwhile, Geraldine McCaughrean has taken home the children's fiction award for 'Not the End of the World' for the third time. The Whitbread Book of the Year award is a prestigious honour, with a £25,000 prize attached to it. A panel of judges, comprising Sir Trevor McDonald, actor Hugh Grant, and writer Joanne Harris, will meet on January 25 to decide the overall winner, with the announcement being made later that evening. Bookmaker William Hill has placed London-based Levy's novel as the 6/4 favourite to win, reflecting the strong public backing for her work.

Have you ever had one of those moments when you start reflecting on life? Like when you're sitting in math class, utterly bored, and you begin to wonder what exactly happens in your brain that allows you to comprehend math? Then it dawns on you—you exist, you're in math class, and you question why. Suddenly, you're struck by how incredible existence is. And just as quickly, it hits you—the fact that you're spiraling into this random train of thought is downright pitiful. Ever experienced that? I despise it.  

You know what else I hate? Block schedules. Who in their right mind thought that was a good idea? It’s obvious that enduring a two-and-a-half-hour class in any subject is nothing short of cruel and unusual punishment.

I am uncomfortable with the concept of investigative journalism. The article I am writing and its implications do not sit well with me, and I am not entirely convinced of its validity. Perhaps it is because I do not fully understand or believe in it. The passion and urgency behind it are not something I am accustomed to, and I struggle to fully grasp the motivations behind it. I have grown up in a society where questioning authority is not encouraged, and I am not used to expressing my doubts out loud. In my country, questioning the government or any authority figure can lead to serious consequences, including imprisonment. As a result, I am not comfortable with the freedom of speech and the ability to question things openly. I am not an easy person to get along with, and this discomfort only adds to the complexity of the situation.

Arun Shourie discusses how, as far back as 1996, the then-governor of Uttar Pradesh warned about the impending threat of the rise of a 'third Islamic state' in the subcontinent. T.V. Rajeswar, the former head of the Intelligence Bureau and the current governor of Uttar Pradesh, had predicted this dire threat in a series of articles published in the Hindustan Times. Shourie raises a valid point when he asks, does the fact that it was Rajeswar, not I, and certainly not Mr. Advani, who wrote these warnings make them more credible? Or do you conclude that Rajeswar's repeated warnings make him a closet communalist and therefore unfit to continue as governor of Uttar Pradesh?

Welsh rugby star Mefin Davies appears set to continue his career with Gloucester Rugby, despite attracting attention from the Ospreys. The 32-year-old hooker, who delivered a standout performance in Wales' recent Six Nations victory over England, is reportedly content with his current club. While Davies' existing one-year contract at Kingsholm is nearing its end, his impressive contributions to the Premiership side make a contract extension highly probable.  

Davies' journey to Gloucester followed a challenging period after the dissolution of the Celtic Warriors, which left him without a regional team. He initially joined Neath RFC on a semi-professional basis before securing opportunities with the Ospreys, albeit under strained circumstances. At the time, Davies openly expressed frustration over his treatment before ultimately making the move to England, where he has since revitalized his career.

We introduce a groundbreaking concept for a novel Skyrme-like conserved current within the framework of the effective theory of pions and vector mesons, rooted in the paradigm of hidden local symmetry. The associated charge is analogous to the skyrmion charge for any smooth configuration. Furthermore, we demonstrate the existence of singular configurations that can be identified as n_f=1 baryons charged under the novel symmetry. Under this identification, the vector mesons assume the role of Chern-Simons vector fields residing on the quantum Hall droplet that constitutes the n_f=1 baryon. We propose that this current represents the correct effective expression for the baryon current at low energies. This proposal yields a unified framework for the two types of baryons, enabling a natural, continuous transformation between them. Moreover, Chern-Simons dualities on the droplet can be interpreted as a consequence of Seiberg-like duality between gluons and vector mesons.

Scotland's Justice Minister, Cathy Jamieson, has issued a stern warning to bigoted soccer fans, stating that she intends to take a tough stance by banning them from matches, effectively hitting them "where it hurts most". As part of the Scottish Executive's campaign against sectarianism, exclusion orders are being considered as one of several measures. Jamieson commended Celtic and Rangers for their efforts in addressing the issue, but emphasized that eradicating sectarian abuse associated with Old Firm matches remains a top priority. Her comments came ahead of the highly anticipated Scottish Cup clash between the Glasgow clubs at Parkhead on Sunday. The Scottish Executive has made tackling sectarianism, long linked to certain sections of support from both clubs, a key objective. Recently, Jamieson and First Minister Jack McConnell met with supporters' representatives from both clubs to discuss the matter.

I'd like to take you on the epic quest of the Rosetta spacecraft. To escort and land the probe on a comet, this has been my passion for the past two years. In order to do that, I need to explain to you something about the origin of the solar system. When we go back four and a half billion years, there was a cloud of gas and dust. In the center of this cloud, our sun formed and ignited. Along with that, what we now know as planets, comets, and asteroids formed. What then happened, according to theory, is that when the Earth had cooled down a bit after its formation, comets massively impacted the Earth and delivered water to Earth. They probably also delivered complex organic material to Earth, and that may have bootstrapped the emergence of life.

Jdasovic.nsf Monday, December 11, 2000 To: All PX Market Participants From: John Yurkanin Chief Operating Officer Re: Loss of Continuity in PX Day-Ahead Auction Due to ISO Rule Changes The emergency tariff filing by the California ISO last Friday has had a negative effect on the PX day-ahead auction market. The mixture of soft and hard caps results in a complete disconnect and unacceptable risk to all of our participants, and will probably not provide the ISO with its sought-after reduction in real-time energy sales and procurement. The PX is petitioning Terry Winter of the ISO to immediately allow for adjustment bids on any submitted schedule at any relevant price.

Yukos, a US-based oil company, is in the process of being broken up by Russian authorities in order to pay a $27bn (£14bn) tax bill. The company had filed for bankruptcy in the US in an attempt to use international business law to halt the forced sale of its key oil production unit, Yuganskneftegas. However, the unit was sold for $9.4bn to state oil firm Rosneft, but only after the state auction had been disrupted. Yukos' lawyers now say that the auction violated US bankruptcy law. The company and its main shareholders have vowed to go after any company that buys its assets, using all and every legal means.

Ahimsa (uh-him-sah) is a noun that refers to the principle of non-violence towards all living beings. The term is derived from Sanskrit, where 'a-' means 'not' and 'himsa' means 'injury', originating from 'himsati', meaning 'he injures'. As my understanding of ahimsa matured, I became more conscious of my thoughts and speech. Phrases such as "scatter her enemies and make them fall," and "confound their policies, frustrate their knavish tricks" clashed with my beliefs in ahimsa," wrote M.K. Gandhi in his autobiography, "The Story of My Experiments with Truth," published in 1927. Today, we commemorate Gandhi's birthday, October 2, as he was born in 1869. Though he was born over a century and a half ago, Gandhi remains profoundly relevant to our world, with his life and message continuing to resonate with us today.

The exploration of innovative concepts, perspectives, and challenges in the measurement and control of open quantum systems through sequential methods is presented. We delve into similar protocols that leverage repeated quantum measurements and dynamical decoupling control pulses, enabling us to: (i) restrict and safeguard quantum dynamics from decoherence, in line with Zeno physics principles; (ii) accurately forecast the likelihood of a quantum system transitioning into a desired quantum state through stochastic sequential measurements; (iii) effectively reconstruct the spectral density of environmental noise by orthogonalizing the filter functions in the frequency domain, which govern the operation of the quantum sensor. Accomplishing these objectives will enhance our ability to observe and manipulate open quantum systems, contributing to progress in quantum science and technology.

Mutation-based fuzzing relies on an initial corpus of non-crashing seed inputs that serve as the foundation for generating new test cases through modification. However, these seed collections frequently contain numerous redundant inputs with minimal variation, resulting in inefficient fuzzing as the system exhaustively processes near-identical samples. To optimize this process, modern fuzzers incorporate distillation techniques (such as afl-cmin) that identify and retain only the most essential seeds—those capable of exercising the same breadth of program instrumentation as the complete corpus. Industry experience indicates that reducing both the quantity and total size of seed inputs enhances fuzzing efficiency, a hypothesis we rigorously examine through extensive experimentation. Our study presents findings from over 34 CPU-years of comprehensive testing, evaluating five distinct distillation methodologies and their effectiveness in uncovering vulnerabilities within production-grade software systems.

On October 4, 1957, the world watched in awe and fear as the Soviet Union launched Sputnik, the world's first man-made satellite, into space. This small metal sphere, less than two feet in diameter, ignited a space race between the U.S. and the U.S.S.R. that would last for eighteen years and reshape the world. Sputnik was not actually the first piece of human technology to enter space. That distinction belongs to the V-2 rocket, used by Germany in missile attacks against Allied cities as a last-ditch effort in the final years of World War II. Although it wasn't very effective, at the end of the war, both the U.S. and the U.S.S.R. captured the technology and scientists who developed it, and began using them for their own projects.

The club has a strong ethos and is a high-profile promoter of peace and co-operation within Israel.

A Georgia woman has filed a class-action lawsuit against Hewlett-Packard (HP), alleging that the company deliberately programs its printer ink cartridges to deactivate prematurely. According to the complaint, an embedded microchip falsely signals that the cartridge is empty, forcing users to replace it even when ink remains. The lawsuit aims to include all U.S. consumers who purchased HP inkjet printers since February 2001.  

HP, the global leader in printer manufacturing, has declined to address the allegations. While the company states that its cartridge chips are designed to monitor ink levels and prompt timely replacements, the lawsuit contends that these chips are also programmed to disable cartridges on a preset expiration date, irrespective of actual ink supply.

A US woman is suing Hewlett-Packard (HP), alleging that its printer ink cartridges are secretly programmed to expire on a specific date. The unnamed woman from Georgia claims that a chip inside the cartridge tells the printer that it needs to be refilled, even when it does not. The lawsuit aims to represent anyone in the US who has purchased an HP inkjet printer since February 2001. HP, the world's leading printer manufacturer, declined to comment on the lawsuit. HP's ink cartridges employ a chip technology to sense when they are low on ink and advise the user to make a change. However, the suit claims that the chips also shut down the cartridges at a predetermined date, regardless of whether they are empty or not.

Metal ion implantation into ceramics has been demonstrated to be an effective and controllable technique for tailoring the surface electrical conductivity of ceramic pieces, and this approach has been used in numerous applications. Importantly, it provides a method for grading the voltage drop across high-voltage insulators, thereby increasing the maximum operational voltage that can be applied without surface flashover. However, a concern regarding this method is the long-term stability of the implantation-induced conductivity, particularly if the implanted metal species is readily oxidized. Here, we report on our examination of the long-term behavior of the surface conductivity of titanium-implanted alumina. The results indicate that after an initial drop of up to 40% in the first few weeks post-implantation, the conductivity exhibits only a very slow decrease of approximately 10% over the following year.

Observations of thermally driven transverse vibrations of a photonic crystal waveguide (PCW) are reported. The PCW consists of two parallel nanobeams with a 240 nm vacuum gap between the beams. Models are developed and validated for the transduction of beam motion to phase and amplitude modulation of a weak optical probe propagating in a guided mode of the PCW for probe frequencies both far from and near the dielectric band edge. Since our PCW has been designed for near-field atom trapping, this research provides a foundation for evaluating the potential detrimental effects of thermal motion on optical atomic traps near the surfaces of PCWs. Long-term goals are to achieve strong atom-mediated links between individual phonons of vibration and single photons propagating in the guided modes of the PCW, thereby enabling optomechanics at the quantum level with atoms, photons, and phonons.

For nearly a quarter century, I've proudly served as a firefighter in Huntington, West Virginia. My team and I dedicate ourselves to protecting lives and property—responding to vehicle accidents, structural fires, and critical medical emergencies. As a woman leading in this traditionally male-dominated field, I've continually sought ways to enhance our community's safety. A decade ago, recognizing that modern threats extend beyond immediate crises, I pursued a nursing degree. This decision came from understanding that our greatest challenges are no longer single-incident disasters where firefighters can heroically intervene and depart. Today's complex emergencies require sustained medical expertise to address ongoing public health needs that persist long after the initial emergency response.

NOTICE NO. 00-338: CFTC APPROVAL OF NEW NYMEX RULE 6.40C

MEMORANDUM

To: All NYMEX and COMEX Members
From: Neal L. Wolkoff, Executive Vice President
Date: October 3, 2000

Subject: Implementation of New Rule 6.40C: Average Price Calculations by Futures Commission Merchants

We are pleased to announce that the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) has approved the introduction of new NYMEX Rule 6.40C, which is now in effect. This new rule, attached herewith, aligns with a recent CFTC advisory and provides Futures Commission Merchants (FCMs) with the flexibility to utilize their proprietary systems for generating average price calculations. This applies to instances where multiple prices are received for a single order or a series of orders, subject to specific conditions outlined in the rule.

During a recent class discussion, our analysis of Edward S. Casey's "Getting Back into Place: Toward a Renewed Study of the Place-World" led to a thought-provoking exploration of the interplay between time and place in shaping our culture. The book's initial chapters, which delve into the concepts of "implacement" and "displacement," offer a compelling critique of our contemporary world's preoccupation with time as the primary situating force. Casey astutely observes that our societal narratives often revolve around time, with phrases such as "running out of time," "saving time," and "just in time" dominating our discourse. In contrast, he posits that a place-centric approach to culture could foster a more grounded and cohesive community. While this summary barely scratches the surface of Casey's nuanced argument, it is clear that his work has resonated deeply, making it the first assigned reading to truly capture my attention.

Routing controllability of autonomous vehicles (AVs) has been demonstrated to mitigate the adverse effects of selfish routing on network efficiency. However, the assumption that AVs would readily accept external control by a central agency is unrealistic. This paper proposes a joint routing and pricing control scheme that incentivizes AVs to adopt centrally controlled system-optimal (SO) routing by offering toll savings, while user equilibrium (UE) seeking AVs and human-driven vehicles (HDVs) are subject to a congestion charge. The problem is formulated as a bi-level optimization, where dynamic tolls are optimized at the upper level, and the lower level employs a mixed equilibrium simulation-based dynamic traffic assignment model that accounts for a mixed fleet of AVs and HDVs.

Entanglement is a fundamental component in quantum information processing, and its detection is crucial for harnessing its power. A widely employed method for identifying entanglement is through the use of entanglement witnesses, which are operators that yield non-negative measurement outcomes for all separable states, while potentially producing negative results for certain entangled states. However, constructing an efficient entanglement witness for general multipartite entangled systems poses significant challenges due to the exponential growth of the Hilbert space dimension with respect to system size. In contrast, for a specific class of n-partite Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ)-like states, a robust witness scheme has been developed, requiring n+1 local measurement settings and capable of withstanding up to 50% white noise contamination. Notably, a more efficient witness for GHZ-like states has also been proposed, necessitating only two local measurement settings and tolerating up to 33% white noise, thereby offering a more practical solution for entanglement detection in these systems.

Entanglement is a key resource for quantum information processing. A widely used tool for detecting entanglement is the entanglement witness, where the measurement of the witness operator is guaranteed to be positive for all separable states and can be negative for certain entangled states. In reality, due to the exponential increase of the Hilbert-space dimension with respect to the system size, it is very challenging to construct an efficient entanglement witness for general multipartite entangled states. For $n$-partite Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ)-like states, the most robust witness scheme requires $n+1$ local measurement settings and can tolerate up to $1/2$ white noise. In comparison, the most efficient witness for GHZ-like states only needs two local measurement settings and can tolerate up to $1/3$ white noise.

Cardinal Cormac Murphy-O'Connor has criticized the Iraq War, stating that the billions of pounds spent on the conflict in Iraq and the Middle East should have been used to reduce poverty. The head of the Catholic Church in England and Wales made the comments on BBC Radio 4 and will reiterate his stance in his Christmas Midnight Mass. In his Christmas message, the cardinal denounced the war in Iraq as a "terrible" waste of money. He and the Archbishop of Canterbury have both spoken out against the war. Speaking on BBC Radio 4's Thought for the Day slot, the cardinal criticized the fact that "billions" have been spent on war, instead of being used to bring people "out of dire poverty, malnutrition, and disease." The cardinal hopes that 2005 will be a year of campaigning to "make history and end poverty.

Iraq is set to invite bids for two telephone licenses, aiming to significantly enhance nationwide coverage in the coming decade. The invitation is open to local, Arab, and international companies, according to Iraq's Ministry of Communications. The selected company will collaborate with the Iraqi Telecommunications and Post Company (ITPC) to establish and operate a fixed-line network that offers voice, fax, and internet services. The ministry aims to increase Iraq's current telephone service penetration rate dramatically, from approximately 4.5% to around 25% within 10 years. It also seeks to develop a highly dynamic and visible telecommunications sector. Details of the bidding and tender process will be available on the ministry's website on February 9. Additionally, a roadshow for investors is planned in Amman, Jordan.

Hello, my name is Simone. You're probably familiar with the advice that if you get nervous on stage, you should imagine the audience naked. It's supposed to be a calming technique, but to be honest, picturing everyone naked in 2018 feels somewhat awkward and outdated. This game offers a wide variety of ship types, with physics-based movement for both ships and projectiles/missiles. It's easy to learn resource management, and the game has a dark humored, non-grindy feel, especially if you utilize the civvy station types. However, it's not without its drawbacks - the AI teammates can be subpar, and there's a limited variety of equipment types and leveling skills, which don't branch out. Despite these minuses, the game still offers some variety and strategy to be had. It starts off quickly and maintains a steady pace. Although there's a limited selection of equipment, it still feels like there's always another ship to unlock or skill to level up. To increase the game's longevity, mods are the way to go. The fight for universe is easily the star, but the big fight for beef is also very good.

After completing the game, my stats indicate that it took me 8.6 hours to reach the end, with 10 collectibles found out of 25. While I appreciated the visuals throughout the game, I was disappointed by the lackluster storyline. However, the ending was somewhat redeeming, with a unique and unexpected twist that made me feel less disappointed with my time invested. Unfortunately, the last level was a letdown, with uninteresting visuals and increasingly difficult gameplay that made me feel frustrated and eager to move on from the experience. Of the 15 achievements, I was able to unlock only a few, which I found to be too challenging and not worth the effort. Overall, I would recommend this game with reservations, as it has some enjoyable elements but ultimately falls short of its potential.

The implantation of metal ions into ceramics has proven to be an effective and controllable method for modifying the surface electrical conductivity of ceramic components, with numerous practical applications. Notably, this technique enables the grading of voltage distribution across high-voltage insulators, thereby enhancing their maximum operational voltage capacity by preventing surface flashover. However, a key concern regarding this method is the long-term stability of the conductivity induced by implantation, particularly when the implanted metal species is prone to oxidation. In this study, we investigate the long-term evolution of surface conductivity in titanium-implanted alumina. Our findings reveal that, after an initial decline of up to 40% within the first few weeks post-implantation, the conductivity subsequently stabilizes, exhibiting only a gradual reduction of approximately 10% over the following year.

Brizzel to run AAA's in Sheffield. Ballymena sprinter Paul Brizzel will be among eight of Ireland's European indoor hopefuls competing in this weekend's AAA's championships. US-based Alistair Cragg and Mark Carroll are the only Irish athletes selected so far for the Europeans who will not run in Sheffield. Brizzel will defend his 200m title in the British trials. In-form James McIlroy will hope to confirm his place in the British team for Madrid by winning the 800m title. McIlroy has been in tremendous form on the European circuit in recent weeks. He is one of the fastest 800m runners in the world this winter and already seems assured of a place in Madrid. Corkman Mark Carroll confirmed midweek that he would join Cragg in the European Championships. Carroll is ranked number three in the world 3000m ranking at the moment, with Cragg occupying top spot.

Doves have soared to the top of the UK album chart with their latest release, securing the number one spot, surpassing flamboyant US act Scissor Sisters. This marks a triumphant debut for the trio, following the success of their single "Black and White Town", which reached number six. Meanwhile, R&B star Nelly has claimed the top spot on the singles chart with "Over and Over", a collaboration with Tim McGraw. Other notable new entries in the top ten include Girls Aloud, Akon, and Kaiser Chiefs, as well as Futureheads and Usher. The latest re-release of Elvis Presley's "Elvis" (formerly known as "Marie's the Name"), his latest flame, has entered the chart at number three, narrowly missing out on the top spot by one position to Girls Aloud's "Wake Me Up". Hip-hop artist Akon's "Locked Up" has landed at number five, while Leeds-based band Kaiser Chiefs have secured their second chart hit with "Oh My God" at number six.

This was a good weekend. I did a lot of God's green leaf, after a long time, I listened to a lot of Nevermore (something I should have started doing much earlier) and read a lot. I finished three books over the weekend—Bruce Sterling's *Heavy Weather*, Larry Niven's *World of Ptavvs*, and Octavia Butler's *Parable of the Sower*. *Heavy Weather* was a good cyberpunk romp through a near-future USA, devastated by global warming and general anarchy. Not that both aren't well constructed and vividly etched—it's a good book, but not a great one, I guess.

Entanglement is a key resource for quantum information processing. A widely used tool for detecting entanglement is the entanglement witness, where measuring the witness operator is guaranteed to yield positive results for all separable states and can be negative for certain entangled states. In practice, due to the exponentially increasing Hilbert-space dimension with respect to system size, constructing an efficient entanglement witness for general multipartite entangled states is highly challenging. For $n$-partite Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ)-like states, the most robust witness scheme requires $n+1$ local measurement settings and can tolerate up to $1/2$ white noise. In comparison, the most efficient witness for GHZ-like states requires only two local measurement settings and can tolerate up to $1/3$ white noise.

Following the suggestion from the Monte Carlo experiments by Jimenez in 2020, it is found that dipole dynamics play a crucial role in the dynamics of decaying two-dimensional turbulence. The kinetic energy of this flow is primarily carried by elongated streams formed by the concatenation of dipoles. The flow separates into two distinct families of vortices  fast-moving, small cores and slower-moving, larger ones that can be described as 'frozen' into a slowly evolving 'crystal'. The kinematics of these two families are vastly different, with the former exhibiting self-similarity and the latter responsible for the majority of the kinetic energy of the flow. The vortices formed by the latter family are responsible for the growth of the slow-moving component, which plays a vital role in the dynamics of the flow.

Extreme mass ratio inspirals (EMRIs) can be classified as dry EMRIs and wet EMRIs based on their formation mechanisms. Dry (or the "loss-cone") EMRIs, previously considered as the main EMRI sources for the Laser Interferometer Space Antenna, are primarily produced by multi-body scattering in the nuclear star cluster and gravitational capture. In this letter, we highlight an alternative EMRI formation channel: (wet) EMRI formation assisted by the accretion flow around accreting galactic-center massive black holes (MBHs). In this channel, the accretion disk captures stellar-mass black holes that are initially moving on inclined orbits, and subsequently drives them to migrate towards the MBH—this process boosts the formation rate of EMRIs in such galaxies by orders of magnitude.

This paper presents a millimeter-scale CMOS 64$\times$64 single charged particle radiation detector system for external beam cancer radiotherapy. A 1$\times$1 $\mu m^2$ diode measures energy deposition by a single charged particle in the depletion region, and the array design provides a large detection area of 512$\times$512 $\mu m^2$. Instead of sensing the voltage drop caused by radiation, the proposed system measures the pulse width, i.e., the time it takes for the voltage to return to its baseline. This eliminates the need for power-hungry and large analog-to-digital converters. A prototype ASIC is fabricated in TSMC 65 nm LP CMOS process and consumes an average static power of 0.535 mW under 1.2 V analog and digital power supply. The functionality of the whole system is successfully verified in a clinical 67.5 MeV proton beam setting.

Nonlinear fluorescence emission has been widely utilized for high spatial resolution optical imaging. In this study, we explored the fluorescence anomalous saturating effect of the nitrogen vacancy defect in diamond. A reduction in fluorescence was observed with high-power laser excitation, which increased the nonlinearity of the fluorescence emission and altered the spatial frequency distribution of the fluorescence image. We employed a differential excitation protocol to extract high spatial frequency information. By modulating the power of the excitation laser, the spatial resolution of imaging was enhanced approximately 1.6 times compared to confocal microscopy. Given the straightforwardness of the experimental setup and data processing, we anticipate that this method can improve the spatial resolution of sensing and biological labeling with defects in solids.

Prince crowned 'top music earner': Prince earned more than any other pop star in 2004, beating artists such as Madonna and Elton John in US magazine Rolling Stone's annual list. The singer banked $56.5m (£30.4m) from concerts, album, and publishing sales with his Musicology tour and album. He kept Madonna in second place, as she earned $54.9m (£29.5m) while embarking on her global Re-Invention Tour. Veterans Simon and Garfunkel were in 10th place, their comeback tour helping them earn $24.9m (£13.4m) last year. "Prince returned to center stage after a decade in the commercial wilderness," the magazine reported. The singer's 2004 tour took $90.3m (£48.5m) in ticket sales, and he sold 1.9 million copies of his latest album Musicology.

World leaders gather to face uncertainty as more than 2,000 business and political leaders from around the globe arrive in the Swiss mountain resort of Davos for the annual World Economic Forum (WEF). Over five days, they will discuss issues ranging from China's economic power to Iraq's future after Sunday's elections. UK Prime Minister Tony Blair and South African President Thabo Mbeki are among the more than 20 government leaders and heads of state attending the meeting. Unlike previous years, protests against the WEF are expected to be muted. Anti-globalization campaigners have called off a demonstration planned for the weekend. The Brazilian city of Porto Alegre will host the rival World Social Forum, timed to coincide with the WEF's more opulent event in Davos.

Novel concepts, perspectives, and challenges in measuring and controlling open quantum systems through sequential schemes are presented. We explore how similar protocols, leveraging both repeated quantum measurements and dynamical decoupling control pulses, can facilitate: (i) confinement and protection of quantum dynamics from decoherence, in line with Zeno physics principles. (ii) analytical prediction of the probability of transferring a quantum system into a target state via stochastic sequential measurements. (iii) optimal reconstruction of the spectral density of environmental noise sources by orthogonalizing filter functions in the frequency domain, thereby enhancing the performance of designed quantum sensors. The accomplishment of these tasks will significantly enhance our ability to observe and manipulate open quantum systems, driving advancements in quantum science and technologies.

Cabinet Discontent Over Brown's Cash Reallocation

There is dissatisfaction among ministers concerning plans to divert Whitehall funds to offset council tax bills, as confirmed by Local Government Minister Nick Raynsford. In Thursday's pre-budget report, Gordon Brown reassigned £512 million from central to local government budgets. Mr. Raynsford mentioned having had some "very candid discussions" with colleagues about these plans. However, he stressed that local governments must provide quality services without imposing significant council tax increases. The central government funds are part of a £1 billion initiative intended to assist local authorities in England in maintaining council tax hikes below 5% in the forthcoming year, which is expected to be an election year. According to Mr. Raynsford, nearly all central government departments have a vested interest in efficiently managed local authorities.

This is part of a plan to ensure that local authorities can continue to deliver good services without big council tax rises. The central government is providing the funding to help local authorities in England keep next year's council tax rises below 5%, in what is likely to be a general election year.
Mr Raynsford said nearly all central government departments had an interest in well-run local authorities.

In 1964, Bob Dylan, at the pinnacle of his career at the age of 23, is hailed as the voice of a generation, with a seemingly endless stream of classic songs pouring forth from his creative genius. However, a small but vocal minority raises a dissenting voice, accusing Dylan of plagiarizing the work of others. Fast-forward to 2004, Brian Burton, aka Danger Mouse, takes the Beatles' iconic "White Album" and Jay-Z's "The Black Album" and skillfully fuses them to create "The Grey Album." This innovative mashup, dubbed "The Grey Album," sparks an immediate online sensation, prompting the Beatles' record label to send out a barrage of cease-and-desist letters, citing "unfair competition and dilution of our valuable intellectual property." The label insists that "The Grey Album" is, in fact, a remix.

One simple vitamin can reduce your risk of heart disease. Eating chocolate reduces stress in students. A new drug prolongs lives of patients with rare disease. Health headlines like these are published every day, sometimes making opposite claims from each other. There can be a disconnect between broad, attention-grabbing headlines and the often specific, incremental results of the medical research they cover. So how can you avoid being misled by grabby headlines? The best way to assess a headline’s credibility is to look at the original research it reports on. We’ve come up with a hypothetical research scenario for each of these three headlines. Keep watching for the explanation of the first example; then pause at the headline to answer the question. These are simplified scenarios.

In 1903, the President of the United States embarked on a three-day camping trip in California’s Yosemite Valley. President Theodore Roosevelt slept in a grove of towering sequoia trees, camped during a snowstorm, and spent hours in conversation around the campfire with his host and guide, conservationist John Muir. While Roosevelt famously loved the outdoors, Muir had invited him for reasons beyond camping: Yosemite was in danger. Although Yosemite had been designated protected land in 1864, by 1903 the valley was still at risk of overdevelopment. It was at the center of a decades-old struggle to reserve land for both preservation and public use—two goals easier said than done. The battle over Yosemite began with the 1849 Gold Rush when miners surged west, seeking gold in the Sierra Nevada foothills.

Deep hashing methods have demonstrated excellent retrieval accuracy and efficiency in large-scale image retrieval. Optimizing discrete hash bits remains a central focus in deep hashing methods. A common approach is to employ an activation function, such as $\operatorname{sigmoid}(\cdot)$ or $\operatorname{tanh}(\cdot)$, and minimize a quantization loss to approximate discrete values. However, this paradigm may cause an increasing number of hash bits to become stuck in the undesired saturated regions of the activation functions, unable to escape, a challenge we refer to as the "dead bits problem (DBP)." Moreover, the existing quantization loss exacerbates DBP. In this paper, we introduce a simple yet effective gradient amplifier that is applied before activation functions to mitigate DBP.

U2, the Irish band, have been honored at France's biggest music awards, but were beaten to a prize by the boy band Blue. U2 received a special achievement prize at the NRJ music awards, but Blue beat them to the international group award. U2 was named the best new international artist, and took the best international song title for 'Beautiful Day.' More than five million radio listeners voted in the awards. The international male and female prizes went to Usher and Avril Lavigne respectively. Collecting his band's award from model Naomi Campbell at the Cannes ceremony, U2 frontman Bono said in French  'I'm not from this country, but I'll make a little confession to you - it's at the Côte d'Azur I feel at home.'

Manchester United narrowed Chelsea's lead in the Premier League to nine points following a determined 2-0 victory over Manchester City. Wayne Rooney capitalized on Gary Neville's cross to the near post by delivering a low shot that deflected off Richard Dunne, giving United the lead. Shortly after, an unfortunate Dunne inadvertently volleyed the ball over goalkeeper David James and into his own net. Steve McManaman squandered City's best opportunity by missing the target from three yards out in the first half. In the initial 45 minutes, United appeared unlikely to secure the crucial win needed to keep their title hopes alive. Their play was more sluggish than strategic, resulting in only one notable chance—a header by Paul Scholes that sailed over the bar.

All of the guns at this point were equally overpowered, so there were no problems with imbalance; everyone could kill everyone at any time. Also, the game used to give you unlimited gun slots and five loadouts. You would think that for a game with 'bajillions of guns', they would at least give you more than six weapon slots and three loadouts before you have to pay to get more. At one point, I had about three pages worth of guns, and was planning on new ideas for more, because personally, that's where I found it fun.

I'm going to talk about a technology we're developing at Oxford that we believe will revolutionize the creation of computer games and Hollywood movies. This technology focuses on simulating humans, featuring a simulated body and a simulated nervous system to control that body. Before I delve further into this technology, let's quickly examine the current portrayal of human characters in computer games. Here is a clip from a game called "Grand Theft Auto 3," which we briefly looked at yesterday. As you can see, it is an exceptionally good game, being one of the most successful of all time.

RPG levelling offers a wide variety of ship types, physics-based movement for both ships and projectiles/missiles, and easy-to-learn resource management. It also has a dark humored grind, but not excessively so. The AI teammates are sub-par, and there's a limited variety of equipment types and leveling skills, with no branching. However, despite these minuses, the game still has some variety and strategy to offer. It starts off fast and maintains a good pace. While there's a limited selection of equipment, it still feels like there's always another ship to unlock or skill to level up. The best way to enhance the game is through mods. The fight for universe is easily the main attraction, but the big fight for beef is also very satisfying.

Establishing a comprehensive weather service for water is imperative, but it will only become a reality when we collectively demand accountability and drive the necessary investment. My journey to address this issue began eight years ago, when I first took the stage at TED, fresh out of graduate school. At the time, I was unaware that my brief presentation would set the tone for the questions I would be grappling with for the next decade. Like many young idealists, I was convinced that I could single-handedly solve the world's water problems through technological innovation. However, I soon discovered that the reality was far more complex. I had initially been misled into believing that the persistence of water quality issues was primarily due to the difficulty in identifying them. I assumed that the solution lay in developing simpler, faster, and more affordable sensors. This assumption proved to be misguided, and I have since come to realize that the challenges we face are far more nuanced.

Interfacial spin-orbit coupling in Josephson junctions presents a compelling approach to unify anomalous Hall and Josephson physics within a single device. We theoretically investigate how the interplay of these phenomena influences the transport properties of superconductor/ferromagnetic insulator/superconductor junctions. The spin-dependent skew tunneling of Cooper pairs through the spin-active ferromagnetic insulator interface generates substantial transverse Hall supercurrents, which we term anomalous Josephson Hall effect currents. We extend the Furusaki-Tsukada formula—originally developed to quantify conventional (tunneling) Josephson currents—to assess the transverse current components, revealing that their amplitudes can be widely tuned via spin-orbit coupling strengths or the superconducting phase difference across the junction. As a distinct spectroscopic signature of these Josephson junctions, tightly localized subgap bound states emerge near the interface.

The world's largest and most devastating environmental and industrial project is located in the heart of the largest and most unspoiled forest on Earth, Canada's boreal forest. This forest spans across northern Canada, including Labrador, where it hosts the largest remaining wild caribou herd: the George River Caribou herd, which numbers approximately 400,000 animals. Unfortunately, when I visited, I couldn't spot any of them, but antlers serve as evidence of their presence. Throughout the boreal, we are fortunate to have an incredible abundance of wetlands. Globally, wetlands are among the most endangered ecosystems. They are absolutely critical, as they purify air and water, sequester large amounts of greenhouse gases, and are home to a vast diversity of species.

When working with survey data, it is common to account for random effects in the analysis by incorporating them into the model as individual survey weights. However, this approach can lead to biased inference if the survey weights are not carefully constructed to be inversely proportional to the unit survey inclusion probabilities. To address this issue, we propose a weight-exponentiated formulation for the random effects distribution that achieves unbiased inference for generating hyperparameters of the random effects. We compare our approach with frequentist methods that rely on numerical integration and show that only the Bayesian method achieves both unbiased estimation with respect to the sampling design distribution and consistency with respect to the population generating distribution. Our simulations and real data example for a survey of business establishments demonstrate the utility of our approach across different modeling formulations and sampling designs. This work serves as a capstone for recent developmental efforts that combine traditional survey estimation approaches with the Bayesian modeling paradigm and provides a bridge across the two rich but disparate sub-fields.

I know in a previous post, I protected parents, almost to the point where I even took their side. Here's my change of heart. Parents don't know anything. They think they know you so well, maybe even to the point where they can read emotions off your face. They can't. Parents know nothing about us. And when I go upstairs after arguing with Nicole about Kelli, like I just did a few minutes ago, and they want me to tell them what's wrong. Yeah, how about you shut the fuck up!? I don't have to tell you what's wrong all the time. Here's the situation with Nicole and Kelli. Kelli and Nicole don't get along. They don't like each other at all.

During my career as a palliative care specialist, I experienced a profoundly humbling moment that remains etched in my memory. Several years ago, I was consulted to evaluate a distinguished retired English professor in her seventies battling pancreatic cancer. She presented with severe pain, persistent nausea, and frequent vomiting. As we discussed symptom management during our consultation, she posed a thoughtful question: did I believe medical cannabis could alleviate her suffering? This inquiry prompted me to reflect on my medical education - a sobering realization, as I suddenly recognized the complete absence of any formal training on medicinal marijuana applications in patient care. The stark gap in my knowledge became painfully apparent in that moment.

Cabinet Anger Over Brown's Cash Reallocation: Ministers are displeased with plans to use Whitehall funds to mitigate council tax increases, as acknowledged by Local Government Minister Nick Raynsford. In his pre-budget report on Thursday, Gordon Brown reallocated £512 million from central to local government budgets. Mr. Raynsford mentioned having had some "pretty frank discussions" with other ministers regarding these plans. However, he emphasized that local governments need to provide effective services without significant council tax increases. The central government funds are part of a £1 billion package aimed at helping local authorities in England keep next year's council tax rises below 5%, which is crucial in a likely general election year. Mr. Raynsford noted that nearly all central government departments have a vested interest in well-managed local authorities.

I'd like to take you on the epic quest of the Rosetta spacecraft. For the past two years, it has been my passion to escort and land the probe on a comet. To do so, I must first explain the origin of our solar system. When we travel back in time approximately 4.5 billion years, we find a cloud of gas and dust. At the center of this cloud, our sun formed and ignited. Alongside that, what we now know as planets, comets, and asteroids formed. According to theory, when the Earth had cooled slightly after its formation, comets massively impacted the Earth, delivering water and possibly complex organic material, which may have kickstarted the emergence of life.

Allan Scott remains confident of securing a medal at next week's European Indoor Championships despite a slightly disappointing debut on the international circuit. The 22-year-old Scot finished fourth in the 60m hurdles at the José M. Cagigal Memorial Meeting in Madrid.  

"It was certainly a learning experience, and I still believe I can challenge for a medal next week," said the East Kilbride athlete.  

The race was won by Felipe Vivancos, who matched the Spanish record, followed by Sweden’s Robert Kronberg in second and Haiti’s Dudley Dorival in third.  

Scott, who won his heat in 7.64 seconds, expressed mild frustration with his performance in the final, where he ran 0.04 seconds slower in his first IAAF Indoor Grand Prix circuit final.

This exceptional port is an excellent entry point for newcomers to the series. Please note that there is some (skippable) dialogue, which may be a consideration for some players. As always, I recommend using a gamepad, but I had no issues with a keyboard and mouse setup, which is fully customizable. The price is reasonable, and I wouldn't have hesitated to make a purchase.
