While not perfect, this game has been greatly enjoyable. The save system and possibility of sudden death leads to some frustration. I lost my main character around hour 10 of 20ish, and I would have really appreciated a save to reload at that point. I've since picked up a third-party mod/save tool to avoid catastrophic failures. The aiming reticule is on the small side, and the zoom is underpowered. Despite these issues, it's been a blast. I'm not normally a zombie fan, but the combination of strategy, missions, first-person shooter, and beat 'em up elements is great. I've been playing it as often as I can get away... Just one more mission, just one more supply run – or I'm running away and want to get safe before shutting the game down.

It's 11 14 pm MST, and I'm currently sitting in my bedroom, fixing the personal computer of my job's personnel director. While I don't mind doing it, I often find myself wondering when the line is crossed from being helpful to being taken advantage of. I'm grateful to be able to use my skills to assist others, but I've been staying up late reading lately, so I was looking forward to an early night, until I realized it would be more practical to get the computer back to its owner. No rest for the weary, indeed! As I finished the task, I found myself watching Joe Schmo 2 again tonight.

For the past 24 years, I have been a firefighter in Huntington, West Virginia. As a firefighter, my team and I are tasked with saving lives and property from disasters such as car wrecks, house fires, and life-threatening medical emergencies. As a woman leading a department in a male-dominated profession, I decided 10 years ago to increase my medical knowledge and received a nursing degree. It became clear that the next big threat facing not only my city, but other cities around the country, was not a one-and-done disaster, where you can ride in like the cavalry, put out the fire and leave, feeling like you've made a difference and everything is okay.

Baking an apple pie? Discount orange warehouse has you covered! After all, a fruit is a fruit, right? In 1988, scientist James Hansen testified to the United States Congress that global warming trends are caused by human activity, and will pose an increasing threat to humanity in the future. Well, well. That's unusually prescient for a human. Looking for a wedding dress? Try a new take on a timeless classic. It's sleek, flattering, and modest – just like the traditional dress. Meanwhile, in 1997, the United States Senate held a hearing about global warming. Some expert witnesses pointed out that past periods in Earth's history were warmer than the 20th century.

For the past 24 years, I have served as a firefighter in Huntington, West Virginia. My team and I are dedicated to saving lives and protecting property from disasters such as car accidents, house fires, and life-threatening medical emergencies. As a woman leading a department in a predominantly male profession, I took a significant step 10 years ago to expand my medical expertise by earning a nursing degree. It became evident that the next major threat facing not just my city, but cities nationwide, was not a one-off event. These aren't situations where you sweep in heroically, extinguish the fire, and depart with a sense of accomplishment, believing all is well.

Ranking algorithms play a crucial role in online platforms, from search engines to recommender systems. In this paper, we uncover an intriguing phenomenon: the fewer items that report a given signal, the higher the collective traffic they attract. This few-get-richer effect emerges in scenarios where there are limited categories of items (e.g., left-leaning news sources versus right-leaning news sources), and popularity governs ranking. We demonstrate theoretically that this effect arises when people tend to favor top-ranked items and have diverse preferences among item categories. Using simulations and experimental data, we investigate how the strength of the effect changes under different assumptions about the environment and human behavior. Our findings have significant implications for understanding the dissemination of misinformation.

Monday, December 11, 2000  

To: All PX Market Participants  
From: John Yurkanin, Chief Operating Officer  
Re: Loss of Continuity in PX Day-Ahead Auction Due to ISO Rule Changes  

The emergency tariff filing by the California ISO last Friday has negatively impacted the PX day-ahead auction market. The combination of soft and hard caps creates a complete disconnect and unacceptable risk for all participants, and it is unlikely to achieve the ISO’s intended reduction in real-time energy sales and procurement.  

The overall effect will be that supply will continue to defer to the higher realized price opportunity offered by the ISO. The PX is petitioning Terry Winter of the ISO to immediately permit adjustment bids on any submitted schedule at any relevant price.

In the finance industry, I have spent 20 years of my career, gaining an intimate understanding of the field. I was well-versed in all aspects of finance, and I had a deep appreciation for the global economy, which was my life's work. While I was knowledgeable about various topics, including ecology and the potential end of the world, I didn't fully subscribe to these concepts, as I didn't find them entirely convincing. In 2007, the global financial system was dealt a severe blow with the subprime crisis and the collapse of several major American banks. This event was a wake-up call, and I had several intense discussions with my wife about the limitations of the financial models I had been using. Ultimately, I came to realize that my previous perspectives were misguided, and we were headed for disaster.

The spreadsheet below provides the implicit commodity based on the effective date of four different rates for the same rate schedule (schedule DR). The rate schedule effective August 2000 through September 2000 yields a much larger commodity charge because SDG&E customers will receive a lump sum reimbursement from SDG&E over those two months. SDG&E customers will expect lower electric bills. Assuming that the lump sum reimbursement is not included in the implicit energy component, the average for June 2000 through January 2001 is 6.2 cents and the implicit commodity average from February 2001 through December 2001 is 7.3 cents. The average implicit commodity from June 2000 through December 2001 is 6.9 cents for deliveries starting September 1, 2000, the average would still be 6.9 cents.

Understanding the profound correlations between two-dimensional (2D) systems is a major open problem in condensed matter physics, particularly in the quest to uncover the underlying mechanisms of unconventional superconductivity with high critical temperatures. Ultracold 2D Fermi gases have emerged as clean and controllable model systems to study the intricate interplay between strong correlations and reduced dimensionality. Despite their promise, direct evidence of superfluidity in these systems has remained elusive. In this work, we provide direct evidence of superfluidity in an ultracold 2D Fermi gas by dynamically introducing a periodic potential into the system and monitoring the absence of dissipation below a critical velocity, v_c. Our measurement of v_c as a function of interaction strength reveals a maximum in the crossover regime between bosonic and fermionic superfluidity. This finding establishes ultracold Fermi gases as a powerful tool for investigating the profound impact of reduced dimensionality on the behavior of strongly correlated superfluids.

Iraq to invite phone license bids. Iraq is set to invite bids for two telephone licenses, aiming to significantly boost nationwide coverage over the next decade. Bids are being invited from local, Arab, and foreign companies, Iraq's Ministry of Communications stated. The winner will work in partnership with the Iraqi Telecommunications and Post Company (ITPC). The firms will be tasked with installing and operating a fixed phone network, providing voice, fax, and internet services. The ministry expressed a desire to increase Iraq's "very low telephone service penetration rate from about 4.5% today to about 25% within 10 years." It also hopes to develop a "highly visible and changeable telecommunication sector." Details of the bidding and tender process will be published on the ministry's website on February 9. It is also planning a roadshow for investors in Amman, Jordan.

Orientational dynamics in the isotropic phase of a comb-shaped nematic polymer with mesogenic and functional side groups were studied using the Kerr effect and dielectric spectroscopy. For the first time, it was discovered that in a mesogenic polymer, unlike low-molecular-weight mesogens, the relaxation of electric birefringence of a melt above the temperature of the nematic-isotropic phase transition can be represented by a sum of several exponential processes, two of which play a decisive role. These main processes replace each other in a temperature range of about fifty degrees. Dielectric spectroscopy also made it possible to distinguish two processes of orientational relaxation: the first is due to the rotation of the side mesogenic groups, and the second is associated with the motion of the main chain segments.

Upon completing my dual degree in molecular and cell biology and linguistics from the University of California, Berkeley, seven years prior, I attended a campus career fair that led to an interview with a startup called Theranos. At the time, information about the company was scarce, but what was available was undeniably impressive. Theranos was pioneering a revolutionary medical device that enabled comprehensive blood panel analysis from a mere finger-stick sample, eliminating the need for invasive venipuncture. This innovation not only promised a more comfortable experience for patients but also held the potential to unlock the doors to predictive diagnostics, a groundbreaking prospect that sparked my interest.

Roxy Music is on the Isle of Wight bill. Roxy Music will appear at June's Isle of Wight Music Festival, along with Morrissey, Supergrass, and Idlewild. REM have already been confirmed as headliners for the three-day event, which takes place from 10–12 June. Original band members Bryan Ferry, Phil Manzanera, Andy Mackay, and Paul Thompson will take to the stage on the Saturday night of the festival. The band is also working in the studio on new material, their first since the 1982 album *Avalon*. Roxy Music was formed in 1971 by Ferry, Mackay, and Brian Eno, with Manzanera joining the following year. Their first hit was 1972's *Virginia Plain*, with other hits including *Street Life*, *Love Is the Drug*, and the 1981 John Lennon cover, *Jealous Guy*.

My shoulder has been afflicted with tendonitis, specifically the right one, which is located beneath my shoulder blade. The constant strain of moving my arm, particularly when I'm using a mouse on a computer for extended periods, has been exacerbating the condition. The pain is quite debilitating, making it challenging for me to move my arm as much as I'd like. Unfortunately, my recent bout of updating my website and working on other online projects has put a significant strain on my body, leaving me with barely enough strength to engage in any physical activity, including drawing. The lack of sleep has only added to my discomfort, making it a particularly trying time for me. Although I'm not thrilled with my current situation, I'm confident that I'll make a full recovery. My mother's recent surgery has been successful, and her eyesight has shown significant improvement. She can now perceive fuzzy shapes and colors, although she still struggles with her overall vision. I'm hopeful that she'll regain her full vision soon.

Before the world of humankind came into being, there existed the divine realm of the gods—a land of sprawling fields, open plains, and lush gardens. Four brothers roamed this celestial domain, bound only by their kinship, for they knew no other family, not even their own parents. Among them, Deminan stood apart, his skin marred by painful scabs, and he often pondered why he alone bore this cruel affliction. One day, while the supreme spirit Yaya tended his gardens, Deminan and his brothers stole into Yaya's dwelling. After indulging in food and exploration, they discovered an enormous gourd suspended in the corner. But as they attempted to peer inside, the gourd slipped from their grasp.

The cross-party group has unveiled a draft bill proposing a smaller second chamber in which 70% of members would be elected. The proposed reform plan aims to raise the pressure on Tony Blair over reform to the House of Lords by publishing a detailed blueprint for change. The group has tried to raise the pressure on Tony Blair over reform to the House of Lords by publishing a detailed blueprint for change. Mps and peers have failed to agree on reform since 1999 when 600 hereditaries lost their seats. The group says it can win support for removing the last 92 hereditaries. The government postponed plans to remove the remaining hereditary peers because they said they were unlikely to succeed after opposition in the lords. Tony Blair has argued. There needs to be consensus on reforms. There have been suggestions there will be proposals for changing at least the powers of the lords in Labour's manifesto.

Good afternoon. I'm delighted to be here at TEDxKrakow. Today, I aim to discuss a phenomenon that is reshaping our world, one we know as "people power." I'll begin with an anecdote, or, for those who appreciate Monty Python, a sketch reminiscent of their humor. Imagine this: it’s December 15, 2010. Someone proposes a wager—you'll gaze into a crystal ball and glimpse the future, knowing it will be accurate. Your challenge is to share this vision with the world. Intrigued, despite the risks, you accept. After an hour, you find yourself in the studios of a national TV network, appearing on a prominent talk show, recounting your extraordinary story.

Sure, I'd be happy to explain the Yap and their unique system of money!

The Yap is a small island nation located in the Pacific Ocean, known for its unique culture and traditions. One of these traditions is their form of money, which is based on large limestone discs called "rai stones."

The Yapese people have been using rai stones as their form of currency for centuries, and the tradition is still alive and well today. The stones are made from limestone and are cut into different shapes and sizes, depending on their intended use. The largest rai stones are called "mwang," and they can weigh up to four tons and measure up to 12 feet in diameter.
The Yapese people have a complex system for tracking who owns which part of each stone. The stones are divided into different sections, and each section is assigned to a specific family or clan. When a stone is created, it is divided into these sections, and the ownership is passed down from generation to generation.
The Yapese people have a deep respect for these rai stones, and they are an integral part of their culture and identity. They are used in many ceremonies and rituals, and they are also used as a symbol of wealth and status.

Spintronic nanodevices exhibit ultrafast nonlinear dynamic and recurrence behaviors on a nanosecond scale, promising to enable spintronic reservoir computing (RC) systems. Here, two physical RC systems, based on a single magnetic skyrmion memristor (MSM) and 24 spin-torque nano-oscillators (STNOs), were proposed and modeled to handle an image classification task and nonlinear dynamic system prediction, respectively. Based on our micromagnetic simulation results regarding the nonlinear responses of MSM and STNO with current pulse stimulation, the handwritten digit recognition task demonstrates that an RC system using a single MSM has outstanding performance in image classification. Additionally, complex unknown nonlinear dynamic problems can also be effectively solved by a physical RC system composed of 24 STNOs, as confirmed by the second-order nonlinear dynamic system and NARMA10 tasks.

Over the weekend, Governor Davis and Senator Polanco reached an informal agreement, whereby Senator Polanco has agreed to sponsor the SoCal MOU bill. As a Democrat currently embroiled in a personal scandal, Polanco's political career is reportedly all but over. He is instead planning to run for Mayor of Los Angeles rather than seeking another Senate term. The bill is expected to be introduced in the new extraordinary session, which begins today or tomorrow. It will first be referred to the Senate Energy Committee, chaired by Senator Bowen, who strongly opposes the MOU. As a result, the bill is likely to remain in committee hearings for some time, undergoing changes and negotiations. The longer the bill is mired in committee politics, the greater the likelihood of a SoCal bankruptcy.

The comedy "Sideways" dominated this year's Independent Spirit Awards, winning all six awards for which it was nominated. The film was named Best Film, while Alexander Payne took home Best Director and Best Screenplay, sharing the latter with writing partner Jim Taylor. The movie also secured acting awards for its stars, Paul Giamatti, Thomas Haden Church, and Virginia Madsen. With five Oscar nominations, "Sideways" is expected to perform well at Sunday's ceremony. Extreme mass ratio inspirals (EMRIs) can be broadly categorized into two distinct types based on their formation mechanisms: dry EMRIs and wet EMRIs. Traditionally, dry EMRIs, also known as "loss-cone" EMRIs, were considered the primary source of EMRIs detectable by the Laser Interferometer Space Antenna. These dry EMRIs are primarily formed through the complex interplay of multi-body scattering within the nuclear star cluster and gravitational capture. However, this letter sheds light on an alternative, yet significant, EMRI formation pathway: the assisted formation of wet EMRIs facilitated by the accretion flow surrounding accreting massive black holes (MBHs) at galactic centers. In this scenario, the accretion disk captures stellar-mass black holes that are initially on inclined orbits, and subsequently, through a process of orbital migration, drives them towards the MBH, thereby dramatically enhancing the formation rate of EMRIs in these galaxies by several orders of magnitude.

Allan Scott remains optimistic about his medal prospects at the upcoming European Indoor Championships despite a bittersweet debut on the international stage. The 22-year-old Scottish hurdler secured fourth place in the 60m event at Madrid's prestigious José M. Cagigal Memorial Meet, marking his inaugural appearance on the global circuit.  

"While it was undoubtedly a valuable learning experience, I'm still fully committed to contending for a podium finish next week," stated the East Kilbride sprinter.  

The race was dominated by Spain's Felipe Vivancos, who matched the national record, followed by Sweden's Robert Kronberg in second and Haiti's Dudley Dorival in third. Though Scott clinched his heat with a time of 7.64 seconds, a marginal 0.04-second slowdown in the final left him with mixed feelings about his performance. Nevertheless, his strong showing has only reinforced his determination to excel at the championships.

Anti-spam laws bite spammer hard: the net's self-declared spam king is seeking bankruptcy protection. Scott Richter, the man behind OptinRealBig.com and billions of junk mail messages, said lawsuits had forced the company into Chapter 11. OptinRealBig was fighting several legal battles, most notably against Microsoft, which is pushing for millions of dollars in damages. The company said filing for Chapter 11 would help it try to resolve its legal problems but still keep trading. Listed as the third biggest spammer in the world by junk mail watchdog Spamhaus, OptinRealBig was sued in December 2003 for sending mail messages that violated anti-spam laws. The lawsuit was brought by Microsoft and New York Attorney General Eliot Spitzer, who alleged that Mr. Richter and his accomplices sent billions of spam messages through 514 compromised net addresses in 35 countries.

Last night, I finally completed this game. While the story isn't particularly deep or interesting, what makes it stand out is the exceptional storytelling. I learned a lot about the world of Batman, so it was a bit disappointing that so few bosses were included in the game. However, there's always plenty of room for improvement in future games. The combat is the perfect balance of challenge and ease of play, and leveling up Batman wasn't as satisfying as I had hoped. Exploring previously inaccessible areas as you progress is a nice sense of progression.

Within general relativity, the unique stationary solution for an isolated black hole is the Kerr spacetime, characterized by a distinctive multipolar structure determined solely by its mass and spin. We have developed a general method to extract the multipole moments of arbitrary stationary spacetimes and have applied it to a broad class of horizonless microstate geometries. These geometries can disrupt the axial and equatorial symmetry of the Kerr metric, resulting in a much more complex multipolar structure, offering a pathway to constrain fuzzball models phenomenologically. We find numerical evidence indicating that all multipole moments are typically larger (in absolute value) than those of a Kerr black hole with the same mass and spin. Current measurements of the quadrupole moment of black-hole candidates can impose only mild constraints on fuzzballs, while future gravitational-wave detections of extreme mass-ratio inspirals with the LISA space mission will significantly improve these bounds.

Quantum walks in continuous time have emerged as a highly effective framework for the design of numerous quantum algorithms. The running time of these algorithms is often characterized by the quantum hitting time, which is the time required for the quantum walk to find a vertex of interest with a high probability. In this study, we provide improved upper bounds for the quantum hitting time that can be applied to various quantum algorithms based on continuous-time quantum walks. Notably, we apply our techniques to the glued-trees problem, significantly reducing their hitting time upper bound by a polynomial factor  from O(n^5) to O(n^2logn). Moreover, our methods also enable exponential improvements in the precision of the continuous-time quantum walk-based algorithm to find a marked node on any ergodic, reversible Markov chain, as demonstrated by Chakraborty et al.

Manchester United secured a crucial nine-point advantage over Chelsea in the Premier League title race with a hard-fought victory over Manchester City. Wayne Rooney capitalized on a Gary Neville cross to the near post with a low shot, which found the back of the net via a deflection off Richard Dunne. Seven minutes later, an unfortunate Dunne hooked a volley over David James' head and into his own net, giving United a two-goal cushion. Steve McManaman wasted City's best chance of the half when he shot wide from three yards out. In the opening 45 minutes, United appeared unlikely to earn the win they needed to maintain any chance of catching Chelsea in the title race. Their approach play was more labored than patient, and they managed to fashion just one chance - a Paul Scholes header over the bar.

Iraq is inviting bids from local, Arab, and foreign companies to bid for two telephone licenses, with the goal of significantly increasing the country's telephone coverage over the next decade. The Ministry of Communications has invited bids from companies to install and operate a fixed phone network, providing voice, fax, and internet services. The ministry aims to increase Iraq's "very low telephone service penetration rate" of around 4.5% today to around 25% within the next 10 years. Additionally, the ministry hopes to develop a "highly visible and changeable telecommunications sector." The bidding and tender process will be published on the ministry's website on February 9, and the ministry is also planning a roadshow for investors in Amman, Jordan.

These are challenging economic times, fellow TEDsters, indeed challenging times. Therefore, I would like to uplift your spirits with one of the great, albeit largely unknown, commercial success stories of the past 20 years. It is comparable, in its own unique way, to the achievements of Microsoft or Google. This is an industry that has weathered the current recession with poise. I am referring to organized crime. Now, organized crime has been around for a very long time, as you might point out, and those words would be quite astute. However, in the last two decades, it has experienced unprecedented growth, now accounting for roughly 15 percent of the world's GDP. I like to call it the global shadow economy, or "McMafia," for short. So, what triggered this extraordinary growth in cross-border crime?

When a new user signs up on a website, we typically have no information about them—no interactions with items, no user profile, and no social connections with other users. Despite this, we expect our recommender systems to attract users from the outset, encouraging them to stay on the website and become active users. This challenge falls into the new user cold-start category, which is crucial to a company's development and even survival. Existing approaches to user cold-start recommendations either require additional user effort, such as setting up an interview process, or utilize side information [10] like user demographics, locations, and social relations. However, users may be unwilling to participate in interviews, and side information on cold-start users is often unavailable.

Weight pruning is widely recognized as a straightforward and effective method to reduce redundancy in deep neural networks (DNNs), thereby achieving acceleration on various platforms. However, most pruning techniques essentially involve trade-offs between model accuracy and regularity, leading to impaired inference accuracy and limited on-device acceleration performance. To address this issue, we introduce a new sparsity dimension, called pattern-based sparsity, which includes both pattern and connectivity sparsity, making it both highly accurate and hardware-friendly. With carefully designed patterns, the proposed pruning consistently achieves unprecedented accuracy enhancement and better feature extraction capabilities across different DNN structures and datasets. Our pattern-aware pruning framework also simultaneously performs pattern library extraction, pattern selection, pattern and connectivity pruning, and weight training. Our approach to the new pattern-based sparsity naturally fits into compiler optimization for highly efficient DNN execution on mobile platforms.

Good afternoon, I'm proud to be here at TEDxKrakow. I'll try to speak a little today about a phenomenon that can, and actually is, changing the world—whose name is people power. I'll start with an anecdote, or for those of you who are Monty Python lovers, a Monty Python-type sketch. Here it is.  

It is December 15, 2010. Someone gives you a bet: you will look into a crystal ball, and you will see the future—the future will be accurate. But you must share it with the world. Okay, curiosity killed the cat—you take the bet, you look into the crystal ball. One hour later, you're sitting in a building of the national TV, on a top show, and you tell the story.

Hello. This is my first trip, the first time in my life I'm outside the walls of Gaza. I’m so happy to be here. (applause) My ambition has always been to be a pilot, to fly a plane, to feel the freedom of the sky. But that hasn’t happened. Simply put, I live in Gaza, where there is no airport. All borders are closed on every side. We live in one of the largest prisons in the world. The only thing I can do is look up at the sky. On some days, we are lucky if we have electricity for four or five hours. When it’s cold, we make a fire at the front or on the roof of our homes.

The emergence of podcasting has revolutionized the audio landscape, ushering in a new era of DIY radio production. While modern digital music players like Apple's iPod boast impressive storage capacities—capable of holding up to 10,000 songs—an increasing number of users are dedicating this space to diverse audio content crafted by an eclectic range of creators. This phenomenon, known as podcasting, has found its most vocal champion in Adam Curry, the former MTV host and video jockey.

Though the term derives from the iPod brand, podcasting isn't exclusive to Apple's device—neither production nor consumption requires specific hardware. At its core, podcasting represents internet-based radio programming, typically produced in home studios with minimal equipment: just a microphone, personal computer, and basic editing software. Creators simply upload their content to the web, making it freely accessible for global audiences to download and enjoy at their convenience. This democratization of audio broadcasting has opened unprecedented opportunities for creative expression and information sharing.

Home phones face an uncertain future, as the traditional fixed-line telephone in households may soon become a rarity. Research conducted by handset manufacturer Nokia reveals that an increasing number of people rely solely on their mobile phones for all calls, whether making or receiving them. The study indicates that over 45 million individuals in the UK, Germany, the US, and South Korea now exclusively use mobile devices. While some retain fixed-line phones due to lower call costs, the majority of respondents agreed that the future undoubtedly lies with mobile technology.  

The Nokia-sponsored research highlighted distinct usage patterns between mobile and landline phones. Fixed-line phones were preferred for longer conversations, whereas mobile calls tended to be shorter, often between mobile users or with friends. In the UK, 69% of participants cited cost savings as the primary reason for still using a landline, acknowledging that it remains cheaper than mobile alternatives.

There are currently hundreds of thousands of people on transplant lists, waiting for critical organs like kidneys, hearts, and livers that could save their lives. Unfortunately, there aren’t nearly enough donor organs available to meet that demand. What if, instead of waiting, we could create brand-new, customized organs from scratch? That’s the idea behind bioprinting, a branch of regenerative medicine currently under development. We’re not able to print complex organs just yet, but simpler tissues, including blood vessels and tubes responsible for nutrient and waste exchange, are already within our reach. Bioprinting is a biological cousin of 3D printing, a technique that deposits layers of material on top of each other to construct a three-dimensional object one slice at a time.

When random effects are correlated with sample design variables, the traditional approach of employing individual survey weights (constructed to be inversely proportional to the unit survey inclusion probabilities) to form a pseudo-likelihood no longer produces asymptotically unbiased inference. We construct a weight-exponentiated formulation for the random effects distribution that achieves unbiased inference for generating hyperparameters of the random effects. We contrast our approach with frequentist methods that rely on numerical integration to reveal that only the Bayesian method achieves both unbiased estimation with respect to the sampling design distribution and consistency with respect to the population generating distribution. Our simulations and real data example for a survey of business establishments demonstrate the utility of our approach across different modeling formulations and sampling designs. This work serves as a capstone for recent developmental efforts that combine traditional survey estimation approaches with the Bayesian modeling paradigm and provides a bridge across the two rich but disparate sub-fields.

To do two things at once is to do neither." It's a great smackdown of multitasking, isn't it, often attributed to the Roman writer Publilius Syrus, although you know how these things are - he probably never said it. What I'm interested in, though, is - is it true? I mean, it's obviously true for emailing at the dinner table or texting while driving, or possibly for live tweeting at a TED talk, as well. But I'd like to argue that for an important kind of activity, doing two things at once - or three or even four - is exactly what we should be aiming for. Look no further than Albert Einstein. In 1905, he published four remarkable scientific papers.

Brizzell to compete in AAA's in Sheffield  
Ballymena sprinter Paul Brizzell will be among eight of Ireland’s European Indoor hopefuls taking part in this weekend’s AAA Championships. US-based athletes Alistair Cragg and Mark Carroll are the only Irish competitors selected so far for the Europeans who will not race in Sheffield.  

Brizzell will defend his 200m title at the British trials, while in-form James McIlroy aims to secure his spot on the British team for Madrid by winning the 800m event. McIlroy has shown outstanding performance on the European circuit in recent weeks, establishing himself as one of the fastest 800m runners globally this winter and virtually guaranteeing his place in Madrid.  

Meanwhile, Corkman Mark Carroll confirmed midweek that he would join Cragg at the European Championships. Carroll currently holds the world number three ranking in the 3000m, with Cragg leading the standings.

British stars miss out on major Oscars, as UK hopes of securing top prizes were dashed when British talent failed to win in the acting and directing categories. Despite receiving three nominations, Mike Leigh's Vera Drake was unsuccessful in securing the director or screenplay awards, and its star, Imelda Staunton, also went home empty-handed. Kate Winslet lost out to Best Actress winner Hilary Swank, while Clive Owen and Sophie Okonedo were unsuccessful in their bids for Best Supporting Actor awards. However, the UK's Andrea Arnold did secure a win, taking home the Short Film award for her gritty drama Wasp. Arnold, a former presenter of children's television shows Number 73 and Motormouth in the 1980s, described her win as "totally overwhelming". Wasp, which tells the story of a single mother struggling to make ends meet, beat out films by fellow UK nominees Gary McKendry and Ashvin Kumar.

A highly anticipated documentary film featuring the life and career of chart-topping singer George Michael will be showcased at this year's Berlin Film Festival. The documentary, set to screen in the Panorama section of the festival, which runs from February 10-20, offers a candid look at the highs and lows of Michael's life, from his early days in Wham! to his more recent experiences. The 93-minute film explores his journey from his early days as a member of Wham! to his later years as a solo artist, including his legal battles with Sony Records and his stance on the Iraq War and American politics. Michael will attend the festival to introduce the screening on February 16, alongside Director Southan Morris and Executive Producer Andy Stephens. This is a unique opportunity to gain insight into the life and career of a music legend, and the Berlin Film Festival is honored to present this special event.

Observations of thermally driven transverse vibrations in a photonic crystal waveguide (PCW) are presented. The PCW comprises two parallel nanobeams separated by a 240 nm vacuum gap. Developed models are validated for transducing beam motion into phase and amplitude modulation of a weak optical probe propagating in a guided mode of the PCW, for probe frequencies both far from and near the dielectric band edge. Given the PCW's design for near-field atom trapping, this research establishes a foundation for assessing potential detrimental effects of thermal motion on optical atomic traps near PCW surfaces. The long-term objective is to establish strong atom-mediated links between individual phonons of vibration and single photons propagating in the guided modes of the PCW, thereby enabling quantum-level optomechanics involving atoms, photons, and phonons.

Interfacial spin-orbit coupling in Josephson junctions offers an intriguing way to combine anomalous Hall and Josephson physics in a single device. We study theoretically how the superposition of both effects impacts superconductor/ferromagnetic insulator/superconductor junctions' transport properties. Transverse momentum-dependent skew tunneling of Cooper pairs through the spin-active ferromagnetic insulator interface creates sizable transverse Hall supercurrents, to which we refer as anomalous Josephson Hall effect currents. We generalize the Furusaki-Tsukada formula, which was initially established to quantify usual (tunneling) Josephson current flows, to evaluate the transverse current components and demonstrate that their amplitudes are widely adjustable by means of the spin-orbit coupling strengths or the superconducting phase difference across the junction. As a clear spectroscopic fingerprint of Josephson junctions, well-localized subgap bound states form around the interface.

This space RPG delivers an exhilarating experience with its engaging leveling system, diverse ship selection, and realistic physics-driven movement for both vessels and projectiles. The resource management is intuitive yet rewarding, complemented by a dash of dark humor. While the game avoids excessive grinding when utilizing civilian stations effectively, it does suffer from lackluster AI allies, limited equipment options, and a linear skill progression without branching paths.  

That said, don’t let the drawbacks fool you—this game is an absolute blast. Despite its shortcomings, it offers surprising depth in strategy and variety. The pacing is brisk from the start and only intensifies as you progress. Even with restricted equipment choices, the thrill of unlocking new ships and advancing skills keeps the excitement alive. To truly extend its replay value, mods are essential—*Fight for Universe* stands out as a masterpiece, with *Big Fight for Beef* also delivering a stellar experience.

Ranking algorithms are fundamental to online platforms, from search engines to recommendation systems. In this study, we uncover an unexpected outcome of popularity-based rankings: items reporting a rare signal tend to capture a disproportionately large share of overall traffic. This "few-get-richer" phenomenon becomes apparent in environments with limited distinct categories of items (such as left-leaning versus right-leaning news sources), where rankings are determined by popularity. We provide a theoretical demonstration that this effect arises when users click predominantly on top-ranked items and exhibit diverse preferences toward different item categories. Through simulations, we examine how varying assumptions about the environment and user behavior impact the intensity of this effect. We further verify our predictions in an online experiment with real participants. Our results hold significant implications for understanding how misinformation spreads.

The tails of diboson production at the LHC are sensitive to the interference between the standard model and higher-dimensional operators, parameterizing the effects of heavy new physics. However, helicity selection rules for the diboson scattering amplitudes set an obstacle to the naive interference contributions of dimension-six operators, causing the total diboson rate correction's leading contribution to cancel. In this case, carefully measuring the azimuthal decay angles "resurrects" the interference, recouping sensitivity to the "non-interfering" operators. We explore these signatures in detail, and find that the EFT uncertainties associated with higher-dimensional operators are uniquely well-suppressed by the construction of an asymmetry variable which is only generated by these non-interfering operators, relegating the effects of higher-dimensional, interfering operators to the same status as statistical errors in this observable.

El Paso Pipeline to Test Market Interest in Mainline Expansion
El Paso Pipeline, a subsidiary of Kinder Morgan, Inc., is set to test market interest in a mainline expansion to address the current capacity constraint at the California border. The pipeline has announced an open season for the expansion, which will run from February 17 to March 17, 2023, to gauge shipper interest in adding 14.4 billion cubic feet per day (Bcf/d) of firm transportation capacity. This expansion is aimed at relieving the pressure on the pipeline due to the significant demand and skyrocketing prices in California.The El Paso Pipeline has been under immense pressure from regulators and shippers to expand due to the unprecedented demand and high prices in California. In a letter to the pipeline dated January 3, 2023, Daniel M. Adamson, Director of FERC's Office of Energy Projects, urged El Paso to consider modifying its Line 2000 project to "assist the difficult situation" currently facing the California gas market.

Ranking algorithms are integral to the functioning of online platforms, including search engines and recommendation systems. In this study, we explore an unexpected outcome of popularity-based rankings: the fewer the number of items that signify a particular indicator, the greater the proportion of total traffic they tend to garner. This phenomenon, known as the few-get-richer effect, becomes evident in scenarios where there are limited distinct categories of items (for instance, news outlets with left-leaning and right-leaning biases) and items are ranked by their popularity. Through analytical methods, we demonstrate that this effect is pronounced when users predominantly click on items that top the rankings and exhibit diverse preferences for the categories. We further examine the effect's intensity under varying assumptions about the environment and human behavior through simulations. Additionally, we validate our hypotheses via an online experiment involving human participants. Our results offer significant insights into the dissemination of misinformation.

The phrase, 'to do two things at once is to do neither,' serves as a compelling critique of multitasking. It's often linked to the Roman writer Publilius Syrus, though, as is common with such quotes, the attribution is uncertain. My curiosity, however, lies in the truth of this adage. While it clearly applies to scenarios like emailing during dinner, texting while driving, or perhaps live tweeting during a TED Talk, I believe there are certain activities where multitasking—or even aiming to accomplish three or four tasks simultaneously—is beneficial. Consider Albert Einstein in 1905, when he published four groundbreaking scientific papers.

In this paper, we examine the landscape of the population negative log-likelihood function for Gaussian mixture models with a general number of components. Due to nonconvexity, there are multiple local minima that are not globally optimal, even when the mixture is well-separated. We demonstrate that all local minima share a similar structure that partially identifies the component centers of the true mixture. Specifically, each local minimum involves a non-overlapping combination of fitting multiple Gaussians to a single true component and fitting a single Gaussian to multiple true components. Our results apply to scenarios where the true mixture components satisfy a certain separation condition and are valid even when the number of components is over or under-specified. For Gaussian mixtures with three components, we provide sharper results regarding the scaling with the separation between the components.

The concept of bioprinting, also known as bioprinting technology or biomanufacturing, is a relatively new field that involves the use of 3D printing techniques to create living tissue structures. The goal of bioprinting is to create functional, three-dimensional tissue structures that can be used to replace or repair damaged organs in the human body.
The idea behind bioprinting is to use 3D printing techniques to create a scaffold or matrix of biological material, such as collagen or gelatin, that can be used to support the growth of cells. The cells are then introduced to the scaffold and allowed to grow and differentiate, eventually forming a fully functional organ.
Bioprinting has the potential to revolutionize the field of organ transplantation by providing a virtually unlimited supply of organs for transplantation. Currently, there is a severe shortage of donor organs available for transplantation, resulting in long waiting lists and many people dying while waiting for a transplant. Bioprinting could potentially overcome this problem by allowing for the creation of custom-made organs that can be grown to order.
Bioprinting is still in its infancy, and there are many technical and ethical challenges that must be overcome before it

Imagine if you could plug your brain into a machine that would bring you ultimate pleasure for the rest of your life. If you were given the choice to sign up for that kind of existence, would you? That's the question philosopher Robert Nozick posed through a thought experiment he called the Experience Machine. The experiment asks us to consider a world in which scientists have developed a machine that would simulate real life while guaranteeing experiences of only pleasure and never pain. The catch: you have to permanently leave reality behind, but you'll hardly know the difference. Your experiences will be indistinguishable from reality. Life's natural ups and downs will just be replaced with an endless series of ups. Sounds great, right?

This study explores multi-agent discrete-event systems operating under partial observation. The system's agents are organized into distinct groups, where members within each group exhibit isomorphic state transition structures. This structural similarity enables the representation of all agents within a group through a single standardized template. Leveraging this template-based approach, we develop a scalable supervisory control framework that maintains constant computational complexity and state space requirements, irrespective of the number of agents. Notably, the proposed supervisor remains valid when agents are dynamically added or removed from the system, eliminating the need for recomputation. We extend previous work by establishing sufficient conditions that guarantee both safety and maximal permissiveness for the template-level supervisor under partial observation. A practical example demonstrates the effectiveness of our scalable supervisory synthesis methodology.

Allan Scott, despite experiencing some disappointment, is feeling optimistic about his chances of winning a medal at the upcoming European Indoor Championships following a promising start on the international stage. The 22-year-old athlete from Scotland achieved a commendable fourth place in the 60m hurdles at the Jose M Cagigal Memorial meeting in Madrid. "It was certainly a learning experience, and I am still very much in the running for a medal next week," said the East Kilbride native. The race saw Felipe Vivancos taking first place while matching the Spanish record, followed by Sweden's Robert Kronberg in second and Haiti's Dudley Dorival in third. Although Scott was somewhat dissatisfied with his performance in the final, having clocked a winning time of 7.64 seconds in his heat, he ran slightly slower by 0.04 seconds in the final of his first IAAF Indoor Grand Prix circuit.

Sir Alex Ferguson lauded Manchester United's resilience following their hard-fought 1-0 victory against Aston Villa, describing it as their most challenging away fixture this season. "It was an exceptional match with relentless action and superb technical play from both sides," remarked the legendary Old Trafford manager. "The team demonstrated tremendous fortitude and determination – we simply refused to accept defeat."

Ferguson highlighted the significance of claiming three points at Villa Park, noting, "We've successfully navigated this difficult away test, while our title rivals Arsenal and Chelsea still face this challenge – potentially against a strengthened Villa squad." The Scottish tactician reserved special praise for veteran substitutes Ryan Giggs and Roy Keane, who replaced the injured John O'Shea. "Roy's introduction brought much-needed stability and leadership to our midfield – qualities uniquely his own," Ferguson observed, acknowledging Keane's invaluable contribution to the victory.
